title: ZEAL/BZea DTA GWAS + JLM on the SNP50K panel — 1000-permutation FWER
section: Composite (A: JLM · B: per-SNP MLM · C: RTIGER-mosaic MLM)
text:
Composite (A: JLM · B: per-SNP MLM · C: RTIGER-mosaic MLM)
Panel B (per-SNP genotype (bcftools HWE-posterior)) is λ_GC-deflated — the 70%-missing per-SNP panel at 0.4× is sparse — but catches sharp SNP hits (ZCN8) the mosaic block-terraces away. Panel C (the ancestry mosaic) is the usable, well-calibrated genotype. The B↔︎C contrast is the notebook’s per-SNP-scatter vs ancestry-block-smoothing, and the ZEAL demonstration that ancestry inference is mandatory at low coverage.


Show code
p_B <- plot_manhattan(gt,
  sprintf("DTA — MLM (Taxon+K), per-SNP genotype (bcftools HWE-posterior) (λ_GC = %.2f; dotted = perm FWER 5%%)", lam(gt$P)),
  overlap_csv = overlap, lod = thr_gt
)
p_C <- plot_manhattan(mlm,
  sprintf("DTA — MLM (Taxon+K), RTIGER ancestry mosaic (λ_GC = %.2f; dotted = perm FWER 5%%)", lam(mlm$P)),
  overlap_csv = overlap, lod = thr_mlm
)
composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(p_lolli, p_B, p_C, ncol = 1, labels = c("A", "B", "C"), label_size = 20, align = "v", axis = "lr")
ggsave(here("results/sim/zeal/dta_composite_snp50k.png"), composite, width = 9, height = 13, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
composite

title: ZEAL/BZea DTA GWAS + JLM on the SNP50K panel — 1000-permutation FWER
section: Multi-caller DTA scan (control · nNIL · RTIGER · LB-Impute · binhmm)
text:
Multi-caller DTA scan (control · nNIL · RTIGER · LB-Impute · binhmm)
The ZEAL analog of the TeoNAM DTA five-caller composite: the same DTA MLM (Taxon+K) scan on each ancestry caller’s recovered genotypes, at the real ~0.4× depth (not a coverage sweep). The scientific content here is caller reliability at low coverage: the ancestry mosaic must be block/bin-aggregated — the two aggregated callers (RTIGER per-donor segments; binhmm 1 Mb bins) recover the correct teosinte fraction (~0.10, matching the established BzeaSeq), while per-SNP calling (control) and the fresh per-SNP HMM recalls (nNIL, LB-Impute) under-call and give λ-deflated scans.


Show code
callers <- c(control = "hwe_post_gt", nNIL = "nnil_mosaic", RTIGER = "rtiger_mosaic", `LB-Impute` = "lbimpute_mosaic", binhmm = "binhmm_mosaic")
tb1 <- fread(here("data/teonam/dta_candidate_genes.tsv"))[symbol == "zmcct10"]
ctab2 <- rbindlist(lapply(names(callers), function(nm) {
  g <- callers[[nm]]
  sc <- fread(here(sprintf("data/zeal/dta_gwas_mlm_%s_donor_accession_snp50k.csv", g)))
  mf <- here(sprintf("data/zeal/zeal_%s.rds", g))
  pres <- if (file.exists(mf)) {
    st <- readRDS(mf)$state
    round(mean(st %in% c(1L, 2L), na.rm = TRUE), 3)
  } else {
    NA_real_
  }
  sc <- sc[is.finite(P) & P > 0]
  z <- sc[CHR == tb1$chr & abs(BP - tb1$start) <= 5e5]
  data.table(
    caller = nm, `teosinte presence` = pres, lambda_GC = lam(sc$P),
    `n > perm 5%` = nsig(sc$P, gwas_thr(TR, "mlm", g)),
    `zmcct10 -log10P` = if (nrow(z)) round(max(z[is.finite(P) & P > 0, -log10(P)]), 2) else NA_real_
  )
}))
knitr::kable(ctab2, caption = "DTA MLM (Taxon+K) by ancestry caller at 0.4×, with n markers above the caller's own 1000-permutation 5% FWER line and the zmcct10 window-max -log10P. Aggregated callers (RTIGER, binhmm) recover teosinte presence ~0.10; per-SNP (control) and per-SNP recalls (nNIL, LB-Impute) under-call and deflate λ_GC.")

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
Scope. This note documents and defends an assumption used by the wideseq benchmark generator (make_wideseq_benchmark.R): that the per-sample coverage distribution can stand in for the per-site coverage distribution when simulating the ~27.6M teosinte-vs-B73 wideseq sites. The motivation is practical — per-site statistics over ~1,400 samples × ~27.6M sites is an unwieldy (sample × site) tensor, whereas per-sample and per-bin summaries are tractable. The point of this note is to state what is actually assumed, assess where it holds and where it does not, and lay out how to defend it (and partly replace it with measurement) for reviewers.
Framing — ergodic inspiration, scale-invariance claim. The inspiration is ergodic: the two axes here — the ensemble of samples and the sequence of positions (1 Mb bins) along the chromosomes — play the roles of ensemble and “time,” and ergodicity’s promise is that averaging along one genome equals averaging across the ensemble. That symmetry is what motivates re-using a per-sample coverage law at the per-bin scale. But the genomic axis is non-stationary (pericentromeres/repeats are systematically low), so the process is not ergodic in the textbook sense. The rigorous, testable claim we actually rely on is therefore narrower: that the missingness law missing(λ) is scale-invariant — the same function whether λ varies because you picked a low-depth sample or a low-mappability region — i.e. λ is a near-sufficient statistic for missingness. Ergodicity is the analogy; scale-invariance of missing(λ) is the claim.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
1. What is actually being assumed
The slogan “per-sample coverage ≈ per-site coverage” is, taken literally, false and should not be defended as written. The defensible claim is narrower and has two parts.

Within-sample exchangeability of sites within a bin. Every wideseq site in a sample is modeled as accumulating reads at a common per-sample rate λ_s, with the only site-level variation being Poisson read sampling plus a structural “always-missing” floor π. There is no systematic per-site coverage structure (GC, mappability, local copy number) beyond that floor.
Transfer of the between-sample law to the within-sample / per-bin scale. The missingness curve and the coverage distribution are estimated across samples, then applied across bins within a sample: a low-coverage bin is assumed to behave like a low-coverage sample.

Named precisely, this is scale-invariance of the missingness law: missing(λ) estimated across samples is the same function as missing(λ) across bins within a genome (equivalently, λ is a near-sufficient statistic for missingness at both scales). That is the testable core the ergodic symmetry points to — and section 4 tests it.

What the generator does

Per-sample mean coverage is drawn λ_s ~ Normal(0.590, 0.226), floored for positivity. (Fit to 1,434 BZea wideseq samples; Normal beats Gamma and lognormal by AIC −192 vs +205 vs +1091; CV 0.38, skew 0.34.)
Missingness uses the wideseq exp-floor model missing(λ) = π + (1−π)·e^(−kλ) with π = 0.346, k = 1.256 (mean missingness ≈ 0.67), fit across samples (missing_data.Rmd).
Each 1 Mb bin then draws, analytically and without materializing individual variants: VARIANT_COUNT ~ Poisson(sites_per_mb), INFORMATIVE_VARIANT_COUNT ~ Binomial(VARIANT_COUNT, present_prob), DEPTH_SUM = IVC + Poisson(IVC·(λ/present−1)), ALT_COUNT ~ Binomial(DEPTH_SUM, p_eff).

So λ_s and the population (π, k) are the only coverage inputs; every bin in a sample shares them. That sharing is the scale-invariance assumption — the ergodic symmetry — in operational form.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
section: 2. Assessment: where it is safe, where it bites
text:
2. Assessment: where it is safe, where it bites

Safe — and this is the load-bearing argument
The caller never sees a site. It consumes the per-bin ALT_FREQ, an aggregate over ~3,000–4,000 informative sites (real per-bin means: DEPTH_SUM ≈ 7,600 reads, INFORMATIVE_VARIANT_COUNT ≈ 4,300, ≈ 1.8 reads per covered site — shallow coverage, many sites). By the central limit theorem the bin statistic is governed by the mean per-site rate (≈ λ_s) and the effective site count, not by the shape of the per-site coverage distribution:
Var(ALT_FREQ) ≈ p(1 − p) / DEPTH_SUM
Consequently the per-site coverage decomposition is a nuisance the caller cannot distinguish and does not need: matching the per-bin DEPTH_SUM and informative-site count is sufficient to match calling behavior. The assumption is strongest exactly where it is used — at the bin aggregation scale.


Where it can genuinely bite
None of these is the per-sample-mean substitution itself; they are the real targets a careful reviewer will probe.

Spatial coverage structure (the principal risk). If low-coverage / low-mappability sites cluster — maize pericentromeres, repeats — then bins are not exchangeable: heterochromatic bins carry systematically fewer informative sites and lower depth than a flat sites_per_mb + constant π implies. This understates bin-to-bin variance and over-covers the hardest regions.
Reference-mapping bias against donor alleles. Teosinte (ALT) reads map worse to the B73 reference, so donor sites can be under-covered and under-called — a coverage × genotype interaction that a genotype-independent λ_s ignores. This is the same effect that makes the 0.4× skim under-call donor, and it is what a reviewer focused on introgression calling will attack first.
Between → within transfer of (π, k). The parameters are fit from between-sample variation but applied across bins within a sample. This is valid only if the missingness mechanism is the same at both scales.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
section: 3. How to defend it — the cheap data is the key
text:
3. How to defend it — the cheap data is the key
The same scale that makes the per-site tensor unwieldy (1,400 × 27.6M) is why it is not needed: the per-bin file (all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv, ~3M rows) is exactly the granularity at which the assumption can be tested rather than asserted. In rough order of impact:

Reframe the claim as bin-level sufficiency, not coverage equivalence. State that the inferential unit is the 1 Mb bin; the caller is a function of ALT_FREQ and the informative-site count; per-site coverage enters only through those bin aggregates; therefore matching the per-bin DEPTH_SUM / INFORMATIVE_VARIANT_COUNT distributions is sufficient. This preempts the “per-site ≠ per-sample” objection at the root.
Run the direct ergodicity test (cheap, decisive). From the per-bin file, fit missing ~ π + (1−π)e^(−kλ) within samples across bins and overlay it on the between-sample (π, k). If the within-sample bin-level curve coincides with the between-sample curve, the ergodic transfer is empirically validated — one figure answers the objection.
Validate the aggregate, not the atoms. Overlay the simulated per-bin DEPTH_SUM and ALT_FREQ distributions on the real per-bin distributions. Coincidence justifies the collapse at the scale that matters, irrespective of per-site detail.
Where it is cheap, replace the assumption with measurement. Instead of flat sites_per_mb + constant π, feed the real per-bin marginal profile (mean informative-site count and present-fraction per genomic bin, averaged over samples — directly computable from the per-bin file) into the generator. This neutralizes the spatial-structure risk and leaves ergodicity responsible only for sub-bin site behavior, which the CLT already protects. The weakest assumption becomes an empirical input.
Pre-empt with a sensitivity analysis. Simulate per-site rate heterogeneity (e.g. site rates ~ Gamma with the same per-sample mean) and show the bin-level ALT_FREQ distribution and the caller’s accuracy are invariant. “We injected per-site coverage dispersion and bin-level inference did not move” is a refutation, not a promise.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
section: 4. Validation result (move 2, run on the real data)
text:
4. Validation result (move 2, run on the real data)
We ran move 2 on the real per-bin file (all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv, 1,434 samples, 3.06M bins; agent/ergodicity_check.R / agent/ergodicity_real_vs_sim.R): fit missing = π + (1−π)e^(−kλ) within genomes across bins and between genomes across samples, and compare. As a control we ran the identical fit on the simulation.

1.256






Within- vs between-genome missingness, real vs simulation


Read this carefully — the test is only meaningful on real data. The simulation is ergodic by construction: make_*_benchmark.R draws every bin from one per-sample λ and a flat per-bin present_prob, so its within- and between-genome curves are identical to 3 decimals (Δπ ≈ 0, Δk ≈ 0.001) and recover the input (π=0.346, k=1.256) exactly. That is the control, not the validation — a coincidence on simulated data would prove nothing.
The real data is what validates the assumption: its within- and between-genome curves nearly coincide too (Δπ = 0.033, Δk = 0.080), separating only slightly in the high-λ tail (> 1×). So the missingness↔︎coverage law measured across samples does hold across bins within a genome — the per-sample coverage model transfers to the per-bin scale. The small residual (the real data being slightly less ergodic than the perfect simulation: bin floor 0.313 vs sample 0.346) is exactly the genuine per-bin spatial structure flagged below — now quantified and shown to be small.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
5. Limitations to state honestly

Spatial coverage structure (heterochromatin / repeats) — the source of the small real-data ergodicity residual above (Δπ ≈ 0.03); mitigated by move 4 (real per-bin profile); otherwise a flat per-Mb density understates bin-to-bin variance.
Reference bias on donor alleles — a separate coverage × genotype term, checkable by asking whether ALT_FREQ reaches ~1.0 in known homozygous-donor regions of high-confidence samples; if it falls short, add an ALT-specific coverage/error term.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
6. Bottom line
The assumption is defensible, provided the narrow version is defended: bin-level sufficiency with within-bin site exchangeability, protected by the CLT at ~10³–10⁴ sites per bin — not the literal “per-sample equals per-site coverage.” The per-bin file lets three of the objections become validation figures and one become a direct empirical input, so ergodicity can be presented as tested, and partly replaced by measurement, rather than assumed. The highest-value check — move 2, the within- vs between-genome missingness-curve overlay — has now been run on the real data and confirms the transfer (Δπ = 0.03, Δk = 0.08; section 4), with the simulation control showing the expected Δ ≈ 0.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
References / artifacts

Generator: make_wideseq_benchmark.R (per-bin analytic draw; λ ~ Normal).
Coverage / missingness model: BzeaSeq docs/missing_data.Rmd (λ = DEPTH_SUM/VARIANT_COUNT; exp-floor fit), wideseq row λ=0.59, π=0.346, k=1.256.
Per-sample λ fit: Normal(0.590, 0.226), n=1,434 (agent/fit_wideseq_lambda.R).
Per-bin data for validation: …/BZea/bzeaseq/ancestry/all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv.
Caller scored against this benchmark: score_wideseq.R (3-state Kgmm_HMM).

title: TeoNAM STAM — OLS GWAS on the authentic 118K GWAS panel (Fig 4C)
text:
What this is. The computation behind the STAM OLS GWAS scan on the authentic 118,838-SNP Chen 2019 GWAS panel (Qiuyue Chen’s Drive release), lifted to B73 v5 (data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_118k.csv), tracked in its own right. It ports the scripts/teonam_stam_gwas118k.R scan into a rendered notebook so the scan — not just its Manhattan — is reproducible from primary data. The annotated Fig-4C Manhattan that consumes this CSV lives in teonam-qtl-recovery; this notebook produces it.
This is the faithful Fig 4C marker set: Chen’s GWAS SNPs (GBS filtered at MAF > 0.01, missing rate < 0.75, then FSFHap-imputed per subpopulation, combined, and re-filtered to MAF > 0.05 / missing < 0.2 → 118,838 SNPs), lifted v2 → v5 (118,514 lifted). It replaces the 51K map-panel approximation used by teonam-stam-ols-gwas-family-imputed; the model is identical (STAM ~ Family + marker, 1-df F), so the two are directly comparable — only the marker set differs (118K GWAS panel vs 51K map panel). As there, each marker is tested only on the families that typed it (cross-family gaps left as NA, not densified), giving natural GWAS scatter.
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(parallel)
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 12))
THREADS <- max(1L, detectCores() - 2L)
LOD <- 5 # Chen 2019 significance line: P < 1e-5

title: TeoNAM STAM — OLS GWAS on the authentic 118K GWAS panel (Fig 4C)
text:
Inputs (primary 118K dosage + phenotype + v5 liftover)

# 118K GWAS-panel dosage: integer matrix [markers x lines], NA = untyped (0/1/2)
g118 <- readRDS(here("data/teonam/teonam_gwas118k_dosage.rds"))
dos <- g118$dos
lines <- colnames(dos)
fam <- factor(substr(lines, 1, 5)) # TIL01A001 -> TIL01

# phenotype: STAM (staminate spikelet), joined by line key
ph <- as.data.frame(read_excel(
  here("data/teonam/9250682/TeoNAM_1257RILs_22traits_phenotype_data.xlsx")
))
names(ph)[1] <- "line"
y <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(setNames(ph$STAM, ph$line)[lines]))

# v5 coordinates; restrict the scan to 118K markers that lifted to v5
mc <- fread(here("data/teonam/markers_v5_gwas118k.tsv"))
chr_by <- setNames(mc$chr_v5, mc$marker)
pos_by <- setNames(mc$pos_v5, mc$marker)
mk <- intersect(rownames(dos), mc$marker)
mk_idx <- match(mk, rownames(dos))

data.table(
  quantity = c(
    "lines", "markers (panel)", "markers (lifted, scanned)",
    "STAM phenotyped", "families"
  ),
  value = c(
    length(lines), nrow(dos), length(mk),
    sum(!is.na(y)), nlevels(fam)
  )
)

                    quantity  value
                      <char>  <int>
1:                     lines   1257
2:           markers (panel) 118838
3: markers (lifted, scanned) 118514
4:           STAM phenotyped   1256
5:                  families      5

title: TeoNAM STAM — OLS GWAS on the authentic 118K GWAS panel (Fig 4C)
text:
The model
Identical to the family-imputed 51K notebook. For each marker \(m\), restrict to the lines \(i\) with a non-missing dosage \(g_{im}\in\{0,1,2\}\) and a non-missing STAM value \(y_i\), then compare two nested least-squares fits:
\[
\textbf{Null:}\quad y_i = \mu + \sum_{f} \beta_f \,\mathbb{1}[\text{family}_i = f] + \varepsilon_i
\] \[
\textbf{Full:}\quad y_i = \mu + \sum_{f} \beta_f \,\mathbb{1}[\text{family}_i = f] + \gamma\, g_{im} + \varepsilon_i
\]
The marker enters as a single additive dosage effect \(\gamma\) shared across families (1 numerator df). Significance is the marginal \(F\)-test of \(\gamma\):
\[
F = \frac{(\mathrm{RSS}_0 - \mathrm{RSS}_1)/1}{\mathrm{RSS}_1/(n - \mathrm{rank})},
\qquad P = \Pr\big(F_{1,\,n-\mathrm{rank}} > F\big).
\]
Family is a fixed categorical covariate (levels dropped to those present for that marker). This is OLS: no kinship/polygenic random effect \(K\) — deliberately the same fixed-effects model as the 51K notebook, not Chen’s Q(5 PCs) + SNP + K(IBS) mixed model, so peak heights run hotter (the \(\lambda_{GC}\)/QQ comparison is a separate analysis).

title: TeoNAM STAM — OLS GWAS on the authentic 118K GWAS panel (Fig 4C)
TeoNAM STAM — OLS GWAS on the authentic 118K GWAS panel (Fig 4C)
text:
QC and results

fin <- scan[is.finite(P) & P > 0]
data.table(
  metric = c(
    "markers scanned", "markers tested (finite P)",
    "median lines / marker", "max -log10P",
    sprintf("genome-wide sig. (P < 1e-%d)", LOD)
  ),
  value = c(
    nrow(scan), nrow(fin), median(scan$n),
    round(max(-log10(fin$P)), 2), sum(fin$P < 10^(-LOD))
  )
)

  geom_point(size = 0.4) +

  scale_colour_manual(values = c("grey60", "black"), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = axis$mid, labels = axis$CHR, expand = c(0.01, 0)) +
  labs(
    x = "chromosome", y = expression(-log[10](italic(P))),
    title = "STAM 118K GWAS (OLS: STAM ~ Family + marker)"
  )

title: TeoNAM STAM — OLS GWAS on the authentic 118K GWAS panel (Fig 4C)
text:
Notes

This notebook is the canonical, tracked source for stam_gwas_scan_118k.csv; it ports scripts/teonam_stam_gwas118k.R verbatim (same OLS model as the 51K family-imputed notebook).
The marker set is the authentic Chen 2019 GWAS panel (118,838 SNPs → 118,514 lifted to v5), not the 51K map panel. Same model, different markers → the two OLS notebooks are directly comparable.
Model is OLS (fixed effects), no kinship term — the peak-height / \(\lambda_{GC}\) comparison against Chen’s Q + K mixed model is deferred to a dedicated inflation notebook (the 118K MLM analog is not yet built).

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Q+K) GWAS on the authentic 118K panel (Fig 4C)
text:
What this is. The GWAS model Chen 2019 actually used for Fig 4C — a linear mixed model with population structure (Q) and kinship (K) — run in TASSEL5 on the authentic 118,838-SNP GWAS panel (Qiuyue Chen’s Drive release, lifted to v5, 118,514 markers), and contrasted with the fixed-effect OLS scan (teonam-stam-ols-gwas-118k).
Crucially, this panel is only ~2.7% missing (Chen’s MR < 0.2 GWAS SNPs), so the MLM runs directly on it — no densification / step-interpolation. That preserves the natural per-SNP scatter of a real GWAS Manhattan. This is the faithful Fig-4C reproduction that the interpolated 51K MLM notebook (teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-interpolated) could not be: there, the 73%-missing 51K map panel had to be step-interpolated to a complete matrix, which made genotypes block-constant → a terraced/plateau Manhattan and a spurious over-correction (kinship absorbing each QTL’s own-chromosome signal). Here, with the real GWAS panel, neither artifact applies. Compute lives in scripts/teonam_mlm118k.R; this notebook loads, documents, and plots.
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 13))
LOD <- 5 # Chen 2019 significance line: P < 1e-5

# genomic inflation factor: median(chi^2_obs) / median(chi^2_null)
lambda_gc <- function(p) {
  p <- p[is.finite(p) & p > 0 & p <= 1]
  round(median(qchisq(p, df = 1, lower.tail = FALSE)) / qchisq(0.5, df = 1), 3)
}

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Q+K) GWAS on the authentic 118K panel (Fig 4C)
text:
The model
The OLS notebook fit fixed effects only: \(y_i = \mu + \sum_f \beta_f\,\mathbb{1}[\text{family}_i=f] + \gamma\,g_{im} + \varepsilon_i\). Chen’s GWAS adds a polygenic random term with covariance \(\mathbf K\) (Yu et al. 2006):
\[
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{1}\mu + \mathbf{Q}\boldsymbol{\alpha}
+ \mathbf{g}_a\,\gamma_a + \mathbf{g}_d\,\gamma_d
+ \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{u} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon},
\qquad
\mathbf{u}\sim N(\mathbf 0,\,\mathbf K\sigma^2_g),\;
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\sim N(\mathbf 0,\,\mathbf I\sigma^2_e).
\]

\(\mathbf Q\) = first 5 PCs (fixed; from a mean-imputed copy of the 118K dosage — PCA only).
\(\mathbf g_a\) additive dosage, \(\mathbf g_d\) dominance indicator; TASSEL tests the joint 2-df \(H_0:\gamma_a=\gamma_d=0\).
\(\mathbf K\) = Centered-IBS kinship, computed on the raw 118K panel (observed allele freq; per-site missing handled).
P3D: variance components fit once under the null (REML/EMMA), reused per marker (GLS \(F\)-test with \(\mathbf V^{-1}\)).

The MLM’s \(\mathbf K\) term is the structure/relatedness correction OLS lacks; its effect is the drop in \(\lambda_{GC}\) below.

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Q+K) GWAS on the authentic 118K panel (Fig 4C)
section: Pipeline (scripts/teonam_mlm118k.R, native arm64 TASSEL5) — NO densification
text:
Pipeline (scripts/teonam_mlm118k.R, native arm64 TASSEL5) — NO densification

# 1. build: 118K dosage (markers x lines, ~2.7% NA) -> hapmap DIRECTLY (N at missing;
#    NO interpolation). Q = 5 PCs from a mean-imputed copy (PCA only). pheno = STAM + PC1..5.
# 2. K: TASSEL Centered_IBS on the raw (2.7%-missing) 118K hapmap
run_pipeline.pl -importGuess geno_gwas_118k.hmp.txt \
  -KinshipPlugin -method Centered_IBS -endPlugin -export kinship_cIBS_118k.txt -exportType SqrMatrix
# 3. MLM (P3D): genotype + phenotype(+Q) + K; TASSEL drops missing taxa per site
run_pipeline.pl -fork1 -h geno_gwas_118k.hmp.txt -fork2 -r pheno_stam_pc_118k.txt \
  -fork3 -k kinship_cIBS_118k.txt -combine4 -input1 -input2 -intersect \
  -combine5 -input4 -input3 -mlm -mlmVarCompEst P3D -mlmCompressionLevel None -export mlm_118k


tim <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/mlm_118k_timings.csv"))
knitr::kable(tim, caption = "TASSEL5 step timings (native arm64; MLM uses pure-Java EJML)")

text:
QQ — OLS vs MLM (Q+K)

qd <- two[, {
  p <- P[is.finite(P) & P > 0 & P <= 1]
  o <- -log10(sort(p))
  e <- -log10(ppoints(length(p)))
  k <- c(which(o > 2), sample(which(o <= 2), min(sum(o <= 2), 8000)))
  .(exp = e[k], obs = o[k])
}, by = mlab]
ggplot(qd, aes(exp, obs, colour = mlab)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal, name = NULL) +
  guides(colour = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 2.5, alpha = 1))) +

    x = expression(Expected ~ -log[10](italic(P))), y = expression(Observed ~ -log[10](italic(P))),

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Q+K) GWAS on the authentic 118K panel (Fig 4C)
text:
Notes

Canonical, tracked source for the 118K MLM: scripts/teonam_mlm118k.R (build → K → MLM). Outputs: data/teonam/tassel/mlm_118k2.txt, results/sim/teonam/mlm_118k_timings.csv.
No densification. The 118K GWAS panel is ~2.7% missing, so the MLM runs on it directly (TASSEL handles per-site missing) — the Manhattan keeps its natural scatter. This is the faithful Fig-4C model that the interpolated 51K MLM notebook explicitly could not provide (it densified the 73%-missing map panel → plateau + over-correction).
Model still OLS-vs-MLM only. The nested (family:marker) specification — Chen’s primary JLM — and the additive-only (1-df) vs joint (2-df) choice are the same open model-specification points discussed in the interpolated notebook; a LOCO over-correction diagnostic is not needed here (no interpolation pseudo-replication), and is left as optional future work.

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
What this is. The MLM counterpart of teonam-qtl-recovery-118k: the same figures — JLM lollipop (Fig 4A), the authentic 118K GWAS Manhattan, the coverage-degradation composite, caller noisiness — but the GWAS model is a mixed model instead of OLS. The fixed structure is the 5-family factor (the OLS/JLM structure), so this is literally “OLS (Family + marker) + a K random effect” and the OLS↔︎MLM contrast is the K term alone. In TeoNAM the structure is the five families, so this matches Chen’s Q = 5 PCs (Q ≈ Family; interchangeable — scripts/teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). Chen’s exact Fig-4C (TASSEL Q = 5 PCs + Centered_IBS K) and the QQ/λ_GC head-to-head are the sibling notebook teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.
The central MLM result is a confound, not a bug: for the domestication traits K = teosinte-ancestry relatedness is nearly collinear with the QTL (a RIL is “teosinte-like” genome-wide because it carries teosinte alleles at tb1 etc.), so the mixed model absorbs the very signal it is meant to protect — worst where K is best estimated (high coverage / full panel). This is why Chen used JLM (Fig 4A), not MLM, for the STAM QTL, and why the degradation sweep runs backwards under MLM.
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(fastman)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)
library(scales)
library(cowplot)
Show code
source(here("scripts/teonam_notebook_plots.R")) # shared plot helpers: get_transformer, plot_manhattan/lollipop/sweep_line, lambda_gc, read_mlm_tassel, LOD

# Panel B = MLM (Family + K) on the authentic per-SNP panel (R-EMMAX; scripts/
# teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). Family (not 5 PCs) so B and C share the OLS structure and
# the OLS<->MLM contrast is the K term alone. The faithful TASSEL Q+K Fig-4C (Chen's
# exact model) is the sibling notebook teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.html; read_mlm_tassel()
# below still parses it if needed.
mlm <- fread(here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_mlm_family_118k.csv"))

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
text:
Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
For each STAM candidate gene (B73 v5), the closest marker (smallest bp distance to the gene body) vs the window-max marker (highest −log₁₀P within ±500 kb) in the authentic 118K MLM (Family+K) scan. As under OLS they are not the same marker: the closest marker sits on the gene but carries little signal; the local peak is a nearby but distinct marker.


Show code
sc <- copy(mlm)[is.finite(P) & P > 0][, logP := -log10(P)]
cand <- c(ts2 = "STAM1.1", tb1 = "STAM1.2", tru1 = "STAM3.1", zmm16 = "STAM3.2", tsh4 = "STAM7.1", an1 = "GWAS(chr1)")
sym <- fread(here("data/ref/maize_v5_gene_symbols.tsv"))
setnames(sym, c("gene_id", "symbol"))
coord <- fread(here("data/ref/b73_gene_coords.tsv"), header = FALSE, col.names = c("gene_id", "chr", "start", "end"))
gg <- merge(sym[symbol %in% names(cand)], coord, by = "gene_id")
gg[, chr := as.integer(sub("^chr", "", chr))][, qtl := cand[symbol]]
WIN <- 5e5
ctab <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_len(nrow(gg)), function(i) {
  ch <- gg$chr[i]
  s <- gg$start[i]
  e <- gg$end[i]
  m <- sc[CHR == ch]
  d <- pmax(0L, pmax(s - m$BP, m$BP - e))
  kc <- which.min(d)
  w <- m[BP >= s - WIN & BP <= e + WIN]
  kw <- which.max(w$logP)
  data.table(
    gene = gg$symbol[i], QTL = gg$qtl[i], chr = ch, `gene start (v5)` = s,
    `closest Δstart (bp)` = m$BP[kc] - s, `closest -log10P` = round(m$logP[kc], 2),
    `window-max Δstart (bp)` = w$BP[kw] - s, `window-max -log10P` = round(w$logP[kw], 2)
  )
}))
setorder(ctab, chr, `gene start (v5)`)
knitr::kable(ctab, format.args = list(big.mark = ","), caption = paste(
  "STAM candidate genes (B73 v5): CLOSEST marker vs highest -log10P marker within +/-500 kb",
  "(authentic 118K MLM Family+K scan). Δstart = marker bp - gene start (v5), signed."
))

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
Authentic 118K panel — MLM (Family + K) scan
The mixed model with the OLS structure — fixed = the 5-family factor, random = K (VanRaden), joint 2-df additive+dominance F (R-EMMAX; scripts/teonam_mlm_family_118k.R) — run on the authentic per-SNP 118K panel (~2.7% missing, per-marker mean-imputed; the real per-SNP scatter is preserved). Family instead of 5 PCs so this is exactly the OLS model (Family + marker) plus K, making the OLS↔︎MLM contrast the K term alone; in TeoNAM the structure is the five families, so Q ≈ Family (interchangeable — candidate peaks within 0.2, λ_GC 1.09 vs 1.12; teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). Chen’s exact Fig-4C model (TASSEL Q = 5 PCs + Centered_IBS K) is the sibling notebook teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.


Show code
p_mlm <- plot_manhattan(
  mlm,
  "STAM — GWAS MLM (Family + K), authentic 118K panel, candidate genes at v5 positions",
  overlap_csv = here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv"),
  lod = teonam_gwas_thr("STAM", "mlm"), # 1000-permutation genome-wide FWER (5%)
  out_png = here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_mlm_manhattan_annotated_118k.png")
)

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: OLS → MLM on the same panel: the tb1 confound
text:
OLS → MLM on the same panel: the tb1 confound
The fixed-effect OLS scan (Family + marker, no K) vs the MLM (Family + K) on the identical 118K panel — same fixed part, K the only addition. The MLM deflates λ_GC toward 1 and collapses the domestication peaks: tb1 (chr1) and tru1/zmm16 (chr3) that dominate OLS are flattened, because teosinte ancestry there is collinear with K.


Show code
ols <- fread(here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_118k.csv"))[is.finite(P) & P > 0, .(SNP, CHR = as.integer(CHR), BP = as.integer(BP), P)]
lam_tab <- data.table(
  model = c("OLS (Family + marker, no K)", "MLM (Family + K)"),
  markers = c(nrow(ols), nrow(mlm)),
  lambda_GC = c(lambda_gc(ols$P), lambda_gc(mlm$P)),
  max_logP = round(c(max(-log10(ols$P)), max(-log10(mlm$P))), 2)
)
knitr::kable(lam_tab, caption = "OLS vs MLM on the authentic 118K panel, same Family fixed part: adding K deflates genomic inflation and the top peak.")

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: Composite (A: JLM lollipop · B: MLM authentic 118K · C: MLM FSFHap ancestry-imputed)
text:
Composite (A: JLM lollipop · B: MLM authentic 118K · C: MLM FSFHap ancestry-imputed)
Panel C is the direct MLM analog of the OLS notebook’s ancestry-imputed panel: the same FSFHap W22↔︎teosinte block mosaic (Chen’s own ancestry inference, teonam_gwas118k_dosage_fsfhap), scanned as Family + K — the OLS panel’s fixed part (the 5-family factor) plus a K random effect, so the OLS↔︎MLM contrast is the K term alone. Family is used instead of 5 PCs because in TeoNAM the structure is the five families (Q ≈ Family; on this mosaic Family+K is the better-calibrated of the two, λ_GC 1.13 vs 1.26 for 5 PCs — scripts/teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). K is estimated from the FSFHap genotypes themselves. Compute: scripts/teonam_mlm_fsfhap_118k.R. So B and C contrast the per-SNP panel against the ancestry-imputed mosaic: peaks at the same loci but C block-terraced, and the mixed model suppresses the domestication peaks in both (K absorbs the ancestry-collinear QTL — the confound).


Show code
fsf <- fread(here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_mlm_fsfhap_118k.csv"))
lgc_fsf <- lambda_gc(fsf$P)
p_anc <- plot_manhattan(
  fsf,
  sprintf("STAM — MLM (Family + K), FSFHap ancestry-imputed 118K panel (λ_GC = %.2f)", lgc_fsf),
  overlap_csv = here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv"),
  out_png = here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_mlm_fsfhap_manhattan_118k.png")
)
composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  p_lolli, p_mlm, p_anc,
  ncol = 1, labels = c("A", "B", "C"), label_size = 20, align = "v", axis = "lr"
)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_composite_mlm_118k.png"), composite, width = 9, height = 13, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
composite

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: RTIGER ancestry × coverage under MLM (degradation sweep)
text:
RTIGER ancestry × coverage under MLM (degradation sweep)
The RTIGER coverage sweep under the MLM (Family+K). Reads are Poisson-sampled from the authentic 118K per-SNP genotypes over λ ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, ∞}×; RTIGER infers ancestry directly on all 118K markers, per chromosome (no thinning, no back-projection), and the per-coverage GL-dosage null (its honest Family+K at that depth) is applied. λ = ∞ is RTIGER’s deterministic ceiling. Compute: scripts/teonam_rtiger_sweep_118k.R (writes the OLS and _mlm sweep CSVs); this chunk plots.
Rigidity. RTIGER’s rigidity is a marker-count minimum-segment length. The calibrated rigidity_star = 2 over-segments ~5–6× on this dense grid; a cM-distance rule fails too because the interpolated cM map is heavily tied (median gap ≈ 0). So rigidity is calibrated empirically on the full grid — chr1, one family, λ=∞, swept against the FSFHap truth breakpoint density (agent/rtiger_rigsweep_chr1.R): rigidity = 60 reproduces FSFHap’s ~10.4 breakpoints/chr1 (recovered 10.6). That truth-matched segmentation is what makes the profile below smooth rather than the earlier over-segmented sawtooth.
The coverage order is inverted relative to OLS: low coverage rides highest, ∞ lowest. At shallow depth the GL-dosage K is too noisy to correct structure, so the ancestry-confounded QTL leaks through; at ∞ the kinship is well-estimated and crushes it. The chr3 broad 0.1× elevation is a low-coverage MLM artifact (poor K + block recovery in the pericentromere). One replicate per cell.


Show code
p_sweep <- plot_sweep_line(
  here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_rtiger_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"),
  "STAM — RTIGER ancestry, MLM (Family+K) −log10P vs coverage",
  base_size = 15, legend_corner = "topright"
)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_rtiger_118k_mlm_sweep_manhattan.png"), p_sweep, width = 9, height = 4.6, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
p_sweep

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: MLM coverage-degradation composite: control / nNIL / RTIGER / LB-Impute / binhmm
text:
MLM coverage-degradation composite: control / nNIL / RTIGER / LB-Impute / binhmm
The five-caller contrast under MLM. Same pipeline as the OLS composite (authentic per-SNP truth → per-caller ancestry on the 0.1 cM grid → back-project → GWAS), scanned with the per-coverage GL-dosage Family+K null instead of OLS. λ = ∞ is each caller’s own deterministic ceiling (caller-specific, no shared baseline).
Because MLM removes the (confounded) tb1 signal from every caller, the panels no longer differentiate on QTL power — they differentiate on false-positive suppression, which follows the caller-noisiness ordering below (a property of the recovered ancestry geometry):

A — interpolation control (GL + HWE, no HMM): noisiest — dense false peaks genome-wide (to ~15), driven by mid coverages; no smoothing for K to work with.
B — nNIL: cleaner but still busy — scattered false peaks, chr3 elevated.
C — RTIGER: cleanest — little survives LOD; only the chr3 0.1× artifact and a modest tb1.
D — LB-Impute: also clean — tb1 to ~10–11 at 0.1×, flat elsewhere.
E — binhmm (1 Mb bins): clean, like C/D — the coarse bin resolution suppresses fine-scale false peaks; a chr1 tb1 cluster (to ~8, strongest at low coverage) and a modest chr3, otherwise flat.

Read the panels for false-positive control, not QTL power (which MLM deliberately removes). One replicate per (family, λ) cell.


Show code
pA <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_control_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "STAM MLM (Family+K) — interpolation control (GL + HWE)", legend = TRUE, legend_corner = "topright")
pB <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_nnil_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "STAM MLM (Family+K) — nNIL ancestry", legend = FALSE)
pC <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_rtiger_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "STAM MLM (Family+K) — RTIGER ancestry", legend = FALSE)
pD <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_lbimpute_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "STAM MLM (Family+K) — LB-Impute ancestry (map-aware cM)", legend = FALSE)
pE <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_binhmm_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "STAM MLM (Family+K) — binhmm ancestry (1 Mb bins)", legend = FALSE)
composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(pA, pB, pC, pD, pE, ncol = 1, align = "v", axis = "lr", labels = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"), label_size = 18)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_sweep_composite_mlm_118k.png"), composite, width = 9, height = 20, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
composite

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: tb1 peak height vs coverage (the inverted trend)
text:
tb1 peak height vs coverage (the inverted trend)
Quantifying the reversal: the tb1 local peak (max −log₁₀P within ±500 kb) by caller and coverage under MLM. Height falls as coverage rises — the opposite of a normal power curve — because a sharper GL-dosage K at high depth more completely absorbs the ancestry-confounded QTL.


Show code
tb1 <- ov[symbol == "tb1"]
callers <- c(control = "control", nNIL = "nnil", RTIGER = "rtiger", `LB-Impute` = "lbimpute", binhmm = "binhmm")
rows <- rbindlist(lapply(names(callers), function(nm) {
  sw <- fread(here(sprintf("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_%s_118k_mlm_sweep.csv", callers[nm])))
  sw[CHR == tb1$chr & abs(BP - tb1$start) <= 5e5 & is.finite(P) & P > 0,
    .(caller = nm, tb1_logP = round(max(-log10(P)), 2)),
    by = coverage
  ]
}))
wide <- dcast(rows, coverage ~ caller, value.var = "tb1_logP")
wide[, covlab := ifelse(is.finite(coverage), formatC(coverage), "Inf")]
setorder(wide, -coverage)
knitr::kable(wide[, .(coverage = covlab, control, nNIL, RTIGER, `LB-Impute`, binhmm)],
  caption = "tb1 peak -log10P (+/-500 kb) by caller x coverage under MLM (Family+K). Height decreases with coverage (Inf lowest): sharper K absorbs the ancestry-confounded QTL more fully at high depth."
)

title: TeoNAM STAM — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
Caller noisiness (band-limited spectral power)
Spurious fine-scale structure each caller injects, as spectral power in a physically- defined noise band (R, P10) — R = 0.1 cM grid resolution, P10 = 10th-percentile resolvable teosinte introgression tract. This is a property of the recovered ancestry geometry (the back-projected block structure), which is identical between OLS and MLM — only the downstream GWAS model differs — so the ranking below (measured on the tracks in the OLS notebook, scripts/teonam_sweep_noisiness.R) carries over: RTIGER ≈ LB-Impute run ~9× cleaner than control ≈ nNIL, which is exactly the false-positive ordering seen in the MLM composite above.


Show code
bp <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/sweep_noisiness_band_118k.csv"))
knitr::kable(bp, caption = sprintf("Noise band: resolution R = %d kb, P10 resolvable introgression = %d kb (median tract %.2f Mb).", bp$R_kb, bp$P10_kb, bp$median_frag_Mb))



Noise band: resolution R = 77 kb, P10 resolvable introgression = 181 kb (median tract 6.09 Mb).

knitr::kable(nz, caption = "Noise-band power fraction at lambda=Inf (each caller's ceiling), least->most noisy. FFT (headline), Haar-wavelet, and lag-1 spatial autocorrelation (acf_lag1).")

Noisiness is the inverse of spatial autocorrelation. The acf_lag1 column — the lag-1 autocorrelation of the −log₁₀P marker track, measured directly (not from the FFT) — ranks the callers in exactly the reverse order of the band-power: binhmm / rtiger are both least noisy and most autocorrelated (acf ≈ 0.999), control / nNIL noisiest and least (acf ≈ 0.959). This is not a coincidence but Wiener–Khinchin: the periodogram is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function, so band-limited high-frequency power and short-range autocorrelation are two views of the same second-order structure. Either one selects the same “reasonably good” callers. The framework behind this diagnostic — using the GWAS association landscape’s spatial geometry to rank ancestry callers — is developed in GWAS association-landscape geometry.

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
text:
What this is. The STAM GWAS Manhattan (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) reproduced on the TeoNAM genotypes, with the domestication candidate genes placed at their real B73 v5 coordinates (MaizeGDB genemodel_locus → b73_gene_coords), a red star at each gene’s genomic position, and a dotted significance line — the raw-panel OLS Manhattan now uses a 1000-permutation genome-wide FWER (the FSFHap-imputed panel and the coverage sweeps keep Chen’s fixed LOD 5 as their reference).
This notebook loads pre-computed CSVs and plots (compute lives in the scripts/ directory): the per-marker GWAS scans (teonam_stam_gwas_scan.R interpolated / teonam_stam_gwas_family_imputed.R family-imputed) and the candidate-gene overlap (teonam_candidate_overlap.R). We show both the family-imputed scan (natural scatter) and the interpolated/densified scan (block-terraced — the artifact of step-interpolated genotypes, see below).
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(fastman)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)
library(scales)
library(cowplot)
Show code
# Coordinate transformer replicating fastman_gg's internal x layout, so we can
# place gene labels / lines at arbitrary (chr, bp). From inv4m make_manhattan_plots.Rmd.
get_transformer <- function(m) {
  cmat <- do.call(rbind, lapply(
    split(m[order(m$CHR, m$BP), ], m$CHR[order(m$CHR, m$BP)]),
    function(d) {
      data.frame(
        CHR = d$CHR[1], min_bp = min(d$BP),
        width = max(d$BP) - min(d$BP),
        medgap = if (nrow(d) > 1) median(diff(sort(d$BP))) else 1
      )
    }
  ))
  cmat <- cmat[order(cmat$CHR), ]
  maxgap <- max(cmat$medgap, na.rm = TRUE)
  numc <- nrow(cmat)
  cmat$base <- 0
  cmat$midp <- 0
  cmat$midp[1] <- cmat$width[1] / 2
  for (i in 2:numc) {
    cmat$base[i] <- cmat$base[i - 1] + cmat$width[i - 1] + maxgap
    cmat$midp[i] <- cmat$base[i] + cmat$width[i] / 2
  }
  fac <- numc / cmat$midp[numc]
  cmat$basef <- fac * cmat$base
  function(chr, bp) {
    i <- match(chr, cmat$CHR)
    fac * (bp - cmat$min_bp[i]) + cmat$basef[i]
  }
}

LOD <- 5 # Chen 2019: P < 1e-5 (LOD = 5) — fixed fallback + FSFHap/sweep panels

# Per-(trait, model) TeoNAM GWAS permutation threshold (raw 118K point Manhattan),
# written by teonam_stam_gwas118k.R / teonam_mlm_family_118k.R. Falls back to LOD.
teonam_gwas_thr <- function(trait, model, geno = "raw118k", alpha = 0.05) {
  d <- tryCatch(fread(here("data/teonam/gwas_perm_thresholds.csv")), error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(d)) {
    return(LOD)
  }
  wt <- toupper(trait)
  wm <- model
  wg <- geno
  wa <- alpha
  h <- d[trait == wt & model == wm & geno == wg & alpha == wa, thr_neglog10p]
  if (length(h) && is.finite(h[1])) h[1] else LOD
}

#' STAM GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes at their true v5 positions.
#' Odd chromosomes black, even gray75; y-axis expanded one major-tick unit above
#' the top tick; gene labels repelled into the headroom above the maximum peak
#' (segments back to the red star at each gene's local peak).
#' @param scan_csv per-marker scan (SNP, CHR, BP[v5], P)
#' @param title    plot title
#' @param out_png  optional path to save a PNG
plot_stam_manhattan <- function(scan_csv, title, out_png = NULL, mark = NULL, lod = LOD) {
  scan <- if (is.data.frame(scan_csv)) as.data.frame(scan_csv) else as.data.frame(fread(scan_csv))
  scan <- scan[is.finite(scan$P) & scan$P > 0, ]
  m <- scan[order(scan$CHR, scan$BP), c("SNP", "CHR", "BP", "P")]

  ov <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv")) # symbol, chr, start (v5)
  # star height = local max -log10P within +/-500 kb IN THIS scan
  ov[, y := vapply(seq_len(.N), function(i) {
    w <- m$P[m$CHR == chr[i] & abs(m$BP - start[i]) <= 5e5]
    if (length(w)) -log10(min(w)) else NA_real_
  }, numeric(1))]
  tr <- get_transformer(m)
  ov[, BPn := tr(chr, start)]
  ov <- ov[is.finite(y)]

  mp <- -log10(min(m$P)) # maximum peak
  ticks <- pretty(c(0, mp))
  unit <- diff(ticks)[1]
  ytop <- max(ticks) + unit # one major-tick unit of headroom

  p <- fastman_gg(
    m = m, snp = "SNP", col = c("black", "gray75"), maxP = mp,
    genomewideline = NULL, suggestiveline = NULL,
    ylab = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), xlab = ""
  ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = lod, linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.7, color = "black") +
    geom_point(
      data = ov, aes(x = BPn, y = y), inherit.aes = FALSE,
      shape = 8, size = 3, stroke = 1, color = "red"
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = ov, aes(x = BPn, y = y, label = symbol),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, fontface = "italic", size = 5,
      segment.color = "grey50", min.segment.length = 0, box.padding = 0.5,
      max.overlaps = Inf, ylim = c(mp + 0.3 * unit, ytop)
    ) + # above the max peak
    ggtitle(title) +
    theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      breaks = ticks, limits = c(0, ytop),
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))
    ) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 1, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2)
    )

  if (!is.null(mark)) { # optional dashed marker at an arbitrary locus
    xm <- tr(mark$chr, mark$bp)
    p <- p + geom_vline(xintercept = xm, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.5)
  }
  if (!is.null(out_png)) ggsave(out_png, p, width = 9, height = 4.6, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
  p
}

#' Chen 2019 Fig 4A: JLM QTL genomic distribution as a lollipop plot.
#' Full 10-chromosome axis is obtained by passing the dense scan to fastman_gg
#' with fully transparent points (col = transparent); the JLM QTL are overlaid as
#' blue stems + points, candidate genes labeled bold-italic above.
#' @param jlm_txt  TASSEL StepwiseOLSModelFitter output (stam_jlm_native1.txt): Name,
#'   Locus, Position (v5 bp), `pr>F`
#' @param scan_csv dense per-marker scan, only used (transparent) to lay out the axis
plot_stam_lollipop <- function(jlm_txt, scan_csv, title, out_png = NULL) {
  scan <- as.data.frame(fread(scan_csv))
  scan <- scan[is.finite(scan$P) & scan$P > 0, ]
  maxis <- scan[order(scan$CHR, scan$BP), c("SNP", "CHR", "BP", "P")]

  j <- fread(jlm_txt)
  j <- j[!(Name %in% c("mean", "Family", "Error"))]
  q <- data.table(
    SNP = j$Name, CHR = as.integer(j$Locus), BP = as.integer(j$Position),
    logP = -log10(as.numeric(j$`pr>F`))
  )
  tr_ <- toupper(sub("_jlm.*$", "", basename(jlm_txt))) # JLM perm threshold (Chen-style FWER)
  .thrcsv <- here("data/teonam/jlm_perm_thresholds.csv")
  .jlm_thr <- if (file.exists(.thrcsv)) {
    .d <- fread(.thrcsv)
    .h <- .d[trait == tr_ & alpha == 0.05, thr_neglog10p]
    if (length(.h) && is.finite(.h[1])) .h[1] else LOD
  } else {
    LOD
  }
  q <- q[logP >= .jlm_thr] # keep QTL clearing the 1000-permutation FWER (5%) line
  ov <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv")) # symbol, chr, start (v5)
  ov <- ov[symbol %in% c("ts2", "tb1", "tru1", "zmm16", "tsh4")] # JLM Fig-4A candidates (an1 is GWAS-only)
  q[, gene := ""] # assign each candidate gene to its NEAREST JLM QTL (1:1)
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(ov))) {
    idx <- which(q$CHR == ov$chr[i])
    if (length(idx)) {
      k <- idx[which.min(abs(q$BP[idx] - ov$start[i]))]
      q$gene[k] <- ov$symbol[i]
    }
  }
  tr <- get_transformer(maxis)
  q[, BPn := tr(CHR, BP)]

  maxlogP <- max(q$logP)
  brks <- seq(0, floor(maxlogP / 10) * 10, by = 10)
  ytop <- maxlogP * 1.18 # headroom for the top label

  p <- fastman_gg(
    m = maxis, snp = "SNP", col = c("#00000000", "#00000000"),
    maxP = maxlogP, genomewideline = NULL, suggestiveline = NULL,
    ylab = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), xlab = ""
  ) +
    geom_segment(
      data = q, aes(x = BPn, xend = BPn, y = 0, yend = logP),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, color = "blue", linewidth = 0.5
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = q, aes(x = BPn, y = logP), inherit.aes = FALSE,
      color = "blue", size = 2.5
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = q[gene != ""], aes(x = BPn, y = logP, label = gene),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, fontface = "bold.italic", size = 5,
      segment.color = NA, box.padding = 0.4, point.padding = 0.3,
      nudge_y = 0.05 * maxlogP, seed = 1, max.overlaps = Inf
    ) +
    ggtitle(title) + theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = brks, limits = c(0, ytop), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 1, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2)
    )
  if (!is.null(out_png)) ggsave(out_png, p, width = 9, height = 4, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
  p
}

#' Coverage-sweep line-Manhattan (geom_line, one line per coverage).
#' −log10P vs genomic position along the union grid; `group = interaction(CHR,
#' coverage)` so lines never connect across chromosomes. Color = coverage on a
#' viridis ramp, the λ = ∞ truth baseline in black. LOD = 5 dotted. NO gene labels.
#' Reused for the control / nNIL / RTIGER panels of the degradation composite.
#' @param sweep_csv combined sweep CSV (SNP, CHR, BP[v5], P, coverage; coverage=Inf
#'   for the λ=∞ baseline rows)
#' @param title panel title (markdown)
#' @param legend show the coverage legend (FALSE for composite panels sharing one)
#' @param base_size theme base size
plot_sweep_line <- function(sweep_csv, title, legend = TRUE, base_size = 14) {
  sw <- as.data.table(fread(sweep_csv))
  sw <- sw[is.finite(P) & P > 0]
  sw[, logP := -log10(P)]
  # x layout from the union grid (every coverage carries the identical marker set)
  tr <- get_transformer(as.data.frame(sw[coverage == sw$coverage[1], .(CHR, BP)]))
  sw[, BPn := tr(CHR, BP)]

  # Legend order: infinity (truth) at top, then coverage in descending order.
  cov_levels <- c("∞", "20", "10", "5", "1", "0.5", "0.2", "0.1")
  sw[, cov_lab := factor(ifelse(is.finite(coverage), formatC(coverage), "∞"),
    levels = cov_levels
  )]
  # Colour mapping is fixed by coverage value (0.1 = darkest viridis ... 20 =
  # lightest; ∞ = black), independent of the legend order above.
  cov_asc <- c("0.1", "0.2", "0.5", "1", "5", "10", "20")
  pal <- setNames(grDevices::hcl.colors(7, "viridis"), cov_asc)
  pal["∞"] <- "black"
  axis_df <- sw[, .(mid = (min(BPn) + max(BPn)) / 2), by = CHR][order(CHR)]
  ytop <- 20 # shared y-limit across all panels for cross-method comparison

  # Draw order (z-stack): 20x first, then descending to 0.1x, and ∞ LAST so the
  # darker low-coverage / black-truth profiles sit on top. dord = -coverage sorts
  # finite coverage descending (20 -> -20 first); ∞ -> Inf sorts last.
  sw[, dord := ifelse(is.finite(coverage), -coverage, Inf)]

  ggplot(
    sw[order(dord, CHR, BP)],
    aes(x = BPn, y = logP, color = cov_lab, group = interaction(CHR, coverage))
  ) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = LOD, linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.7, color = "grey30") +
    scale_color_manual(values = pal, name = "coverage (×)", drop = FALSE) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = axis_df$mid, labels = axis_df$CHR, expand = c(0.01, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ytop), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))) +
    labs(x = "chromosome", y = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), title = title) +
    theme_classic(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 0, size = base_size, face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2),
      # legend INSIDE the plotting area, top-right corner (top panel only)
      legend.position = if (legend) c(0.995, 0.97) else "none",
      legend.justification = c(1, 1),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.7), colour = NA),
      legend.key.size = unit(0.85, "lines"),
      legend.title = element_text(size = base_size - 3),
      legend.text = element_text(size = base_size - 4),
      legend.margin = margin(1, 3, 1, 3)
    ) +
    guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(linewidth = 1.2)))
}

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
section: Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
text:
Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
For each STAM candidate gene (B73 v5), two different “nearby marker” choices from the authentic 118,514-marker 118K GWAS scan (OLS): the closest marker (smallest bp distance to the gene body) and the window-max marker (highest −log₁₀P within ±500 kb — a 1 Mb window centered on the gene). They are not the same marker at any gene: the closest marker sits essentially on the gene but carries little signal, while the significant local signal is a nearby but distinct marker. This is the reference for what “near the gene” means in the Fig 4A lollipop below (which labels each gene onto its nearest JLM QTL).


Show code
sc <- fread(here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_118k.csv"))
sc <- sc[is.finite(P) & P > 0][, logP := -log10(P)]
cand <- c(
  ts2 = "STAM1.1", tb1 = "STAM1.2", tru1 = "STAM3.1",
  zmm16 = "STAM3.2", tsh4 = "STAM7.1", an1 = "GWAS(chr1)"
)
sym <- fread(here("data/ref/maize_v5_gene_symbols.tsv"))
setnames(sym, c("gene_id", "symbol"))
coord <- fread(here("data/ref/b73_gene_coords.tsv"),
  header = FALSE,
  col.names = c("gene_id", "chr", "start", "end")
)
gg <- merge(sym[symbol %in% names(cand)], coord, by = "gene_id")
gg[, chr := as.integer(sub("^chr", "", chr))][, qtl := cand[symbol]]
WIN <- 5e5 # +/-500 kb around the gene body (1 Mb window)
ctab <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_len(nrow(gg)), function(i) {
  ch <- gg$chr[i]
  s <- gg$start[i]
  e <- gg$end[i]
  m <- sc[CHR == ch]
  d <- pmax(0L, pmax(s - m$BP, m$BP - e))
  kc <- which.min(d) # closest to gene body
  w <- m[BP >= s - WIN & BP <= e + WIN]
  kw <- which.max(w$logP) # max signal in window
  data.table(
    gene = gg$symbol[i], QTL = gg$qtl[i], chr = ch, `gene start (v5)` = s,
    `closest Δstart (bp)` = m$BP[kc] - s, `closest -log10P` = round(m$logP[kc], 2),
    `window-max Δstart (bp)` = w$BP[kw] - s, `window-max -log10P` = round(w$logP[kw], 2)
  )
}))
setorder(ctab, chr, `gene start (v5)`)
knitr::kable(ctab, format.args = list(big.mark = ","), caption = paste(
  "STAM candidate genes (B73 v5): the CLOSEST marker vs the highest -log10P marker",
  "within +/-500 kb (authentic 118K GWAS scan, 118,514 markers). Δstart = marker bp -",
  "gene start (v5), signed (negative = upstream); the two markers differ at every gene."
))

JLM QTL genomic distribution (Fig 4A)
The STAM QTL detected by JLM as a lollipop plot across all 10 chromosomes (height = −log10 P from TASSEL’s stepwise model), candidate genes bold-italic above the corresponding QTL. Reproduces Chen 2019 Fig 4A.


Show code

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
text:
Authentic 118K panel — OLS scan (Fig 4C)
Chen 2019’s imputed 118,838-SNP GWAS panel, scanned here with OLS (STAM ~ Family + marker, 1-df F) — not Chen’s Fig-4C model. Chen used a mixed model (Q = 5 PCs + K = kinship); without that structure/kinship correction this OLS scan runs inflated (hotter, broader peaks), so it does not reproduce Fig 4C. Chen’s Fig-4C model — the same 118K panel run through the MLM (Q+K) — is in teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.


Show code
p_unimp <- plot_stam_manhattan(
  here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_118k.csv"),
  "STAM — GWAS OLS (Family + marker), authentic 118K panel, candidate genes at v5 positions",
  here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_manhattan_annotated_118k.png"),
  lod = teonam_gwas_thr("STAM", "ols") # 1000-permutation genome-wide FWER (5%)
)
p_unimp

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
text:
Ancestry-imputed panel (FSFHap block-smoothed) — OLS scan
The same OLS model (STAM ~ Family + marker), but on the ancestry-imputed 118K truth: the panel block-smoothed with TASSEL5 FSFHap (Chen’s params; parental A/C output, ~9 breakpoints/chr) into a clean W22↔︎teosinte mosaic, then scanned. Because ancestry is piecewise-constant, contiguous markers share a call → identical p-values → block-terraced profile (peaks still correctly located). Contrast with the per-SNP raw panel above: same peaks, but the scatter collapses to ancestry blocks. This is the n=∞ complete-truth reference the coverage sweeps degrade from.


Show code
p_interp <- plot_stam_manhattan(
  here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_118k_complete_baseline.csv"),
  "STAM — GWAS OLS (Family + marker), ancestry-imputed (FSFHap) 118K panel",
  here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_manhattan_118k_fsfhap.png")
)
p_interp

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
section: RTIGER ancestry vs sequencing coverage (degradation sweep)
text:
RTIGER ancestry vs sequencing coverage (degradation sweep)
How low-coverage sequencing degrades the STAM signal through the RTIGER ancestry caller: for each family × coverage λ ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, ∞}× we Poisson-sample reads from the authentic 118K per-SNP genotypes (.draw_counts, pi_floor=0, k_decay=1, error=0.01), infer ancestry with call_ancestry(caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 2) (calibrated rigidity_star) on the 0.1 cM inference grid, back-project the segments onto the full 118,514-marker union, and run the STAM ~ Family + marker scan. λ = ∞ is RTIGER at perfect coverage (its own ceiling). Compute: scripts/teonam_rtiger_sweep_118k.R --generate; this chunk loads and plots.
Line-Manhattan (geom_line, one line per coverage; chromosomes never connect via group = interaction(CHR, coverage)): coverage on a viridis ramp, λ = ∞ in black, LOD = 5 dotted. RTIGER holds the tb1 / chr3 signal well down to low coverage, but on the real per-SNP truth the peak height is not monotone in coverage — RTIGER’s fixed rigidity smooths differently as read depth changes, so moderate coverage can look cleaner than perfect coverage. Read where signal survives, not exact peak heights; one replicate per cell.


Show code
p_sweep <- plot_sweep_line(
  here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_rtiger_118k_sweep.csv"),
  "STAM — RTIGER ancestry, GWAS −log10P vs coverage",
  base_size = 15
)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_rtiger_118k_sweep_manhattan.png"),
  p_sweep,
  width = 9, height = 4.6, dpi = 200, bg = "white"
)
p_sweep

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
section: Coverage-degradation composite: interpolation control / nNIL / RTIGER / LB-Impute
text:
Coverage-degradation composite: interpolation control / nNIL / RTIGER / LB-Impute
The four-panel contrast (agent/teonam-control-sweep-plan.md). Reads are Poisson- sampled per SNP from the authentic 118K per-SNP genotypes (teonam_gwas118k_dosage_polar.rds) — the real genotypes, not an ancestry-smoothed mosaic — over λ ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, ∞}×. Each caller infers ancestry on the cached 0.1 cM grid (markers_v5_gwas118k_cm_thin01.tsv; the exact distance-d independent set, teonam_gwas118k_thin01.R) and its segments are back-projected onto the full 118K union for the same STAM ~ Family + marker GWAS — only the caller differs. λ = ∞ is that caller at perfect coverage (deterministic decisive counts) — its own ceiling, computed in-pipeline, so each panel’s ∞ line is caller-specific (no shared baseline).

A — interpolation control (nilHMM::call_gt(prior = "hwe") + per-RIL step-interpolation; scripts/teonam_control_sweep_118k.R): the deliberately het-prone no-HMM caller — a single ALT read is called HET via the 2p(1−p) HWE prior term.
B — nNIL (call_ancestry(caller = "nnil", rrate = rrate_star, err = 0.01), f_1 = 0.08, f_2 = 0.15; scripts/teonam_nnil_sweep_118k.R): count emission + geometric duration.
C — RTIGER (caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 2; scripts/teonam_rtiger_sweep_118k.R): count emission + rigidity duration.
D — LB-Impute (call_ancestry(caller = "lbimpute", unit = "cm", recombdist = recombdist_star ≈ 5.5); scripts/teonam_lbimpute_sweep_118k.R): coverage-aware emission + distance-dependent (map-aware cM) transition with a double-recombination penalty, full-chromosome Viterbi (nilHMM native port of Fragoso et al. 2014). recombdist is the donor-fragment-Dice-optimal recombdist_star from the BC1S4 calibration (scripts/02_calibrate.R), read from calib_params.csv — not hardcoded.

The panels differ in het handling (het-call fraction table below): the no-HMM control (A) opens het at a lone ALT read via its per-site HWE prior, while the duration/distance HMMs (B–D) tie neighbouring markers into blocks. Because the truth is now the real per-SNP panel (not an ancestry mosaic), each caller’s smoothing interacts with genuine per-SNP het, so the tb1 peak height is not a clean monotone function of coverage — it reflects how each caller trades noise-smoothing against faithfully honouring the data (e.g. at high depth a caller is forced to honour scattered het that at moderate depth it would smooth away), and is confounded accordingly. Read the panels for where signal survives, not exact peak heights. One replicate per (family, λ) cell — trends, not point values. geom_line, coloured by coverage, LOD = 5, chromosomes never connect (group = interaction(CHR, coverage)).


Show code
het_ctrl <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_control_het_fraction_118k.csv"))[, .(coverage, control = round(het_frac, 3))]
het_nnil <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_nnil_het_fraction_118k.csv"))[, .(coverage, nnil = round(het_frac, 3))]
het_tab <- merge(het_ctrl, het_nnil, by = "coverage")
het_tab[] # HET-call fraction vs coverage: control (het excess at low λ) vs nNIL (flat ~truth)

pA <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_control_118k_sweep.csv"),
  "STAM — interpolation control (GL + HWE)",
  legend = TRUE
)
pB <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_nnil_118k_sweep.csv"),
  "STAM — nNIL ancestry",
  legend = FALSE

pC <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_gwas_rtiger_118k_sweep.csv"),

  ncol = 1, align = "v", axis = "lr",

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
text:
Caller noisiness (band-limited spectral power)
How much spurious fine-scale structure each caller injects, as spectral power in a physically-defined noise band. Each Manhattan track is a 1-D signal −log10P(bp): real ancestry structure is long-wavelength (≥ introgression-fragment size), sub-resolution structure is not recoverable, so power in between is over-fragmentation. The band is (R, P10): R = the 0.1 cM inference-grid resolution (median adjacent-marker bp gap), P10 = the 10th-percentile resolvable teosinte introgression-tract length (from the FSFHap mosaic; the raw P10 is sub-resolution because ~a third of “tracts” are single-marker het blips, so it is taken over tracts ≥ R). Thinning that band to a max distance-d independent set is the same interval-graph argument used for the inference grid ([teonam_gwas118k_thin01.R]). Headline = FFT band-power fraction (Hann-tapered periodogram, per chromosome, uniform bp grid); corroborated by Haar-wavelet detail energy in the same octaves. Compute: scripts/teonam_sweep_noisiness.R.


Show code
bp <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/sweep_noisiness_band_118k.csv"))
knitr::kable(bp, caption = sprintf(
  "Noise band: resolution R = %d kb, P10 resolvable introgression = %d kb (median tract %.2f Mb).",
  bp$R_kb, bp$P10_kb, bp$median_frag_Mb
))

title: Per-coverage caller calibration on simulated TeoNAM truth
text:
What this is. The coverage-degradation GWAS sweep is only fair if each ancestry caller runs at its per-coverage optimal parameter. This note is the calibration that produces those parameters: for each caller and each coverage \(\lambda\) we simulate a known-truth NIL panel, degrade it to \(\lambda\) with the same read model the sweep uses, sweep the caller’s one segmentation parameter, and pick the value that minimizes the donor-fragment false-discovery rate.






NoteMethod (identical across callers)




Simulated ground truth — simulate_nil("BC1S4", n = 200, map = <118K v5 cM grid>, seed = 12345), with known crossovers. Truth is simulated, never a real family’s caller output (that would score a caller against another caller).
Degrade with the sweep’s read model — .draw_counts(state, λ, error = 0.01), same seeds — at \(\lambda \in \{0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, \infty\}\) (\(\infty\) = deterministic depth-100).
Sweep the caller’s single parameter and score each fit against truth.
Pick by minimum donor_frag_FDR (fewest spurious donor fragments), at min_reads = 1. Donor recall stays \(\approx 0.99\)–\(1.0\) throughout, so recall does not discriminate — FDR does.



The three callers differ only in the segmentation parameter and whether it is map-aware:









caller
parameter
type
map-aware?




RTIGER
rigidity
integer — minimum run length in markers
no (index-based)


nNIL
rrate
continuous — per-marker recombination rate (self-stay \(= 1-\)rrate)
no (one fixed transition every marker)


LB-Impute
recombdist
continuous — linkage-decay distance (cM)
yes (consumes the cM gaps)





Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(here)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})

COVS <- c("0.1", "0.2", "0.5", "1", "5", "10", "20", "Inf")
read_pick <- function(caller) {
  d <- fread(here(sprintf("results/sim/teonam/%s_calib_bycov.csv", caller)))
  setnames(d, tolower(names(d)))
  parm <- intersect(c("rigidity", "rrate", "recombdist"), names(d))
  data.table(
    caller = caller, coverage = as.character(d$coverage),
    param = parm, param_value = d[[parm]],
    fdr = d[["fdr"]], recall = d[["recall"]], dice = d[["dice"]]
  )
}
picks <- rbindlist(lapply(c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"), read_pick))
picks[, coverage := factor(coverage, levels = COVS)]
picks[, caller := factor(caller, levels = c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"))]

curves <- rbindlist(lapply(c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"), function(c) {
  d <- fread(here(sprintf("results/sim/teonam/%s_calib_bycov_sweep.csv", c)))
  d[, caller := c]
  d
}), fill = TRUE)
curves[, coverage := factor(as.character(coverage), levels = COVS)]
curves[, caller := factor(caller, levels = c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"))]



The chosen parameter by coverage
param*(λ), the value carried into the sweep at each coverage. Each caller’s parameter lives on a different scale, so the panels have independent \(y\)-axes.


Show code
knitr::kable(
  dcast(picks, coverage ~ caller, value.var = "param_value"),
  caption = "param*(λ): rigidity (RTIGER), rrate (nNIL), recombdist cM (LB-Impute)."
)



param*(λ): rigidity (RTIGER), rrate (nNIL), recombdist cM (LB-Impute).

nnil
lbimpute
