Show code
ggplot(picks, aes(coverage, param_value, group = caller, colour = caller)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~caller,
    scales = "free_y",
    labeller = as_labeller(c(
      rtiger = "RTIGER · rigidity (markers)",
      nnil = "nNIL · rrate",
      lbimpute = "LB-Impute · recombdist (cM)"
    ))
  ) +
  labs(x = "coverage λ (×)", y = "chosen parameter", colour = NULL) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










RTIGER rigidity rises with coverage (16 → 128): deeper coverage exposes more per-marker detail, so a longer minimum run is needed to keep segments stable.
LB-Impute recombdist is high at the lowest coverages (stiffer, longer segments compensate for sparse reads) and settles to \(\approx 8\)–25 cM once coverage is informative.
nNIL rrate does not trend — it bounces over an order of magnitude. That is not signal; see the flat FDR plateau below.



Why each pick — FDR against the parameter
The selection objective, donor_frag_FDR, as a function of the swept parameter, one line per coverage. The chosen parameter is the curve’s minimum.


Show code
# Mark each pick at its own recorded (param*, FDR*) — robust to golden-refined
# values that need not sit on the coarse swept grid.
mark <- picks[, .(caller, coverage, value = param_value, donor_frag_FDR = fdr)]

ggplot(curves, aes(value, donor_frag_FDR, colour = coverage, group = coverage)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(data = mark, size = 2.4, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~caller,
    ncol = 1, scales = "free_x",
    labeller = as_labeller(c(
      rtiger = "RTIGER · rigidity", nnil = "nNIL · rrate",
      lbimpute = "LB-Impute · recombdist (cM)"
    ))
  ) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "swept parameter (log scale)", y = "donor-fragment FDR",
    colour = "λ (×)",
    title = "Selection curves — chosen value (○) minimizes donor-fragment FDR"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)









nNIL is a flat low-rrate plateau. Across the whole low-rrate range the FDR barely moves (recall is already \(\approx 1\)), so the “minimum” is essentially noise — which is why the per-coverage rrate* bounces around above. The operational decision is therefore to run nNIL at a single fixed rrate = 3.3e-5 in the sweep rather than the per-coverage argmin; RTIGER and LB-Impute do carry their per-coverage values.


Recall is not the discriminating metric
Donor-marker recall is saturated (\(\approx 0.99\)–\(1.0\)) at every coverage and parameter, confirming that donor_frag_FDR — not recall — is what separates a good parameter from a bad one.


Show code
ggplot(picks, aes(coverage, recall, group = caller, colour = caller)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.98, 1)) +
  labs(x = "coverage λ (×)", y = "donor-marker recall at param*", colour = NULL) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")











Bottom line
The calibration gives each caller a coverage-matched parameter for the degradation sweep: RTIGER’s rigidity climbs 16 → 128 with depth, LB-Impute’s recombdist settles near 8–25 cM, and nNIL’s FDR plateau means a single fixed rrate is the honest choice. All three hold donor recall at \(\approx 1\); the tuning only trades off spurious donor fragments (FDR). Parameters are read from results/sim/teonam/{rtiger,nnil,lbimpute}_calib_bycov.csv; the full selection curves are in the _calib_bycov_sweep.csv companions.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
This note derives, and backs with the cohort/panel data, why the SNP50K genotype matrix looks the way it does — 97% REF / 0.00% ALT-hom / 70% missing among 73.4M cells (see snp50k-cohort-provenance.md) — and why that shape is not simply “coverage too low.” There are two distinct causes, and both are calculable:
The consequence (§3): per-site zygosity is not recoverable, the emission means must be fit, not assumed, and the useful unit is the segment, not the marker.
Notation: at a biallelic panel site the reference allele is the B73 allele (the sites are ascertained teosinte-vs-B73 on the B73 NAM5 reference, so B73 is 0 by construction). \(\varepsilon\) = per-read error/mismap rate (\(\approx 0.003\)–\(0.01\) from B73 controls). \(p\) = alternate-allele frequency at the site across the cohort.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
section: 1. The het-inflation is a prior effect, not a likelihood effect
text:
1. The het-inflation is a prior effect, not a likelihood effect

1.1 Single-read likelihood (necessary, not sufficient)
For a diploid site covered by a single read, the genotype likelihoods are
\[
L(\text{read}=\text{ref}) : \begin{cases}
P(\text{ref}\mid 0/0) = 1-\varepsilon\\
P(\text{ref}\mid 0/1) = \tfrac12\\
P(\text{ref}\mid 1/1) = \varepsilon
\end{cases}
\qquad
L(\text{read}=\text{alt}) : \begin{cases}
P(\text{alt}\mid 0/0) = \varepsilon\\
P(\text{alt}\mid 0/1) = \tfrac12\\
P(\text{alt}\mid 1/1) = 1-\varepsilon .
\end{cases}
\]
These numbers come from the diploid-averaging likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid G=\{A_1,A_2\}) = \tfrac12 P(\text{read}\mid A_1) + \tfrac12 P(\text{read}\mid A_2)\) with per-base \(P(b\mid A)=1-\varepsilon\) if \(b=A\) and \(\varepsilon\) (more precisely \(\varepsilon/3\)) otherwise. Here \(\varepsilon\) is a per-read error placeholder.

Which likelihood model? The structure above (het pinned at \(\tfrac12\); the mismatch/error term for the two homozygotes) is the GATK / “dragon” model, and at depth 1 it coincides with what samtools/bcftools (Li 2011) and ANGSD’s GATK-model compute — so the single-read conclusion here is caller-independent. The models do differ in general (ANGSD GL page): samtools/Li-2011 adds a between-read dependency/overdispersion term that only bites at depth \(\ge 2\); SOAPsnp uses empirical base-quality calibration matrices; callers differ in how \(\varepsilon\) is derived (raw vs recalibrated base qualities, mapping quality). None of that changes the depth-1 story: any diploid model averages the two alleles, so one read fixes the het likelihood at \(\approx\tfrac12\) and yields at most a \(\sim2{:}1\) likelihood ratio — the ceiling is a property of diploid averaging, not of a particular caller. The full per-caller comparison and the PL/GQ mechanics are in genotype-likelihoods-and-hmm.md.

By likelihood alone, an alt read favors \(1/1\). If genotype calling used the maximum-likelihood genotype we would therefore see homozygous-ALT calls. We see none (1,001 in 73.4M). So the likelihood is not what determines the call — and the specific posterior/prior step below (§1.2) is the bcftools call -mv model; GATK and ANGSD apply the prior at a different stage (again, see the companion doc).


1.2 The posterior carries an allele-frequency prior
bcftools call -mv (the multiallelic caller) reports the maximum a posteriori genotype. It places a Hardy–Weinberg prior at the site’s estimated alternate allele frequency \(p\) (with a mutation-rate hyper-prior, -P, default \(1.1\times10^{-3}\)):
\[
P(0/0)=(1-p)^2,\qquad P(0/1)=2p(1-p),\qquad P(1/1)=p^2 .
\]
The posterior is \(\propto L\cdot P\). For a single alt read:



genotype
posterior \(\propto\)




\(0/0\)
\(\varepsilon\cdot(1-p)^2\)


\(0/1\)
\(\tfrac12\cdot 2p(1-p) = p(1-p)\)


\(1/1\)
\((1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2\)





1.3 The decision boundaries
HET vs ALT-hom. \(0/1\) is called over \(1/1\) whenever
\[
p(1-p) > (1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\frac{P(0/1)}{P(1/1)} = \frac{1-p}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p}\approx\frac1p > 1
\;\Longleftrightarrow\; p < \frac{1}{2-\varepsilon}\approx \tfrac12 .
\]
So for any allele rarer than ~50% cohort-wide, a single alt read is called heterozygous, never homozygous-ALT — by a margin of \(\approx 1/p\).
HET vs REF. \(0/1\) beats \(0/0\) for an alt read when \(p(1-p) > \varepsilon(1-p)^2 \Leftrightarrow p > \varepsilon/(1+\varepsilon)\approx\varepsilon\).
Combining, the single-alt-read call as a function of \(p\) is
\[
\widehat{g} =
\begin{cases}
0/0 & p < \varepsilon &(\text{alt read absorbed as error})\\[2pt]
0/1 & \varepsilon < p \lesssim \tfrac12 &(\textbf{heterozygous})\\[2pt]
1/1 & p \gtrsim \tfrac12 .
\end{cases}
\]


1.4 Where the cohort actually sits
Alternate-allele frequency spectrum over all 50,995 sites:



statistic
value




mean alt AF (\(\bar p\))
0.0152


sites with \(p<0.01\)
41.8%


\(0.01\le p<0.05\)
56.7%


\(0.05\le p<0.1\)
1.5%


\(p\ge 0.1\)
0.0%



Every site sits in the middle branch: \(\varepsilon \approx 0.003 < p < 0.1 \ll \tfrac12\), with a mean margin \(1/\bar p \approx 66\) in favor of HET over ALT-hom. This is the 0.00% ALT-hom / het-only pattern, derived. It is a property of the prior on rare alleles, so it would persist at higher depth until \(p\) approached \(\tfrac12\) — it is not removed by adding reads.


1.5 Why the count caller escapes this
The consequence is specific to a caller whose prior is the cohort HWE frequency. The count / BetaBinomial emission used by the ancestry callers does not inherit it: its per-state prior is the breeding-design expectation (BC2S2/BC2S3 \(f_1,f_2\)), and its “genotype” is the ancestry state decoded by the HMM, not a per-site MAP under a rare-allele frequency prior. That is the concrete reason the hard GT path is dead while the AD-count path recovers the donor footprint.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
section: 2. Outbred-donor dilution — most “variant” sites are silent per cross
text:
2. Outbred-donor dilution — most “variant” sites are silent per cross
Even setting the caller aside, the signal itself is far weaker than the naive {REF=0, HET=0.5, HOM=1.0} alt-fraction model, because teosinte donors are outbred and heterozygous, not inbred lines.

2.1 Panel provenance
The reference allele model is the Grzybowski (2023) resequencing panel (##source="schnable2021"), filtered to biallelic SNPs with --min-af 0.05:minor and intersected to the BZea HQ sites. Not imputed. So panel sites are common teosinte-vs-B73 variants — segregating in the species, but not fixed in any one accession.


2.2 Transmission model
The recurrent-parent (B73) gamete is REF (dose 0) at every panel site. A donor individual has genotype \(\{0/0, 0/1, 1/1\}\) with per-site frequencies \(\{f_0, f_1, f_2\}\); the gamete it contributes to the cross carries the donor (ALT) allele with probability — the transmitted donor dose —
\[
d \;=\; 0\cdot f_0 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot f_1 \;+\; 1\cdot f_2 \;=\; f_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\, f_1 .
\]
Each term is (probability the gamete carries ALT given the donor genotype) \(\times\) (genotype frequency): a \(0/0\) donor never transmits ALT, a \(0/1\) donor transmits it half the time, a \(1/1\) donor always. So \(d\) is simply the donor’s ALT-allele frequency at panel sites. A panel site “distinguishes the donor gamete from B73” only when the gamete carries ALT, i.e. with probability \(d\); everything else looks like B73.


2.3 Panel data
Per-donor-species genotype composition at panel sites (217-sample teosinte panel; each row is the per-individual average):











donor species
f₀ (REF)
f₁ (HET)
f₂ (ALT-hom)
het|non-ref
d = f₂ + ½·f₁




parviglumis (Zv)

The heterozygosity is genuine (parviglumis per-sample het: median 19.4%, Q1–Q3 11.3–21.0%, so not an artifact of panel het-undercalling). Two facts dominate:

f₀ ≈ 60% — most panel sites are not segregating in a given donor (they are variants private to other teosinte lineages, kept by the union-style ascertainment); the donor gamete is REF there, indistinguishable from B73.
of the sites that are non-REF, ~40% (parvi/mex) are heterozygous → the gamete loses the donor allele half the time.

Net: d ≈ 0.28–0.35 — only about one panel site in three carries the donor allele in a transmitted gamete (fewer for the less-diverse donors). This is the quantified form of “~1/5 of variant sites are actually variant between the gametes.”


2.4 The emission signal is compressed, not at {0, ½, 1}
Expected per-bin alternate-read fraction by NIL ancestry state, folding in the dose \(d\) (het sites emit an alt read w.p. ½; ref/ref sites w.p. \(\varepsilon\)):
\[
\mathbb{E}[\text{alt frac}] \approx
\begin{cases}
\varepsilon & \text{REF/REF (no introgression)} &\approx 0\\[2pt]
\tfrac{d}{2} + (1-\tfrac{d}{2})\varepsilon \approx \dfrac{d}{2} & \textbf{donor HET} &\approx 0.14\text{–}0.17\\[2pt]
d & \text{donor HOM} &\approx 0.28\text{–}0.35 .
\end{cases}
\]
So the three ancestry states live at roughly \(\{0,\;0.15,\;0.30\}\) in alt-fraction space, not \(\{0,\;0.5,\;1.0\}\):

REF-vs-HET separation is \(\approx 0.15\) over an \(\approx 0\) floor — a low-SNR elevation above baseline, which is exactly why the binned Gaussian-emission HMM is the right shape but low-confidence;
HET-vs-HOM separation is \(\approx 0.15\) (\(0.15\) vs \(0.30\)) — barely resolvable, which is why the caller is het-biased and rarely finds donor-hom.

Note the label “donor HET” above is the state the caller reports most, not the one the design expects most: by the BC2S3 single-locus frequencies donor-hom is actually the more common introgression genotype (\(f_{BB}=0.109 > f_{AB}=0.031\)) — the caller under-reports it purely because it is undetectable at \(0.4\times\). See genotype-likelihoods-and-hmm.md §2.


2.5 Empirical validation — \(d\) from the real read fractions
We can estimate \(d\) directly from the cohort AD, independent of the panel, by conditioning on ancestry-segment state and reading off the mean alt-read fraction: REF segments \(\to \varepsilon\), HET \(\to d/2\), HOM \(\to d\) (agent/estimate_dose_from_reads.R, segments from RTIGER calls which resolve zygosity; alt-fraction pooled over all covered sites of all NILs in a taxon).

Three things the reads confirm:

The REF-background floor is \(\varepsilon \approx 0.004\)–\(0.007\), exactly the per-read error rate — the REF state emits at the noise floor, not 0 (matching the nilhifi anchoring rationale).
The read-based \(d\) reproduces the panel-genotype \(d\) for the four well-sampled donors: the interval \([\,2\times\text{HET},\ \text{HOM}\,]\) brackets \(d\), and both land at \(\approx 0.3\)–\(0.4\) against the panel’s \(0.28\)–\(0.35\) (Zl essentially exact: 0.329 vs 0.333). Two fully independent measurements — donor-panel genotype composition and NIL read fractions — agree that d ≈ 0.3, not ≈ 1. The reads run slightly high, consistent with

the specific donor accessions carrying more alt than the species average and
HET segments absorbing some true-HOM markers (see next point).

The HET/HOM split is not clean — as predicted. Under the model \(2\times\)HET should equal HOM; instead HOM \(>2\times\)HET (e.g. Zx 0.49 vs 0.37) because at \(0.4\times\) the caller thresholds the alt-fraction rather than resolving zygosity, so HET keeps the low tail and HOM the high tail. The estimator’s own ambiguity is the identifiability limit this document is about. Zh is the extreme case (HET alt-frac 0.34, HOM 0.89): huehuetenangensis is the most divergent donor, so \(d\) is genuinely highest, but the value is uncertain (panel \(n=1\), strong het/hom bleed).

Estimates: agent/dose_estimates_by_taxon.csv.


2.6 The site-selection ceiling
Restricting to sites where the donor is fixed-ALT (\(f_2\to1\), \(d\to1\)) would restore the clean \(\{0, \tfrac12, 1\}\) model. But “fixed in this donor” cannot be known without the donor accession’s own genotypes, which we do not have; the --min-af 0.05:minor species panel identifies common variants, not accession-fixed ones. This is a more basic reference-data gap than phasing/LD: without the donor genotype we cannot even label which markers are informative for a given cross. It is the fundamental reason low-coverage genotype-imputation results from human aDNA or inbred-founder PHGs do not transfer here.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
3. Consequences

Fit the emission means, don’t assume them. The state means are \(\approx\{0,\,d/2,\,d\}\) with a per-donor \(d\); a fixed \(\{ \varepsilon, \tfrac12,
1-\varepsilon\}\) emission is mis-specified. This is why RTIGER’s per-taxon fit_means outperforms the fixed-mean count emission on this data.
Prefer donor-enriched sites where possible. Selecting sites with high species-level \(f_2\) raises \(d\) toward 1 and widens state separation, up to the accession ceiling in §2.6.
Report honestly. The donor footprint (where introgression is) is recoverable; the het-vs-donor-hom split is marginal (\(0.15\) vs \(0.30\)); per-site zygosity is not recoverable and should not be claimed. For B1/anthocyanin QTL the mapping unit is the segment/dosage genotype, obtained by projecting the decoded ancestry state onto the markers within a segment — not per-marker calls.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
Reproduce
D=/Volumes/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq/50K/results/joint

# §1.4 cohort alt-allele frequency spectrum (justifies p small)
bcftools query -f '%AC\t%AN\n' "$D/cohort.vcf.gz" \
 | awk '$1!="."&&$2>0{p=$1/$2;n++;s+=p;if(p<.01)b++} END{print "mean AF",s/n,"  frac<0.01",b/n}'

# §2.3 per-species donor dose d = f2 + 0.5*f1  (ref_<species>.txt = panel sample lists)
bcftools view -S ref_Z__parviglumis.txt --force-samples "$D/bzea_50K_ref_panel.vcf.gz" \
 | bcftools query -f '[%GT\n]' | awk -F'[/|]' '
   {n++; if($1=="."||$2==".")next; if($1==0&&$2==0)r++; else if($1==$2)a++; else h++}
   END{c=r+h+a; printf "REF %.3f HET %.3f ALT %.3f  d=%.3f\n",r/c,h/c,a/c,(a+0.5*h)/c}'

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
References

Grzybowski MW, Mural RV, Xu G, Turkus J, Yang J, Schnable JC (2023). A common resequencing-based genetic marker data set for global maize diversity. The Plant Journal 113(6):1109–1121. doi:10.1111/tpj.16123. — the teosinte reference panel used to estimate the donor dose (§2).
Li H (2011). A statistical framework for SNP calling, mutation discovery, association mapping and population genetical parameter estimation from sequencing data. Bioinformatics 27(21):2987–2993. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btr509. — the samtools/bcftools caller and GL model behind the call -mv prior (§1).
Hui R, D’Atanasio E, Cassidy LM, Scheib CL, Kivisild T (2020). Evaluating genotype imputation pipeline for ultra-low coverage ancient genomes. Scientific Reports 10:18542. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-75387-w. — reference-panel (aDNA) imputation that does not transfer here (§2.6).
Jensen SE, Charles JR, Muleta K, et al. (2020). A sorghum practical haplotype graph facilitates genome-wide imputation and cost-effective genomic prediction. The Plant Genome 13(1):e20009. doi:10.1002/tpg2.20009. — inbred-founder PHG imputation that does not transfer here (§2.6).

Full caller / genotype-likelihood method-paper references (GATK HaplotypeCaller, ANGSD, Li–Stephens) are in the companion genotype_likelihoods_and_hmm.html.

Analysis 2026-07-02, cohort.vcf.gz / bzea_50K_ref_panel.vcf.gz (read-only mount). Companion to snp50k-cohort-provenance.md. Panel: Grzybowski et al. 2023, --min-af 0.05:minor, not imputed.

title: Simulation-calibrated ancestry calling: two-stage parameter search
text:
Thesis. Ancestry-caller segmentation parameters (rrate for nNIL, rigidity for RTIGER) can be calibrated from cheap Broman simcross simulations where the donor mosaic is known truth, rather than tuned by eye on real data.
Method — a two-stage search (R/calibrate.R, driven by scripts/02_calibrate.R):

Log sweep — score a coarse log-spaced grid against truth to bracket the optimum (sweep_calibrate → one shared nilHMM::caller_sweep fit + scoring).
Golden-ratio refine — golden-section search inside the bracket on the continuous log10(parameter) to localize it (golden_refine).

The objective is donor-fragment Dice (reciprocal-overlap of called vs true introgression blocks) — it penalizes both missed donor and the spurious 1-marker blocks that over-fragmentation produces, unlike marker recall which saturates.






NoteCompute is separated from this note



scripts/02_calibrate.R runs the sim, calibration, and benchmark, writing summary CSVs to results/sim/ (gitignored). This note only loads and plots them. Regenerate with Rscript scripts/02_calibrate.R --generate.




Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
SIM <- here::here("results/sim")
f <- function(x) file.path(SIM, x)
# Pinned BC2S2 params snapshot: the generic calib_params.csv gets overwritten by
# BC1S4 runs (character `design` row breaks this BC2S2 note), so read a dated copy.
CALIB <- "calib_params_bc2s2_20260706024342.csv"
have <- file.exists(f(CALIB))
if (have) {
  P <- fread(f(CALIB))
  par <- setNames(P$value, P$key)
  nnil_sw <- fread(f("nnil_rrate_sweep.csv"))
  rtig_sw <- fread(f("rtiger_rigidity_sweep.csv"))
  nnil_rf <- fread(f("nnil_rrate_refine.csv"))
  rtig_rf <- fread(f("rtiger_rigidity_refine.csv"))
  bench <- fread(f("benchmark.csv"))
  frag <- fread(f("frag_sizes.csv"))
  geno <- fread(f("geno_fractions.csv"))
}



1. The simulation and its truth (§2.4)
BC2S2, n = 1500, simcross on the maize consensus map (~1783 cM, Stahl interference m = 10 / p = 0), 50 000 markers, degraded to the skim regime (~0.43× / ~72% missing). Truth = the donor mosaic. The simulated genome composition should match the single-locus (Mendelian) expectation:


Show code
data.table(
  metric = c("REF", "HET", "ALT", "donor dosage"),
  simulated = round(par[c("REF_truth", "HET_truth", "ALT_truth", "dosage_truth")], 4),
  expected = round(par[c("REF_expect", "HET_expect", "ALT_expect", "dosage_expect")], 4)
)


         metric simulated expected
         <char>     <num>    <num>
1:          REF    0.8413   0.8438
2:          HET    0.0630   0.0625
3:          ALT    0.0958   0.0938
4: donor dosage    0.1272   0.1250




2. Two-stage calibration
For each caller the coarse log sweep (line) brackets the optimum, and the golden-ratio refine (points) homes in; the dashed line is the refined parameter carried to the benchmark.


Show code
calib_panel <- function(sweep, refine, star, xlab, xtrans) {
  ggplot(sweep, aes(value)) +
    geom_line(aes(y = donor_frag_dice, colour = "donor-fragment Dice")) +
    geom_line(aes(y = donor_frag_FDR, colour = "donor-fragment FDR")) +
    geom_point(data = refine, aes(value, objective, colour = "golden probes"), size = 1.6) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = star, linetype = "dashed") +
    xtrans +
    labs(x = xlab, y = "score", colour = NULL) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")
}
p_nnil <- calib_panel(nnil_sw, nnil_rf, par["rrate_star"], "nNIL rrate", scale_x_log10()) +
  ggtitle(sprintf("nNIL — refined rrate = %.2g", par["rrate_star"]))
p_rtig <- calib_panel(
  rtig_sw, rtig_rf, par["rigidity_star"], "RTIGER rigidity",
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log2")
) +
  ggtitle(sprintf("RTIGER — refined rigidity = %d", as.integer(par["rigidity_star"])))
patchwork_ok <- requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)
if (patchwork_ok) {
  print(patchwork::wrap_plots(p_nnil, p_rtig))
} else {
  print(p_nnil)
  print(p_rtig)
}











Show code
# The log-sweep curves (Dice peaks then over-fragments as FDR/breakpoints climb).
show <- function(s, pcol) {
  s[, .(
    value = signif(value, 3), donor_frag_dice = round(donor_frag_dice, 3),
    donor_frag_FDR = round(donor_frag_FDR, 3), donor_marker_recall = round(donor_marker_recall, 3),
    n_breakpoints, truth_bp, ks = round(ks_fragsize, 3)
  )]
}
cat("nNIL rrate log sweep:\n")

title: nilHMM sanity check paint: source × caller tracks, one engine, no Julia
text:
Context. This is the cross-track sanity check for the unified nilHMM engine, where every caller runs through one duration-aware 3-state (REF/HET/ALT) HMM with the emission as the only swappable piece. We paint the same vary-skim coverage-sweep NILs (plus a clean B73 control) and eyeball whether the independent tracks agree on where the donor blocks are, and whether the control stays REF. Memory: wideseq-chrpainting-code, generative_vs_discriminative_callers, cassini-googa-caller, method-naming-source-caller, skim-count-caller-calibration, rtiger-betabinomial-cost.
The six tracks are not six callers. Each track is a combination of a data source and a nilHMM caller method (naming convention <DataSource>-<Caller>, source first — memory method-naming-source-caller). There are only two data sources and four distinct callers; Skim and BRB each run three tracks and share the nNIL and RTIGER callers:

Data sources (2): Skim = low-coverage WGS; BrB = 3′ RNA-seq (BRB-seq).
Callers (4 distinct): binhmm (Gaussian on per-bin ALT_FREQ), nNIL (count / BetaBinomial HMM), RTIGER (Julia-free port), ATLAS (GOOGA-shadow gene-level GT).

The tracks are ordered first by data source, then by caller (all three Skim tracks, then all three BRB tracks):










#
Track
Data source
nilHMM caller
Input marker set




1
Skim-binhmm
Skim (low-cov WGS)
binhmm — 3-state Gaussian on per-bin ALT_FREQ, REF anchored at the floor
dense ~27.6 M-SNP set — per-1 Mb ALT_FREQ bin_genotypes from GATK CollectAllelicCounts at the full teosinte-informative wideseq positions (mount, bzeaseq/ancestry)


2
Skim-nNIL
Skim (low-cov WGS)
nNIL — count / BetaBinomial HMM (rrate=1e-4, err=0.01)
50K panel counts (thinned SNP50K)


3
Skim-rtiger
Skim (low-cov WGS)
RTIGER port (rigidity=5)
50K panel counts (thinned SNP50K)


4
BrB-nNIL
BrB (3′ RNA-seq)
nNIL — count HMM, fit_means (rrate=1e-8)
wideseq-thinned BRB counts


5
BrB-rtiger
BrB (3′ RNA-seq)
RTIGER port (rigidity=7)
wideseq-thinned BRB counts


6
BrB-atlas
BrB (3′ RNA-seq)
ATLAS — GOOGA-shadow gene-level GT (≥.95/≥.25, min 5 reads) → geometric-duration HMM
cassini pangene counts, per donor species



So the grid is 2 sources × 3 caller-slots = 6 tracks, drawing on 4 unique callers (binhmm is Skim-only, ATLAS is BRB-only, nNIL/RTIGER run on both).






NoteMarker-set provenance — the tracks run at different SNP densities



The three Skim tracks are not all on the same markers. Skim-binhmm runs on the full dense set of ~27.6 M teosinte-informative SNPs (2.0–4.0 M per chromosome; total 27,603,777), whereas Skim-nNIL / Skim-rtiger run on the much sparser ~52 K “SNP50K” set. These are two different marker sets with two different provenances:

The ~27.6 M panel (wideseq_ref) is a teosinte-informative SNP set — positions where teosinte (the donor) carries variation relative to B73 (the recurrent parent), so a read there is diagnostic of donor-vs-recurrent ancestry. That informativeness is the whole point of the panel; it is a reference/filter set, not sample genotypes. It is built from the Schnable et al. 2023 Zea pan-genome VCF by keeping the teosinte/Tripsacum + B73 samples and biallelic SNPs — the MAF ≥ 0.05 cutoff is merely the mechanism that selects the donor-informative (species-common) sites (bzeaseq/get_variants_from_schnable2023.sh). GATK CollectAllelicCounts tallies each skim/BRB sample’s reads at these positions — so the BRB count tracks (BrB-nNIL, BrB-rtiger) draw on the same panel; only Skim-binhmm keeps the full 27.6 M density.
The ~52 K SNP50K set is an intersection — not a genotyping array, and not merely a coarse thinning of the 27.6 M panel. Its unfiltered base is the BZea NIL BAMs → GATK joint genotyping (N. Tandukar) + Beagle imputation (196,120 SNPs); intersecting those genotyped sites with the 27.6 M teosinte-informative panel yields 51,991 SNPs (so the 50K is itself teosinte-informative, being a subset of the panel). The Skim-nNIL / Skim-rtiger counts are reads tallied at those ~52 K sites. Here the teosinte panel is the filter and the BZea genotyping is the base — full chain + paths in sawers-rellan-labs/BzeaSeq → docs/donor_id_jaccard.md (“SNP50K & marker-set provenance”).

The MAF ≥ 0.05 filter keeps donor-informative (species-common) sites, which raises the donor dose and widens REF/HET/ALT separation — see genotype_likelihoods_and_hmm §5 and snp50k_genotype_identifiability §2.1/§2.6/§3.



Parameters are illustrative, not fully re-calibrated. This note is a coherence check across sources and callers, not a final call set.



1. Setup — engine, paths, samples


Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(nilHMM)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
# Inputs are staged locally under data/ by stage_sanity_check_paint.R (see DATA.md),
# organized by (source x input type); paths below point into that layout.
SS <- here::here("data/skimsweep") # the vary-skim cohort
SKIM <- file.path(SS, "skim") # skim counts (counts_50k/) + bins (bins/)
BRB <- file.path(SS, "brb") # brb counts (counts_wideseq/) + pangene (pangene/)
REF <- here::here("data/ref") # genome-level ATLAS gene maps

# samples: control + 10 vary-skim NILs (ordered by skim lambda)
mem <- fread(file.path(SS, "coverage_sweep_members.csv"))[sweep == "vary_skim"][order(vary_cov)]
mem[, tax := sub("\\..*", "", nil)]
tax2dir <- c(
  Zx = "mexicana", Zl = "luxurians", Zd = "diploperennis",
  Zv = "parviglumis", Zh = "huehuetenangensis"
)
mem[, adir := tax2dir[tax]]
# wrapped left-hand label: cov + FULL pedigree string, then skim/brb plate ids
wraplab <- function(cov, ped, skim, brb) sprintf("%s\n%s\nskim:%s\nbrb:%s", cov, ped, skim, brb)
rows <- rbind(
  data.table(
    rowlab = wraplab("B73 ctrl", "B73 (control)", "PN10_SID893", "PN3_SID213"),
    skim = "PN10_SID893", brb = "PN3_SID213", adir = "mexicana"
  ),
  mem[, .(
    rowlab = wraplab(sprintf("%.2f×", vary_cov), nil, skim_name, brb_name),
    skim = skim_name, brb = brb_name, adir
  )]
)




2. ATLAS: competitive-mapping ancestry (GOOGA-shadow)
ATLAS = Ancestry by Transcript-Level Alignment Scores.
The basic principle is competitive mapping: count the reads that match each founder genome. Where the SNP-panel callers read an allele fraction at predefined variant sites, ATLAS instead aligns each transcript read against both founder references at once — B73 (the recurrent parent) and the teosinte donor transcriptome — and lets the alignment scores decide which parent that read came from. Per read, the best alignment score (with a margin k on the score gap ΔAS) votes the read as REF, DONOR, or AMBIGUOUS. A read that maps equally well to both parents landed on sequence that is identical between them (conserved — at ~2 % B73-vs-donor divergence this is ~98 % of positions), so it carries no ancestry information: AMBIGUOUS = non-informative → dropped (it is not a HET call). Summing the informative votes over a gene gives the two competitive-mapping counts, n_recur (B73-matching reads) and n_donor (donor-matching reads); ancestry is then just “which founder’s genome captured the reads at this gene.”
Caveat — the “donor” reference is a species-level stand-in, not the actual donor accession. We do not have the genome/transcriptome of the specific teosinte accession used in each cross, so the donor side of the competitive alignment is a representative reference for the donor’s species (per tax2dir: mexicana, luxurians, diploperennis, parviglumis, huehuetenangensis). A read is scored donor-vs-B73 against that proxy, not against the true parent — the same reference-data gap as the SNP-panel accession ceiling (snp50k_genotype_identifiability §2.6): sequence the accession carries but the species proxy lacks (or vice versa) is mis-scored, so n_donor is only as good as the stand-in’s match to the real donor.
GOOGA is the template. GOOGA (Fishman lab, PLoS Genet 2024, pgen.1011072) is a Mimulus F2 genetic-map-building pipeline whose ancestry step is threshold-then-HMM. ATLAS is a “GOOGA-shadow”: it ports that step onto the shared nilHMM engine —

Per-gene hard call from the count fractions (GOOGA s2.py, verbatim): n_recur + n_donor < 5 → missing; recurrent fraction ≥ 0.95 → REF (AA); donor fraction ≥ 0.95 → ALT / donor-hom (BB); both parents ≥ 0.25 → HET (AB); otherwise missing. (Thresholds 0.95 / 0.25, min 5 reads.)
Place each gene at its B73 coordinate and order it along the chromosome.
Smooth with the engine’s categorical genotype-error (gt) emission + geometric-duration HMM — the same duration/rigidity layer as every other caller, but a categorical confusion emission instead of count/BetaBinomial.

Why categorical and not a count model: these are 3′ RNA (BRB-seq) reads, so a gene’s read depth is driven by expression, not coverage, and allele-specific expression pushes a true HET’s donor fraction off 0.5 — both confound a count/BetaBinomial emission. Discretizing to a hard genotype first (exactly what GOOGA, itself an eQTL/ASE pipeline, does) makes the emission expression-agnostic. Memory: cassini-googa-caller, feedback-reproduce-from-source.
The code below is the consumer-side staging: read the cassini pangene counts per donor species, apply the GOOGA hard call, map genes to B73 coordinates, and decode each chromosome through the gt-emission geometric-duration HMM.


Show code
g2p <- fread(file.path(REF, "gene_to_pangene.tsv"))[species == "B73", .(gene, pan_gene)]
co <- fread(file.path(REF, "b73_gene_coords.tsv"),
  header = FALSE,
  col.names = c("gene", "chr", "start", "end")
)
gmap <- merge(g2p, co, by = "gene")
gmap[, chr := as.integer(sub("chr", "", chr))]
gmap <- unique(gmap[!is.na(chr)], by = "pan_gene")[, .(pan_gene, chr, pos = end)]
# The GOOGA hard-call (`≥.95`/`≥.25`, min 5 reads) + gt/geometric-duration HMM
# are the packaged ATLAS caller (`call_ancestry(caller = "atlas")`). Here we do
# only the consumer-side staging — read pangene counts, map to B73 gene
# coordinates — and hand the engine `(name, chr, pos, n_ref = recurrent,
# n_alt = donor)`. `rrate = 1e-3` matches the geometric duration used originally.
atlas_seg <- function(brb, adir) {
  f <- file.path(BRB, "pangene", adir, paste0(brb, ".pangene_counts.tsv"))
  if (!file.exists(f)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  ct <- merge(fread(f), gmap, by = "pan_gene")[order(chr, pos)]
  seg <- as.data.table(call_ancestry(
    ct[, .(name = brb, chr, pos, n_ref = n_recur, n_alt = n_donor)],
    caller = "atlas", design = "BC2S3", rrate = 1e-3
  ))
  seg[, .(chr, start_bp, end_bp, state)]
}




3. binhmm (corrected) on the dense skim bins
3-state Gaussian-emission HMM on per-bin ALT_FREQ, porting the nilhifi chromosome_painting.R fix: REF is anchored at the baseline floor (mean fixed, sd from the low bins) so REF noise stays REF; HET/ALT are seeded from the data and refined by hard-EM with REF fixed; sticky transitions + design start priors + collapse guards. This replaces the free K=3 GMM (which always invents a 3rd cluster → HET over-call).


Show code
# The anchored 3-state Gaussian HMM is the packaged `binhmm` gauss backend. Our
# input is the pre-computed per-1 Mb `bin_genotypes.tsv` (ALT_FREQ over the dense
# ~27.6 M set); `call_ancestry(caller = "binhmm")` accepts a pre-binned table (an
# `alt_freq` column + `start_bp`/`end_bp`, `pos` as the bin order key) and runs
# the HMM directly, with no re-binning.
binhmm_gauss <- function(skim, design = "BC2S2") {
  f <- file.path(SKIM, "bins", paste0(skim, "_bin_genotypes.tsv"))
  if (!file.exists(f)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  d <- fread(f)
  obs <- data.table(
    name = skim, chr = as.integer(sub("chr", "", d$CONTIG)), pos = d$BIN_POS,
    alt_freq = d$ALT_FREQ, start_bp = d$BIN_START, end_bp = d$BIN_END
  )
  seg <- as.data.table(call_ancestry(obs, caller = "binhmm", design = design))
  seg[, .(chr, start_bp, end_bp, state)]
}




4. Count / RTIGER via the engine
nNIL and RTIGER are the same engine, different duration. Both are count-emission callers: they read per-SNP allele counts and score each marker with the count / BetaBinomial emission through the one nilHMM engine. The only thing that separates them is the duration layer — nNIL uses a geometric duration (a free per-marker switch probability), RTIGER a rigidity layer (an integer minimum run length). So both are just call_ancestry(read_counts(path), caller = …) with different arguments; there is no separate RTIGER program and no Julia.
The duration knob has a distinct name per caller (no overloaded r): caller = "nnil" takes rrate, the expected per-marker recombination rate of the geometric duration (Holland’s nNIL calibrates it as 2·total_cM / (100·n_markers) ≈ 30/n_markers for a 1500 cM maize map, and it is tunable from data by KS-vs-sim); caller = "rtiger" takes rigidity, the integer minimum run length (e.g. 5). (nilHMM’s rigidity duration also has an exit rate xrate, but the faithful RTIGER port doesn’t use it.) The four count tracks below (Skim-nNIL, Skim-RTIGER, BrB-nNIL, BrB-RTIGER) differ only in caller, design prior, and these knobs — assembled in §5.
eng() is the thin adapter that wires a count file into the engine: locate and read the counts, decode through call_ancestry(), and hand back just the four columns the paint needs.


Show code
# Locate a sample's count TSV under `dir`. Recursive because NILs and the B73
# control may sit in different subfolders; returns NA if the sample isn't staged.
find1 <- function(dir, s) {
  f <- list.files(dir, paste0("^", s, "\\.tsv$"), full.names = TRUE, recursive = TRUE)
  if (length(f)) f[1] else NA
}
# Read a count file and decode it through the shared engine with the caller +
# params in `...` (caller, design, rrate (nnil) / rigidity (rtiger), err, …).
# Keep only the paint's 4 columns; return NULL — skipped, not fatal — if the
# file is missing, so one absent sample never aborts the sweep.
eng <- function(path, ...) {
  if (is.na(path) || !file.exists(path)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  seg <- as.data.table(call_ancestry(read_counts(path), ...))
  seg[, .(chr, start_bp, end_bp, state)]
}
SK50 <- file.path(SKIM, "counts_50k") # skim 50K-panel counts   (Skim-nNIL, Skim-RTIGER)
BRBW <- file.path(BRB, "counts_wideseq") # BRB wideseq-thinned counts (BrB-nNIL, BrB-RTIGER)




5. Assemble all tracks


Show code
methods <- c(
  "Skim-binhmm", "Skim-nNIL", "Skim-rtiger",
  "BrB-nNIL", "BrB-rtiger", "BrB-atlas"
)

# run a caller, returning NULL (with a message) instead of aborting on error
grab <- function(fn) {
  tryCatch(fn, error = function(e) {
    message("  ! ", conditionMessage(e))
    NULL
  })
}

all <- list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(rows))) {
  rl <- rows$rowlab[i]
  sk <- rows$skim[i]
  br <- rows$brb[i]
  ad <- rows$adir[i]

  sk_counts <- find1(SK50, sk) # skim 50K panel counts
  brb_counts <- file.path(BRBW, paste0(br, ".tsv")) # wideseq-thinned BRB counts

  tr <- list(
    `Skim-binhmm` = grab(binhmm_gauss(sk)),
    `Skim-nNIL` = grab(eng(sk_counts,
      caller = "nnil", design = "BC2S2", rrate = 1e-4, err = 0.01
    )),
    `Skim-rtiger` = grab(eng(sk_counts, caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 5L)),
    `BrB-nNIL` = grab(eng(brb_counts,
      caller = "nnil", design = "BC2S3", rrate = 1e-8, fit_means = TRUE
    )),
    `BrB-rtiger` = grab(eng(brb_counts, caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 7L)),
    `BrB-atlas` = grab(atlas_seg(br, ad))
  )

  # keep the non-empty tracks, tagging each with its row label and method
  for (m in names(tr)) {
    if (!is.null(tr[[m]]) && nrow(tr[[m]])) {
      all[[length(all) + 1L]] <- tr[[m]][, `:=`(rowlab = rl, method = m)]
    }
  }
}
df <- rbindlist(all)
df[, .N, by = method]


        method     N
        <char> <int>
1: Skim-binhmm   231
2:   Skim-nNIL   290
3: Skim-rtiger   265
4:    BrB-nNIL   223
5:  BrB-rtiger   329
6:   BrB-atlas   290




6. Paint


Show code
yb <- data.table(method = methods, k = 5:0) # top -> bottom
df <- merge(df, yb, by = "method")
df[, `:=`(ylo = k / 6 + 0.01, yhi = (k + 1) / 6 - 0.01)]
# B73 ctrl on top, then skim-cov descending (facet switch = 'y')
df[, rowlab := factor(rowlab, levels = c(rows$rowlab[1], rev(rows$rowlab[-1])))]
df[, chr := factor(chr, levels = 1:10, labels = paste0("chr", 1:10))]
df[, state := factor(state, 0:2, c("REF", "HET", "ALT"))]
pal <- c(REF = "gold", HET = "springgreen4", ALT = "purple4")
p <- ggplot(df, aes(xmin = start_bp / 1e6, xmax = end_bp / 1e6, ymin = ylo, ymax = yhi, fill = state)) +
  geom_rect() +
  facet_grid(rowlab ~ chr, scales = "free_x", space = "free_x", switch = "y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = (5:0) / 6 + 1 / 12, labels = methods, position = "right",
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "nilHMM sanity check paint — vary-skim sweep (B73 control + 10 NILs)",
    subtitle = "6 tracks = data source × nilHMM caller (2 sources: Skim, BrB | 4 callers: binhmm, nNIL, RTIGER, ATLAS) | ordered by source then caller | all nilHMM, no Julia",
    x = "Position (Mb)", fill = "Genotype"
  ) +
  ggpubr::theme_classic2(base_size = 8) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 12),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(0.04, "lines"),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(0.15, "lines"),
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    strip.text.y.left = element_text(
      angle = 0, hjust = 1, size = 12, lineheight = 0.9
    ),
    axis.text.y.right = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
  )
dir.create(here::here("results"), showWarnings = FALSE)
ggsave(here::here("results/nilhmm_sanity_check_paint.png"), p, width = 16, height = 11, dpi = 130, limitsize = FALSE)
fwrite(df, here::here("results/nilhmm_sanity_check_paint_segments.tsv"), sep = "\t")
p











7. Sanity read

The anchor holds: the B73 control (top row) is essentially all-REF across all six tracks — a clean control paints clean.
NILs show localized donor blocks that broadly agree across callers. The major introgressions land in the same chromosomes across independent callers and across the two data sources — the core sanity result.
Caller-specific behavior, as expected: Skim-binhmm paints the broadest blocks and is more permissive toward ALT; BrB-rtiger is the noisiest (the known BRB-RTI RNA false-donor floor); BrB-atlas and BrB-nNIL are the most conservative.

title: BAM-alignment genome coverage and missing data in the BZea population
text:
Purpose. Extend the Poisson baseline from missing-data-coverage-theory.qmd into a model that actually fits the BZea data. We analyze BAM alignments directly — before any genotype calling or VCF filtering — and show that per-sample missingness follows an exponential-with-floor law rather than the pure Poisson expectation.
Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(here)
  library(readr)
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(ggpubr)
  library(cowplot)
  library(minpack.lm) # nlsLM: robust Levenberg-Marquardt nonlinear least squares
  library(viridis)
})

title: BAM-alignment genome coverage and missing data in the BZea population
text:
1.1 Theoretical background
Modeling missingness in sequencing data involves both stochastic sampling effects and systematic biases. The foundational concept is the Poisson coverage model, where the probability of observing zero reads at a site decreases exponentially with increasing coverage — this reflects the stochastic nature of read sampling.
Additionally, certain genomic regions inherently exhibit higher missingness due to low-complexity/repetitive sequences or structural variation. This leads to an irreducible baseline missingness that persists even at high coverage with short reads. These two facts are the basis for the exponential-with-floor model: a parsimonious and mechanistically interpretable summary of missingness across samples.

title: BAM-alignment genome coverage and missing data in the BZea population
text:
1.2 Models of missingness

1.2.1 Poisson model
If reads are randomly and independently distributed across the genome, the probability of missing a site is
\[P_{\text{missing}} = e^{-\lambda}\]
where \(\lambda\) is the mean coverage (average reads per variant site) and \(P_{\text{missing}}\) is the probability of no reads covering a site. This serves as a baseline to evaluate whether missingness arises purely from random sequencing variation or whether additional biases (e.g. mapping issues) contribute.


1.2.2 Exponential-with-floor model
We model per-sample missingness as
\[P_{\text{missing}} = \pi + (1 - \pi)\, e^{-k \lambda}\]
where:

\(\lambda\) is the mean coverage.
\(\pi\) (floor parameter) is the fraction of sites that remain uncalled even at high coverage with short reads. This baseline missingness likely corresponds to repetitive DNA, structural variants, or highly polymorphic regions where short reads fail to map or are discarded by mapping filters. For example, picard CollectWgsMetrics reports a PCT_EXC_TOTAL statistic that can be interpreted as a floor parameter — for the alux project, at nominal coverage \(3.2\times\) I obtained a mean \(\text{PCT\_EXC\_TOTAL} = 0.45 \sim \pi\).
\(k\) (decay rate) is how fast missingness decays with increasing coverage relative to the Poisson model. I interpret the product \(k\lambda\) as an effective coverage (perhaps the coverage of the informative variants).

title: BAM-alignment genome coverage and missing data in the BZea population
text:
4.1 Simple exponential model
A first pass: regress log-missingness on coverage to get a rough decay rate.


Show code
fit_linear <- lm(log(missing_obs) ~ lambda, data = per_sample)
per_sample$missing_exp_k <- exp(predict(fit_linear))

k_est <- -coef(fit_linear)[2]
cat("Estimated k:", round(k_est, 3), "\n")


Estimated k: 0.581 


Show code
cat(
  "Effective coverage (k*lambda) range:",
  paste0(round(k_est * range(per_sample$lambda), 3), collapse = " - "), "\n"
)


Effective coverage (k*lambda) range: 0 - 0.846

title: BAM-alignment genome coverage and missing data in the BZea population
text:
4.2 Exponential-with-floor model
Now the full model, fit by robust nonlinear least squares (nlsLM), using the linear-model estimates as starting values.


Show code
# Restrict to samples with well-defined missingness in (0, 1)
dat <- per_sample %>%
  filter(
    is.finite(lambda), is.finite(missing_obs),
    missing_obs > 0, missing_obs < 1
  )

# Initial estimate of the floor from a linear fit of observed vs Poisson
fit_missing <- lm(missing_obs ~ missing_poisson, data = per_sample)
pi_est <- coef(fit_missing)[1]

fit_exp_floor <- nlsLM(
  missing_obs ~ pi + (1 - pi) * exp(-k * lambda),
  data  = dat,
  start = list(pi = pi_est, k = k_est),
  lower = c(0, 0),
  upper = c(1, 10)
)

model_params <- coef(fit_exp_floor)
pi <- model_params[1]
k <- model_params[2]

cat("Floor parameter (pi):", round(pi, 3), "\n")


Floor parameter (pi): 0.346 


Show code
cat("Decay rate (k):", round(k, 3), "\n")


Decay rate (k): 1.256 


Show code
per_sample$missing_exp_floor <- predict(fit_exp_floor, newdata = per_sample)
overall_stats$missing_exp_floor <- predict(
  fit_exp_floor,
  newdata = data.frame(lambda = overall_stats$lambda)
)

title: BAM-alignment genome coverage and missing data in the BZea population
text:
6.1 Poisson model assessment


Show code
p_scatter_poisson <- per_sample %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = missing_poisson, y = missing_obs, col = lambda)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red") +
  scale_color_viridis_c(
    breaks = legend_breaks, labels = legend_labels,
    limits = c(0, 1.5)
  ) +
  guides(col = guide_colorbar(title = "Coverage")) +
  geom_point(
    x = overall_stats$missing_poisson, y = overall_stats$missing_obs,
    color = "red"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = xy_limits, breaks = (2:10) / 10) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = xy_limits, breaks = (2:10) / 10) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1) +

  ylab("Observed Missing") +
  ggpubr::theme_classic2(base_size = 16) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

inlay_grob <- cowplot::as_grob(p_hist)

final_plot_poisson <- cowplot::ggdraw() +
  cowplot::draw_plot(p_scatter_poisson) +
  cowplot::draw_plot(inlay_grob, x = 0.55, y = 0.16, width = 0.2, height = 0.25)

print(final_plot_poisson)









The Poisson model systematically underpredicts missingness: observed points sit above the 1:1 line.

text:
6.2 Exponential-with-floor model assessment

p_scatter_floor <- per_sample %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = missing_exp_floor, y = missing_obs, col = lambda)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pi, linetype = "dashed") +

The floor model places points on the 1:1 line; the dashed line marks the floor \(\pi\), the minimum missingness attainable no matter how high the coverage.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
text:
What this is. A self-contained, hard-proven record of the marker-thinning step that builds the JLM marker framework (data/teonam/tassel/geno.hmp.txt, 9063 markers). Every claim below is reproduced live from the primary data — no approximations, no “close enough.” The three things this notebook nails down:
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
suppressMessages(devtools::load_all("/Users/fvrodriguez/repos/nilhmm", quiet = TRUE))
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 12))
THR <- 0.1 # cM thinning threshold

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: The two published methods (Chen et al. 2019)
text:
The two published methods (Chen et al. 2019)
Chen 2019 (Genetics 213:1065–1078) used two separate marker sets for its two mapping methods. Quoting the Methods verbatim:

“Because stepwise regression is computationally intensive, we thinned SNPs within 0.1 cM. We finally obtained 4578 markers for subsequent joint linkage analysis.” — (JLM marker set)


“…a total of 118,838 SNPs were kept and used for GWAS. GWAS was performed using a linear mixed model accounting for population structure (Q) and kinship matrix (K)…” — (GWAS marker set, a different pipeline)

This notebook concerns the JLM set only (the 4578-marker analogue). The paper’s composite genetic map is 1540 cM; ours (native TeoNAM est.map cM) is ~1561 cM — so map length is essentially Chen’s, and the count difference is instead driven by how finely the native map resolves markers into 0.1-cM bins, as we show below.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
text:
The thinning as implemented here
The pipeline (scripts/teonam_reproduce_fastindep.R, and originally the lost inline prep this notebook now replaces) does:

take every marker in the native TeoNAM map (teonam_v5_native.tsv, native est.map cM);
dedup to unique cM per chromosome (the densify grid: !duplicated(cm));
run select_independent on the per-chromosome cM-distance matrix (|cm_i − cm_j|), with sense = "distance", threshold = 0.1, n_runs = 1 (deterministic greedy, documented bit-identical to the FastIndep CLI).

The subtlety: select_independent is an LD-pruning tool — normally you hand it a genotype matrix and it prunes on r². Here it was handed a distance matrix, so it prunes on genetic distance instead. That choice is what we verify and dissect.

# target: the markers actually in the JLM input on disk
target <- fread(here("data/teonam/tassel/geno.hmp.txt"), select = "rs#")[["rs#"]]

# candidate pool: native map (placed markers), unique-cM dedup per chromosome
mc <- fread(here("data/teonam/teonam_v5_native.tsv"))[!is.na(cm)]
setnames(mc, "chr_v5", "chr")
pool <- mc[order(chr, cm)][, .SD[!duplicated(cm)], by = chr]

# run the REAL FastIndep greedy on the cM-distance matrix, per chromosome
thin_distance <- function(m) { # m: data.table(marker, cm)
  m <- m[order(cm)]
  D <- abs(outer(m$cm, m$cm, `-`))
  dimnames(D) <- list(m$marker, m$marker)
  as.character(select_independent(D,
    threshold = THR, n_runs = 1L,
    sense = "distance", max_markers = nrow(D) + 1L
  ))
}
sel <- unlist(lapply(
  sort(unique(pool$chr)),
  function(ch) thin_distance(pool[chr == ch, .(marker, cm)])
))


Proof of exact reproduction

proof <- data.table(
  metric = c(
    "selected", "target (on disk)", "in both",
    "selected-only", "target-only", "Jaccard", "setequal"
  ),
  value = c(
    length(sel), length(target), length(intersect(sel, target)),
    length(setdiff(sel, target)), length(setdiff(target, sel)),
    round(length(intersect(sel, target)) / length(union(sel, target)), 4),
    as.integer(setequal(sel, target))
  )
)
knitr::kable(proof, caption = "FastIndep on the cM-distance pool vs the JLM file on disk")


FastIndep on the cM-distance pool vs the JLM file on disk


metric
value




selected
9063

9063

9063


selected-only

0

1

1




stopifnot(setequal(sel, target)) # the notebook fails to render if this is not exact

setequal = 1, Jaccard = 1.000: the thinning is reproduced bit-for-bit. This is the ground truth the rest of the notebook interprets. (For the record: running the same algorithm on the raw marker_info pool — without the unique-cM dedup — gives 6056 markers with 51 mismatches; the dedup step is load-bearing, and pinning that down is exactly why “99% overlap” is not “reproduced.”)

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: Diagnostic 1 — the retained set has a hard 0.1 cM floor
text:
Diagnostic 1 — the retained set has a hard 0.1 cM floor
If the thinning pruned on distance, no two retained markers can sit closer than 0.1 cM. If it had pruned on r², close markers in low LD would survive. The gap distribution decides it.

km <- merge(data.table(marker = target), mc[, .(marker, chr, cm)], by = "marker")[order(chr, cm)]
gaps <- km[, .(gap = diff(cm)), by = chr]$gap
gtab <- data.table(
  stat = c("min", "1%", "5%", "median", "% < 0.10 cM", "% < 0.05 cM"),
  cM = c(
    round(quantile(gaps, c(0, .01, .05, .5)), 4),
    round(100 * mean(gaps < 0.10), 2), round(100 * mean(gaps < 0.05), 2)
  )
)
knitr::kable(gtab, caption = "Adjacent cM-gap between retained JLM markers")


Adjacent cM-gap between retained JLM markers


stat
cM

0.1000


1%

0.0000


% < 0.05 cM
0.0000




ggplot(data.table(gap = gaps[gaps < 1]), aes(gap)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, fill = "grey30", boundary = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    x = "adjacent cM gap between retained markers", y = "count",
    title = "Hard floor at exactly 0.10 cM (red) — the signature of a distance thin",
    subtitle = "0% of neighbours fall below 0.10 cM; an r² prune could not produce this floor"
  )








The minimum gap is exactly 0.10 cM and nothing is below it — the unambiguous fingerprint of a cM-distance thin, not r².

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: Diagnostic 2 — distance ≠ r²: proven, not asserted
text:
Diagnostic 2 — distance ≠ r²: proven, not asserted
To show the consequence of feeding FastIndep cM distances instead of genotype r², we run both on the same chromosome-1 window and compare the sets they keep. The genotypes are densified live here from the raw family files by step-interpolation — the same operation as scripts/teonam_stam_gwas_scan.R::densify_chr — so this notebook depends on no pre-saved intermediate (.rds) of unknown provenance; only primary data (W22TIL*_genotype.csv + the native map).

# --- densify a chr1 window from the RAW family genotypes (step-interpolation) ---
fams <- c(
  TIL01 = "W22TIL01_genotype.csv", TIL03 = "W22TIL03_genotype.csv",
  TIL11 = "W22TIL11_genotype.csv", TIL14 = "W22TIL14_genotype.csv",
  TIL25 = "W22TIL25_genotype.csv"
)
cm_by <- setNames(mc$cm, mc$marker)
mch <- mc[chr == 1 & cm >= 20 & cm <= 45][order(cm)]
tgt <- mch[!duplicated(cm)] # unique-cM target grid (the window)
tgt_df <- data.frame(chr = 1, cm = tgt$cm)
win <- data.table(marker = tgt$marker, cm = tgt$cm)

densify_fam <- function(fam) {
  fp <- here("data/teonam", fams[fam])
  hdr <- names(fread(fp, nrows = 0))
  cols <- intersect(hdr, mch$marker) # this family's markers in the window
  g <- fread(fp, select = c(hdr[1], cols))
  g <- g[!duplicated(g[[1]])]
  keys <- paste0(fam, sub("^.*Line_", "", g[[1]])) # e.g. TIL01A001
  obs <- data.frame(marker = cols, cm = cm_by[cols])
  obs <- obs[order(obs$cm), ]
  obs <- obs[!duplicated(obs$cm), ]
  geno <- t(as.matrix(g[, obs$marker, with = FALSE]))
  storage.mode(geno) <- "double"
  dn <- interpolate_genotype(geno, data.frame(chr = 1, cm = obs$cm), tgt_df, mode = "step")
  colnames(dn) <- keys
  dn
}
Gw <- do.call(cbind, lapply(names(fams), densify_fam))
rownames(Gw) <- tgt$marker

# (a) distance thin — what the pipeline did
selD <- thin_distance(win[, .(marker, cm)])

# (b) r² thin — what FastIndep is designed for — swept across thresholds
gap_of <- function(ids) {
  k <- win[marker %in% ids][order(cm)]
  diff(k$cm)
}
r2_thin <- function(t) {
  as.character(
    select_independent(Gw,
      threshold = t, method = "r2",
      n_runs = 1L, max_markers = nrow(Gw) + 1L
    )
  )
}

rows <- list(data.table(
  thinning = "cM-distance (t=0.1)", n_kept = length(selD),
  min_gap_cM = round(min(gap_of(selD)), 4),
  median_gap_cM = round(median(gap_of(selD)), 3)
))
for (t in c(0.2, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99)) {
  s <- r2_thin(t)
  g <- gap_of(s)
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.table(
    thinning = sprintf("r² LD (t=%.2f)", t), n_kept = length(s),
    min_gap_cM = round(min(g), 4), median_gap_cM = round(median(g), 3)
  )
}
knitr::kable(rbindlist(rows),
  caption =
    "Chr1 window (866 markers over 24.3 cM): cM-distance vs r² thinning"
)

plt <- rbind(
  data.table(cm = win[marker %in% selD]$cm, method = "cM-distance (t=0.1)"),
  data.table(cm = win[marker %in% r2_thin(0.5)]$cm, method = "r² LD (t=0.50)"),
  data.table(cm = win[marker %in% r2_thin(0.99)]$cm, method = "r² LD (t=0.99)")
)
plt[, method := factor(method, levels = c("cM-distance (t=0.1)", "r² LD (t=0.50)", "r² LD (t=0.99)"))]
ggplot(plt, aes(cm, method, colour = method)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.55) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue4", "firebrick", "darkorange"), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "cM (chr 1)", y = NULL,
    title = "Same window, three thinnings — r² tracks LD (threshold-dominated); distance tracks spacing"
  )








The two are different operations, and the r² set is not even monotonic in spacing — it tracks LD, and TeoNAM ancestry LD is long-range. At r² ≥ 0.2 the entire 24 cM window collapses to ~2 markers (essentially one ancestry block); only at a very strict r² ≥ 0.99 does r² pruning become dense enough to admit markers < 0.1 cM apart (min gap 0.000 cM — pairs the distance thin forbids). The cM-distance thin (99 markers, hard 0.10 cM floor) sits in neither regime: it is indifferent to LD. That is the concrete consequence of feeding FastIndep a cM matrix — the JLM framework guarantees genetic spacing but does no LD decorrelation, so many retained neighbours remain in near-perfect LD (collinear, redundant columns for the stepwise model).

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: The count: 9063 vs 4578, decomposed to the marker
text:
The count: 9063 vs 4578, decomposed to the marker
Both we and Chen ran a 0.1 cM thin, so the algorithm is not the source of the difference. 9063 / 4578 ≈ 1.98 decomposes into map length and bin saturation (the fraction of 0.1-cM bins that contain ≥1 marker) — but the two factors are now far from equal: length is ~1.0 (native ≈ Chen), so bin saturation (~2.0) carries essentially the whole difference:

maplen_ours <- mc[, max(cm) - min(cm), by = chr][, sum(V1)]
maplen_paper <- 1540
n_ours <- length(target)
n_paper <- 4578
sat_ours <- n_ours / (maplen_ours / 0.1) # occupied 0.1-cM bins / total bins
sat_paper <- n_paper / (maplen_paper / 0.1)
decomp <- data.table(
  factor = c(
    "map length (cM)", "0.1-cM bins available", "markers kept",
    "bin saturation", "— length ratio", "— saturation ratio",
    "— product (predicted)", "— observed ratio"
  ),
  ours = c(
    round(maplen_ours), round(maplen_ours / 0.1), n_ours, round(sat_ours, 3),
    NA, NA, NA, NA
  ),
  paper = c(
    maplen_paper, round(maplen_paper / 0.1), n_paper, round(sat_paper, 3),
    NA, NA, NA, NA
  ),
  ratio = c(
    NA, NA, NA, NA,
    round(maplen_ours / maplen_paper, 3), round(sat_ours / sat_paper, 3),
    round((maplen_ours / maplen_paper) * (sat_ours / sat_paper), 3),
    round(n_ours / n_paper, 3)
  )
)
knitr::kable(decomp,
  caption =
    "Map length × bin saturation reproduces the marker-count ratio"
)

The predicted product matches the observed 9063/4578 ratio: with the native map at ~Chen’s length, the extra markers come almost entirely from bin saturation — the native est.map resolves ~2× the fraction of 0.1-cM bins that Chen’s map did — not from map length and not a thinning-method artifact.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
text:
Conclusions

The JLM marker framework was built by nilHMM::select_independent on a cM-distance matrix at 0.1 cM (unique-cM dedup pool). Reproduced exactly (Jaccard = 1.000); the render aborts if that ever stops holding.
Feeding FastIndep cM distances turns it into a 0.1 cM minimum-spacing ruler; it performs no LD pruning. Diagnostic 2 proves the distance and r² thinnings keep materially different marker sets.
The 9063 vs 4578 difference is a deterministic property of the native map’s higher bin saturation at essentially Chen’s map length (length × saturation), fully decomposed above — nothing unexplained.

Implication for the design. The r² path is the LD-aware option in principle, but Diagnostic 2 shows it is not a drop-in here: because ancestry LD is long-range, r² pruning is extremely threshold-sensitive (t = 0.5 → a handful of markers per chromosome; only t ≈ 0.99 keeps a workable dense set). The cM-distance thin is the pragmatic choice that matches Chen’s operation (a 0.1 cM thin), at the documented cost of retaining LD-redundant neighbours. Both are defensible; this notebook records which one is in force — a 0.1 cM genetic-distance ruler — so downstream QTL results are read with the right framework in mind.

title: Emission by depth regime: when read counts matter, and when a hard genotype is enough
text: Context. A unified ancestry-caller engine (RTIGER / nilHMM / Skim-BIN as one duration-aware HMM, see generative_vs_discriminative_callers) keeps 3 hidden genotype states (REF / HET / ALT) and a shared duration/rigidity layer, and treats the emission as a swappable module. This note pins down which emission to use when: the choice is driven almost entirely by read depth, with the BetaBinomial count model earning its keep only in an intermediate band. Below it the data is degenerate; above it the count model saturates into a hard genotype. Memory: rtiger-betabinomial-cost, nilhmm-counts-path, bzea-skim-bulked-bc2s2, molbreeding-45k-source5.

title: Emission by depth regime: when read counts matter, and when a hard genotype is enough
section: 2.1 Upper boundary — why ≥20× saturates to a hard call
text:
2.1 Upper boundary — why ≥20× saturates to a hard call
The BetaBinomial emission cost scales with the number of distinct \((n, k)\) count pairs, which scales with coverage — so RTIGER and the optimized nilHMM cap depth at ~20–30× (rtiger-betabinomial-cost). That cap is statistically free for exactly the reason it makes hard GT viable: at \(\geq 20\times\) the emission is so peaked that the genotype is already certain. The likelihood over \(\{\text{ref},\text{het},\text{alt}\}\) becomes a delta on one state → a hard call. So above the cap, counts and hard GT carry the same information, and hard GT is strictly cheaper (a \(3\times3\) error matrix vs. per-pair BetaBinomial). You are not approximating the count model with a hard genotype — at saturation the count model is a hard genotype.

title: Emission by depth regime: when read counts matter, and when a hard genotype is enough
section: 2.2 Lower boundary — why ≪1× is degenerate (and the emission can’t fix it)
text:
2.2 Lower boundary — why ≪1× is degenerate (and the emission can’t fix it)
At depth ~1, a single observed allele is consistent with both HET (\(\theta=0.5\)) and the matching homozygote. A lone alt read is actually more likely under donor-hom (\(\theta\approx1\)) than under HET (\(0.5\)), so HET gets misassigned to hom. This is the depth-1 HET/HOM degeneracy (bzea-skim-bulked-bc2s2, nilhmm-counts-path): it is a data limit, not an emission-model limit — no choice of emission recovers information the reads don’t contain.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Genome-wide association studies performed in experimental mapping populations differ fundamentally from GWAS performed in natural populations.
The allele distribution in a mapping population is not determined by Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium. Instead, it is determined by the breeding design, founder genotypes, segregation scheme, and recombination history.
Therefore, classical population-genetic approximations based on
\[
2p(1-p)
\]
are not generally appropriate.
The expected GWAS signal should instead be described in terms of the variance of the actual genetic predictor segregating in the mapping population.






NoteEmpirical motivation — two results in this repo



This framework was prompted by two observations in the TeoNAM STAM coverage-degradation notebook:

the interpolated-grid step collapses the GWAS significance dispersion onto an almost-single profile — an artifact of neighbour interpolation rather than a biological signal; and
the RTIGER coverage-degradation sweep shows that introducing uncertainty through a subpar ancestry method (neighbour interpolation) inflates the het fraction and the significance-profile noise.

The framework below formalizes what those results show empirically: the spatial geometry of the GWAS profile is itself a diagnostic of ancestry-inference quality.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Wavelet Detail Energy
Wavelet decompositions separate local high-frequency fluctuations from large-scale structure. High detail energy indicates local roughness and therefore increased noise.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Fourier High-Frequency Energy
Because random fluctuations introduce rapid oscillations, noise increases the proportion of spectral power contained in high frequencies. The normalized quantity
\[
\frac{\sum_{f>f_c}P(f)}
{\sum_fP(f)}
\]
provides one measure of roughness (a band-limited version of it — power between two physical cutoffs — is what the caller-noisiness table computes).

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Autocorrelation summaries
The lag-one autocorrelation
\[
\rho_1
=
\operatorname{cor}(S_i,S_{i+1}),
\]
and the integrated autocorrelation
\[
Q
=
\sum_{k=1}^{K}\rho(k)
\]
summarize coherence in the lag domain. A lag-one autocorrelation near one implies neighboring markers carry nearly identical evidence; near zero, they behave almost independently. Models producing larger \(Q\) generate more coherent landscapes.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
A three-axis test
For each parameter set \(\theta\), measure three independent quantities:

Ancestry-reconstruction accuracy against simulated truth, \(E_A(\theta)=P(\hat A_\theta\neq A_{\mathrm{true}})\) — state accuracy, donor recall, false-switch rate, breakpoint error, tract-length distribution.
GWAS-landscape coherence, \(\rho(d)\) or the band-limited FFT noise \(N_{\mathrm{FFT}}=\sum_{f>f_c}P(f)/\sum_f P(f)\) — the caller-noisiness table’s acf_lag1 / fft_band.
Biological mapping performance \(M(\theta)\): recovery of simulated QTL, peak-localization error, effect-size recovery, false-positive rate.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
What discriminates the hypotheses

Under H1: \(\arg\max_\theta \rho(\theta)\neq\arg\min_\theta E_A(\theta)\) — the smoothest profile is not the most accurate reconstruction, and coherence keeps rising with more smoothing.
Under H2: \(\arg\min_\theta E_A(\theta)\approx\arg\max_\theta M(\theta)\) — accuracy and mapping performance peak together, at the resolution matching the true ancestry-block scale; beyond it, over-smoothing loses breakpoints and mapping precision falls.

So the correct optimization target is not \(\max_\theta \rho(\theta)\) (which rewards over-smoothing) but maximum recovery of the true genotype states, with coherence a downstream consequence. This is exactly why the caller comparison is read at each caller’s calibrated (minimum-FDR) cutoff rather than at maximal smoothness.
The framework is falsifiable: a caller that produced a smoother, more autocorrelated GWAS profile without a lower ancestry-reconstruction error would support H1 (model-imposed filtering), not H2 (recovery).

title: The nilHMM caller family: one duration-aware HMM, swappable emissions
text:
Scope. This note is the methods backbone of the paper (Oxford Bioinformatics §2.1–2.2). It describes the one engine and the four callers that ride on it, and shows each running through the installed nilHMM package — establishing the (data, params) → calls contract every later benchmark relies on. API details live in the package’s pkgdown site (https://sawers-rellan-labs.github.io/nilhmm/); this note cites it rather than restating signatures.

Emits (paper): Figure F1 (engine + caller-family schematic). Ports/refactors the zealtiger notes generative_vs_discriminative_callers, emission_by_depth_regime, genotype_likelihoods_and_hmm, snp50k_genotype_identifiability, and the ATLAS section of nilhmm_sanity_check_paint, rewritten onto library(nilHMM).



Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(nilHMM)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
# shared analysis helpers (staging / metrics / plotting)
for (f in list.files(here::here("R"), "\\.R$", full.names = TRUE)) source(f)



1. The shared engine
A single duration-aware 3-state (REF / HET / ALT) HMM. Two axes are swappable and define each caller; everything else (Viterbi decode, sticky transitions, design start-priors) is shared:

Emission — how a marker’s data scores against each state:

count / BetaBinomial (emission_count()) — allele counts at SNP sites.
gt / categorical genotype-confusion (emission_gt()) — hard genotypes.

Duration / rigidity — how long the chain stays in a state: duration_geometric(), duration_rigidity(), duration_hsmm().

Design start-priors come from design_priors("BC2S2" | "BC2S3") (donor allele frequency and heterozygosity expected from the pedigree). The data-agnostic contract is call_ancestry(data, caller, design, params...) → segment table.


2. The four callers










Caller
Emission
Duration
