The Julia-free RTIGER port (memory rtiger-fork) is what lets all four share one R + Rcpp engine. Each caller gets a §2.2 subsection in the manuscript; the prose is drafted in the RILAB LaTeX template in the separate manuscript repo (see the README section→note→figure map).


3. Smoke test — every caller returns a valid segment table
This chunk proves the pipeline: the same engine, four callers, each producing a common-schema segment table. It renders off-mount by falling back to a tiny bundled fixture when the real count files are absent.


Show code
# TODO: point at a small bundled fixture under data/ once the manifest (B3) is
# frozen. Until then this documents the intended call surface.
demo_calls <- function(path, ...) {
  if (!file.exists(path)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  as_common_schema(call_ancestry(read_counts(path), ...),
    source = "demo", name = basename(path)
  )
}
# nNIL:   demo_calls(f, caller = "nnil",   design = "BC2S2", rrate = 1e-4, err = 0.01)
# RTIGER: demo_calls(f, caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 5L)
cat("nilHMM", as.character(packageVersion("nilHMM")), "loaded; engine ready.\n")


nilHMM 0.1.0 loaded; engine ready.




4. What each caller is for

nNIL — the count baseline; Holland’s near-isogenic-line caller.
RTIGER — rigidity replaces a free geometric duration; robust at low depth.
binhmm — anchors REF at the coverage floor on dense per-bin ALT_FREQ; the only track that keeps the full ~27.6 M-SNP density (skim only).
ATLAS — competitive transcript mapping → hard GT → categorical emission; expression-agnostic, built for BRB-seq (RNA), where a count model is confounded by expression and allele-specific expression.

See simulation-calibration.qmd for how each caller’s parameters are calibrated from simulations, and source-method-comparison.qmd for the cross-modality benchmark.

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
text:
At ~0.4× coverage the SNP50K genotype matrix looks pathological: 70% of cells are missing, of the rest 97% are hom-REF, essentially none are hom-ALT, and wherever a minority (ALT) read appears the site is overwhelmingly called heterozygous (the depth-split below). Taken at face value that reads as a genome that is almost entirely B73 with a thin, ubiquitous sprinkle of heterozygosity — which is not what a BC2S3 NIL is. This note asks the two questions that pattern forces:
Short answer. (1) The pattern is the caller’s prior, not biology: a MAP / HWE caller (bcftools, GLIMPSE, …) turns any lone minority read into a HET because the rare-allele prior overturns the 2:1 single-read likelihood for hom-ALT; GATK, being maximum-likelihood, instead scatters spurious hom-ALT. Neither is trustworthy per site at this depth. (2) GLs are the correct currency, and accumulation across a stretch does help — but only for a shared latent state (the ancestry of the segment), which the count-based HMM already exploits because the read counts are the GL’s sufficient statistic. Accumulation does not manufacture per-site zygosity information, and the dilution ceiling (d ≈ 0.3, snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md) is unchanged by moving from counts to GLs.
Notation: biallelic site, reference (B73) allele \(A\), alternate (donor) allele \(B\); genotypes \(\{AA, AB, BB\}=\{0/0, 0/1, 1/1\}\); per-base error \(\varepsilon = 10^{-Q/10}\).

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
section: 1. The single-site genotype likelihood — identical core in GATK, ANGSD, samtools
text:
1. The single-site genotype likelihood — identical core in GATK, ANGSD, samtools
All three tools compute the likelihood \(P(\text{reads}\mid G)\) per genotype with the same diploid-averaging model (ANGSD calls it “the GATK/dragon model”; samtools adds a read-dependency term that vanishes at depth 1):
\[
P(\text{reads}\mid G=\{A_1,A_2\}) = \prod_{i}\Big[\tfrac12 P(b_i\mid A_1) + \tfrac12 P(b_i\mid A_2)\Big],
\qquad
P(b\mid A) = \begin{cases} 1-\varepsilon & b = A\\[2pt] \varepsilon/3 & b \neq A. \end{cases}
\]
Each allele of the diploid genotype contributes with weight \(\tfrac12\) — this is why the heterozygote likelihood of any single read is pinned near \(\tfrac12\).

One ALT read (\(b=B\), \(\varepsilon=0.01\))










genotype
likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid G)\)
value
Phred \(-10\log_{10}L\)
normalized PL




\(AA\)
\(\varepsilon/3\)
0.0033
24.8
25


\(AB\)
\(\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)\)
0.498
3.0
3


\(BB\)
\(1-\varepsilon\)
0.99
0.04
0




PL \(= -10\log_{10} L\), normalized by subtracting the smallest so the best genotype is 0 (the GATK worked example: probabilities \(100\times\) apart give PLs \(20\) apart). PL is a likelihood, emitted under an effectively flat genotype prior — despite GATK’s docs writing “\(P(\text{Genotype}\mid\text{Data})\)”.
GQ \(=\) (2nd-smallest PL) \(-\) (smallest PL), capped at 99. Here GQ = 3.

So by likelihood alone, one alt read points to BB (homozygous-alt), GQ 3 — a \(\approx 2{:}1\) call (\(\tfrac{1-\varepsilon}{1/2}\approx 2 \Rightarrow 3\) PL). That 3-PL likelihood ratio is the entire information content of one read. No downstream step adds to it; it can only be combined with a prior or with other sites.

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
section: 2. Likelihood → posterior: where the prior lives, and what swapping it does
text:
2. Likelihood → posterior: where the prior lives, and what swapping it does
The genotype call and its meaning come from the posterior
\[
P(G \mid \text{reads}) \;\propto\; \underbrace{P(\text{reads}\mid G)}_{\text{GL — identical across tools}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\pi(G)}_{\text{prior — tool-specific, swappable}} .
\]
Where each tool applies \(\pi\):








tool
GL stage
prior stage




GATK
HaplotypeCaller PairHMM → PL (likelihood)
GenotypeGVCFs allele-freq/HWE prior (--heterozygosity, default \(10^{-3}\)); CalculateGenotypePosteriors for custom (pedigree / population-AF) priors


ANGSD
-GL models (GATK / samtools / SOAPsnp) → GLs, no calling by default
-doPost 1 = HWE prior at estimated allele freq; -doPost 2 = uniform


bcftools
mpileup (Li 2011) → GLs
call -m allele-freq prior with -P mutation-rate hyperprior




HWE prior at the cohort frequency → forces HET
The genotype prior is Hardy–Weinberg at the cohort alt-allele frequency \(p\) (measured mean \(\bar p = 0.015\)): \(\pi(AA,AB,BB)=((1-p)^2,\,2p(1-p),\,p^2)\). Multiplying the one-read likelihoods (from §1) by this prior and normalizing, at \(p=\bar p=0.015\) and \(\varepsilon=0.01\):












genotype
likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid G)\)
\(L\)
HWE prior \(\pi(G)\)
\(\pi\)
posterior \(\propto L\cdot\pi\)
\(P(G\mid\text{read})\)




\(AA\)
\(\varepsilon/3\)
0.0033
\((1-p)^2\)
0.970
\(\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3}\cdot(1-p)^2\)
0.178


\(AB\)
\(\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)\)
0.498
\(2p(1-p)\)
0.0296
\(p(1-p)\)
0.810


\(BB\)
\(1-\varepsilon\)
0.99
\(p^2\)
0.000225
\((1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2\)
0.012



Taking the ratio of the two non-REF rows’ posteriors (\(\propto L\cdot\pi\)), HET beats ALT-hom by
\[
\frac{P(AB\mid\text{read})}{P(BB\mid\text{read})}
= \frac{p(1-p)}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2}
= \frac{1-p}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p} \approx \frac1p \approx 66 ,
\]
matching the table’s \(0.810/0.012 \approx 67\). The rare-allele prior overturns the \(2{:}1\) likelihood — it collapses the ALT-hom posterior to ~1% and hands 81% to the heterozygote, even though the read’s likelihood most favored \(BB\). The call is the prior’s, not the read’s. This is exactly the depth-1 row of the motivating table above — 448,805 HET vs 355 ALT calls on single reads — and the overall 97% REF / 0.00% ALT-hom pattern in the SNP50K matrix (snp50k-cohort-provenance.md).
Posterior callers inherit this, and most assume HWE. bcftools call -m, freebayes, ANGSD -doPost, and the imputation tools Beagle and GLIMPSE are all posterior callers: each weights the genotype likelihood by a prior and reports the genotype that maximizes the product, the maximum-a-posteriori (MAP) estimate.
\[
\hat G = \arg\max_G P(G\mid\text{data}) = \arg\max_G P(\text{data}\mid G)\,\pi(G) .
\]
The maximum-likelihood choice, by contrast, keeps only the likelihood \(P(\text{data}\mid G)\) and drops the prior \(\pi\). The prior these callers assume is almost always Hardy–Weinberg: at the estimated allele frequency for bcftools, freebayes, and ANGSD -doPost, and at a reference panel’s frequencies for Beagle and GLIMPSE. So at a rare site every one of them turns a lone ALT read into a HET call. The HWE prior, not the read, decides.
GATK is the exception. It does not call genotypes on the posterior: its per-sample GT is the maximum-likelihood genotype, and the heterozygosity prior only gates whether the site is variant. There a lone ALT read is called hom-ALT, not HET. The companion note GATK does not call genotypes on posteriors explains why, and why GATK’s own heterozygote excess has a separate paralog and coverage cause.


Breeding prior — the single-locus NIL genotype frequencies
The NIL design is not HWE. Selfing after backcross drives heterozygotes to homozygotes. Single-locus genotype frequencies (donor allele freq \(0.125\); selfing preserves it):










design
\(f_{AA}\) (REF-hom)
\(f_{AB}\) (het)
\(f_{BB}\) (donor-hom)
het : donor-hom | introgressed




BC2S2 (bulked skim)
0.844
0.0625
0.0938
40 : 60


BC2S3 (nominal NIL)
0.859
0.0312
0.1094
22 : 78



Note the sign — \(f_{BB} > f_{AB}\) — by design, an introgressed locus is more often donor-homozygous than heterozygous (78% vs 22% for BC2S3), because selfing removes heterozygosity. (This corrects a claim in an earlier draft that the introgression is “mostly het” — the design expects mostly donor-hom; the callers report mostly het only because donor-hom is undetectable at \(0.4\times\), §5.)


Swapping the prior flips the single-read call
The same one alt read, now with the BC2S3 breeding prior (likelihood from §1 \(\times\) prior, at \(\varepsilon=0.01\), posterior normalized to sum to 1):

Taking the ratio of the two non-REF rows’ posteriors, HET now loses to ALT-hom:
\[
\frac{P(AB\mid\text{read})}{P(BB\mid\text{read})}
= \frac{\tfrac12 f_{AB}}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot f_{BB}}
= \frac{0.5 \times 0.0312}{0.99 \times 0.1094} \approx 0.14
\quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{DONOR-HOM} \ (\approx 7{:}1),
\]
matching the table’s \(0.123/0.855\).
So the identical read and likelihood is called HET under the HWE prior and DONOR-HOM under the breeding prior. This is the crux for the “fix the prior” argument: swapping HWE → breeding frequencies is correct and worthwhile (it counteracts the coverage-induced het-bias), but it only relabels the default — it adds no zygosity information. The data still speaks at only \(\approx 3\) PL. One read cannot resolve het from hom under any prior; the prior decides the tie.

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
section: 3. From one site to a stretch: what accumulation can and cannot do
text:
3. From one site to a stretch: what accumulation can and cannot do
Accumulating across a gene / haplotype / bin is the right instinct. The subtlety is what latent variable the accumulation is evidence for. Two targets:
(a) The genotype at each individual site. Borrowing across sites requires an explicit haplotype/LD model — a reference panel (Beagle/GLIMPSE, Li–Stephens) or a founder graph (PHG). Multiplying GLs across sites of different genotypes is meaningless without such a model to link them. This route needs the donor founders / phased panel we lack, and teosinte’s short-range LD undercuts it.
(b) A single latent state shared over the stretch — the ancestry (REF / HET / donor-hom segment, or “which founder”). If the stretch has one state \(S\), each site’s reads are a conditionally-independent emission and the product concentrates the posterior on \(S\):
\[
P(S \mid \text{reads}_{1:n}) \;\propto\; \pi(S)\prod_{j=1}^{n}
\underbrace{\Big[\textstyle\sum_{G} P(\text{reads}_j\mid G)\,P(G\mid S)\Big]}_{\text{emission}_j \;=\; \sum (\text{GL}) \times (\text{state-conditional genotype dist})}.
\]
This is exactly RTIGER / binhmm / PHG’s findPaths. It works without a panel because the linkage is physical contiguity plus the breeding design (few recombinations) — not population LD.

The count emission is the GL emission
For a biallelic site under an independent per-read error model, the pair \((n_{\text{ref}}, n_{\text{alt}})\) is the sufficient statistic of the per-site genotype likelihood: the emission \(\sum_G P(\text{reads}_j\mid G)P(G\mid S)\) depends on the reads only through those counts. nilHMM’s count / BetaBinomial emission is a direct parameterization of this (the BetaBinomial simply adds overdispersion for mapping/paralog noise). Therefore:

“Use genotype likelihoods in the HMM instead of counts” is, for a biallelic site, already implemented — phrased as counts. The caller never hard-calls per site (so it never inherits the bcftools 0%-ALT-hom prior artifact); it feeds soft per-site evidence into the along-chromosome HMM and decodes the segment.

What full GLs add over raw counts is real but marginal at 1 read/site: per-base quality weighting (down-weight low-\(Q\) bases rather than counting equally) and mapping-quality / multiallelic handling. The information axis is the same: \((n_{\text{ref}}, n_{\text{alt}})\) and their qualities.

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
section: 4. Two different HMMs — do not conflate GATK’s PairHMM
text:
4. Two different HMMs — do not conflate GATK’s PairHMM
GATK’s HaplotypeCaller does use an HMM, but it is orthogonal to the one Ruben wants:









GATK PairHMM
ancestry HMM (RTIGER / nilHMM / PHG)




hidden path over
bases within one read
marker sites along the chromosome


emission
read base vs candidate haplotype (indels, base-Q)
reads at a site vs latent ancestry state


purpose
local re-assembly / alignment likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid\text{hap})\)
segment ancestry / genotype imputation



GATK implements the alignment PairHMM, not the along-genome ancestry HMM. “They already do this” is true only for the alignment step; it is not a reusable component for ancestry segmentation. Keep the two distinct in the paper.

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
text:
5. What survives — the information ceiling
Accumulation via the ancestry HMM buys a confident segment state, and the per-marker genotype is then that state projected/imputed onto each marker — PHG-style, and valid for GWAS/QTL (the mapping unit is the segment/dosage genotype). It is not an independent per-site zygosity measurement.
Three limits no GL/HMM refinement removes:

The signal is compressed, not hidden by hard-calling. Outbred-donor dilution puts the states at alt-fractions \(\approx\{0,\,d/2,\,d\}=\{0,\,0.15,\,0.30\}\) with \(d\approx0.3\) (empirically confirmed, snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md §2.5). GLs cannot create information the reads do not carry.
Het vs donor-hom stays weak — \(0.15\) vs \(0.30\) separation. A breeding prior (\(f_{BB}>f_{AB}\)) helps counteract the coverage het-bias, but does not resolve the zygosity.
The real levers are upstream, not in the emission: (i) site selection to donor-informative markers (raise \(d\) toward 1, restoring \(\{0,\tfrac12,1\}\) separation), (ii) fit the emission means per taxon, (iii) use the BC2S2/BC2S3 prior in place of HWE.

Bottom line for the “GLs will rescue it” claim: GLs are the right soft evidence and the ancestry HMM is the right accumulator — and both are essentially what the count-emission caller already does. They rescue the segment/haplotype call (and the imputed marker genotypes), not the isolated site, and they do not lift the dilution ceiling.

title: Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM
text:
References
Genotype-likelihood models and callers

Li H (2011). A statistical framework for SNP calling, mutation discovery, association mapping and population genetical parameter estimation from sequencing data. Bioinformatics 27(21):2987–2993. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btr509. — the samtools/bcftools GL model.
Korneliussen TS, Albrechtsen A, Nielsen R (2014). ANGSD: Analysis of Next Generation Sequencing Data. BMC Bioinformatics 15:356. doi:10.1186/s12859-014-0356-4. — GL-model taxonomy + likelihood-propagation framework; software page: https://www.popgen.dk/angsd/index.php/Genotype_Likelihoods.
Poplin R, Ruano-Rubio V, DePristo MA, et al. (2017). Scaling accurate genetic variant discovery to tens of thousands of samples. bioRxiv 201178. doi:10.1101/201178. — GATK HaplotypeCaller.
DePristo MA, Banks E, Poplin R, et al. (2011). A framework for variation discovery and genotyping using next-generation DNA sequencing data. Nature Genetics 43(5):491–498. doi:10.1038/ng.806. — the GATK framework; PL/GQ mechanics documented at https://gatk.broadinstitute.org/hc/en-us/articles/360035890451.

Linkage / imputation models

Li N, Stephens M (2003). Modeling linkage disequilibrium and identifying recombination hotspots using single-nucleotide polymorphism data. Genetics 165(4):2213–2233. doi:10.1093/genetics/165.4.2213. — the haplotype-mosaic (Li–Stephens) model behind panel imputation (§3a).
Jensen SE, Charles JR, Muleta K, et al. (2020). A sorghum practical haplotype graph facilitates genome-wide imputation and cost-effective genomic prediction. The Plant Genome 13(1):e20009. doi:10.1002/tpg2.20009. — the founder-graph (PHG) route (§3, §5).
Hui R, D’Atanasio E, Cassidy LM, Scheib CL, Kivisild T (2020). Evaluating genotype imputation pipeline for ultra-low coverage ancient genomes. Scientific Reports 10:18542. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-75387-w. — reference-panel imputation at ultra-low coverage (§3a).


Companion to snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md and snp50k-cohort-provenance.md. 2026-07-02.

title: Genome coverage theory: the Poisson model of missing data
text: Purpose. Establish the theoretical baseline for reasoning about missing genotype data in a low-coverage sequencing population. Before we fit any model to the BZea data (missing-data-floor-model.qmd) or compare fits across datasets (missing-data-model-comparison.qmd), we need the expectation that a purely random, Poisson sampling of reads would produce. Everything downstream is measured as a departure from this baseline.

title: Genome coverage theory: the Poisson model of missing data
text:
Theory
Ewens & Grant (2005) describe the coverage of a genome as a Poisson process. The probability of observing \(x\) reads at a given site is
\[P(x) = \frac{\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}}{x!}\]
where:

\(x\) is the number of short reads covering a given site on the genome,
\(\lambda = \dfrac{LN}{G}\) is the sequencing redundancy (mean coverage),

and:

\(G\) is the genome length (in bp),
\(L\) is the short-read average length,
\(N\) is the number of short reads sequenced.

title: Genome coverage theory: the Poisson model of missing data
text:
For the BZea population
The NAM v5 B73 genome is about 2.2 Gb:


Show code
G <- 2.2e9


The mean read length is 150 bp:


Show code
L <- 150


The nominal coverage for BZea is 0.8:


Show code
lambda <- 0.8


Then the implied number of sequenced reads per sample is:


Show code
N <- G * lambda / L
N / 1e6


[1] 11.73333


i.e. each sample has ~12 million reads. Checking the other direction:


Show code
12e6 * 150 / 2.2e9


[1] 0.8181818


If 1400 samples were sequenced:


Show code
n <- 1400
total_reads <- n * 12e6


and a lane yields ~4e9 reads:


Show code
total_reads / 4e9


[1] 4.2


so roughly 4 lanes were sequenced.
Fraction of the genome covered (at least one read at a site) under the Poisson model:


Show code
p <- 1 - exp(-lambda)
p


[1] 0.550671


Expected number of samples with at least one read at each position:


Show code
round(n * p, 0)


[1] 771


with standard deviation:


Show code
sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))


[1] 18.61197


Expected number of missing genotypes per position (median):


Show code
n - qbinom(0.50, n, p)


[1] 629

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text: Date: 2026-07-03 (updated after fixing a determinism bug in rtiger_fit_cpp) Machine: Apple Silicon (arm64), 10 cores, macOS (Darwin 25.5); R 4.5. Reference implementation: RTIGER R package (Julia backend via juliaup), rHMM_methods.jl. Test implementation: nilHMM::call_ancestry(caller = "rtiger") — a C++ (Rcpp) port of the RTIGER fork in src/rtiger.cpp / R/rtiger.R. Data: the real SNP50K BZea NIL counts staged in the zealtiger repo (data/rtiger_50K/): per-taxon cohorts of counts/<donor>/<sample>.tsv, the 51,991-SNP teosinte-informative panel. Taxa Zh (n=61) and Zl (n=121). Reference RTIGER calls/fits: calls_taxa_r8.csv, fits_taxa*/<taxon>/rtiger_result.rds.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Summary
nilHMM’s rtiger reproduces RTIGER faithfully on both Zh and Zl: identical optimizer, identical convergence criterion, and — after fixing a determinism bug found during this validation — the same converged emission and the same iteration count. Three findings underpin this, each verified against the actual Julia source and the saved RTIGER fits (not from code comments):

Same optimizer. nilHMM’s EM is a line-for-line C++ port of RTIGER’s Julia Baum–Welch: identical getlogpsi / productpsi / forward / backward / Viterbi kernels, and the identical emission M-step (closed-form BetaBinomial mean + univariate Brent on the precision, same bracket).
Same convergence criterion — a delta, not a deviance. Both stop on round(max(|Δα|,|Δβ|), 6) ≤ eps with eps = 0.01 (RTIGER’s Julia source, line 1547, and its R wrapper default; confirmed against a run’s fit_progress.log). An earlier draft of this note said RTIGER used a “deviance” criterion — that was wrong; the EMdev/dev() function is unused.
Same preprocessing. RTIGER decodes covered markers only (~12–16 k of 51,991 per sample) and requires ≥ 2·rigidity covered markers per chromosome, aborting otherwise. nilHMM now matches both (see fixes below).

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Correctness







taxon / comparison
result




Zh — identical params + identical covered markers (decode of RTIGER’s exact fit)
100.00% per-marker; ALT 4387/4387 (recall & precision 1.000)


Zh — nilHMM’s own r=8 fit + covered filter vs RTIGER
99.95% per-marker; donor-present Dice 1.000; ALT recall 0.965


Zl — nilHMM’s own r=8 fit vs RTIGER (emission means, QC’d)
19 iterations, means 0.99 / 0.870 / 0.659 vs RTIGER 19 iters, 0.99 / 0.870 / 0.661


Zh — nilHMM’s own r=5 fit vs RTIGER (per-marker, 848 k)
99.76%; ALT recall 0.944 / precision 0.802


Zl — nilHMM’s own r=5 fit vs RTIGER (per-marker, 1.98 M)
99.95%; ALT recall 0.997 / precision 0.989




Given the same parameters and marker support, the two Viterbis are bit-for-bit identical, including the rare donor-hom state.
With nilHMM doing its own fit, it now converges to RTIGER’s emission on both taxa — Zl matches iteration-for-iteration (19) and mean-for-mean (to ~2e-3, i.e. float-summation / Brent-tolerance noise). The residual on Zh (0.05% of markers) is HET↔︎ALT boundary jitter on the rare, overdispersed donor-hom state.
Note: RTIGER’s own Zl fit is a poorly-identified optimum (states at 0.99/0.87/0.66, barely separated — Zl sits on a flat likelihood ridge). nilHMM now lands on that same ridge point rather than wandering off it.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
section: The determinism bug (found and fixed during this validation)
text:
The determinism bug (found and fixed during this validation)
An earlier draft claimed nilHMM’s fit was “deterministic (seeded).” It was not. On Zl, repeated identical calls (same data, seed, threads) returned different iteration counts (1, 2, 3, 8…) and different converged means — sometimes returning the init unchanged after “1 iteration.”
Root cause, traced through src/rtiger.cpp:

A chain (sample × chromosome) with fewer than 2·rigidity covered markers has an empty zeta window; its E-step collapses to exp(-Inf − (-Inf)) = NaN, which poisons the pooled sufficient statistics. The emission M-step’s NaN fallback then silently kept the init, reporting a bogus 1-iteration “convergence.”
The E-step was always dispatched through RcppParallel parallelFor, even at threads = 1 (there was no real serial path). parallelFor did not reliably process the whole range across repeated calls, so it nondeterministically skipped the degenerate chains — masking the NaN on some calls and not others.

Fixes (src/rtiger.cpp):

Real serial path for threads ≤ 1 — the worker is called directly, not through parallelFor. Deterministic; threads > 1 keeps the parallel fold.
Hard stop on under-covered chains (< 2·rigidity), matching RTIGER’s Julia abort — up front on the main thread, with a clear message — instead of silently skipping or NaN-ing. A totw guard remains as a backstop for empty input.

Verified after the fix: QC’d input is deterministic across threads = 1 and threads = 4 and repeated calls (all 19 iterations, means 0.99/0.870/0.659, matching RTIGER); under-covered input hard-stops with "rtiger: chain N has M covered markers, below the 2*rigidity = K floor …".

min_cov (covered-marker preprocessing)
call_states/call_ancestry gained min_cov on the rtiger path (default 1L): markers with n_ref + n_alt < min_cov are dropped before fit/decode, matching RTIGER’s covered-only decoding. min_cov = 0L restores decoding every marker.

At matched threads = 4, nilHMM is consistently ~30% faster than the Julia RTIGER across the whole rigidity grid.

On the earlier “1.4× slower” numbers. An earlier draft reported nilHMM at ~1.2–1.9× slower. That was a machine-contention artifact: the pre-fix sweep ran alongside other jobs, inflating nilHMM’s times (r=2 was 36.2 s under load vs 14.5 s quiet) while Julia’s stayed ~21 s. On a quiet machine at matched threads, nilHMM wins. Timing is load-sensitive; single-run, single-machine.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
nnil vs rtiger (same covered support)
With min_cov applied to both callers, nnil and rtiger now decode the identical covered-marker set, so their calls join directly, per-marker, with no support mismatch. This is a caller-vs-caller concordance (there is no ground truth on real data; rtiger is the reference, itself validated against Julia RTIGER above). Taxon Zh (61 samples, 848,498 covered markers), design = "BC2S2".












caller

< 0.1 Mb

vs rtiger (per-marker / donor-present)




rtiger r=8
1.0 s
743

—

1.0 s

98.2% / 98.8%


nnil rrate=1e-3
1.0 s
797

114
5.4 Mb
98.0% / 98.5%



Reading it — the two are different models and agree to ~98%:

Soft (nnil) vs hard (rtiger) segmentation. rtiger’s rigidity is a hard minimum run length — it structurally cannot emit a donor block shorter than r markers. nnil’s rrate is a soft per-marker switch prior — it only discourages short blocks. So nnil admits a larger tiny-block tail (40 vs 18 sub-0.1 Mb blocks): single covered markers with a spurious donor read open a 1-marker block that rtiger forbids. (Raising nnil’s err toward the real per-read error rate absorbs these; see the fragmentation exploration.)
3-state breakpoints go the other way — nnil 484 < rtiger 743. rtiger’s extra transitions are HET↔︎ALT flips within donor regions; nnil’s duration prior smooths those, so it fragments the donor state less even while admitting a few more tiny donor blocks.
rrate is the fragmentation knob for nnil: 1e-4 → 465 blocks (median 9.9 Mb); 1e-3 → 705 blocks (median 5.4 Mb), matching rtiger’s block count less well. Neither rrate nor err was calibrated here — these are illustrative defaults, not an optimized operating point.
Speed is equal on the covered support (~1.0 s each for 61 samples); both benefit from the min_cov filter (nnil ~4× faster than on the full panel).

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Performance (engineering, measured during this work)
Parallelism and amortization added to nilHMM while validating against RTIGER — each verified to preserve exact output:

Per-sample parallelism. call_states’ per-sample loop (emission fit + decode) is independent across samples; it now runs via parallel::mclapply (threads) with an rbindlist assembly. fit_means on Zl (121 samples): 45 s → 13.4 s (3.3×) at threads = 8, bit-identical output.
to_segments vectorized. Replaced the per-(name, chr) RLE loop + do.call(rbind) with one O(n) pass (order by group+pos; a run starts wherever the group or state changes). On 1.98 M markers: ~6.5 s → 1.3 s (5×), bit-identical. Speeds every caller and both sweep modes.
caller_sweep() — fit-once parameter scan. The segmentation parameter (rigidity/rrate) is a prior, not an emission property, so the emission is fit once (at ref) and the grid is swept by decodes fanned across threads. On a 12-rigidity Zl grid (threads = 8): 10.4 s vs 37.1 s cold (3.6×); the advantage grows with grid size and shrinks when the grid fits in cores (cold’s fits parallelize). Workflow: scan with refit = "none" → pick the best value → take the exact final calls from one call_ancestry() at that value.

refit = "none" (fit once, reuse) is exact at ref and a close approximation elsewhere: mean seg-overlap-to-cold 0.93, but min 0.62 at the grid extremes — keep ref central and the grid not too wide.
refit = "cold" refits per value (exact baseline; == per-value call_ancestry).
A warm-start mode was evaluated and dropped: on the multimodal rtiger fit it reaches a different optimum than cold, so it is neither exact nor better than none for ranking (warm seg-overlap-to-cold 0.94–0.96, below none’s 0.97–0.99, and slower).


Two dead ends, recorded for honesty: an is.data.frame “hotspot” in the fit_means EM was a profiling artifact (fixing it changed nothing; reverted), and the warm-start’s “exact but cheaper” premise fails on multimodal fits (above).

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Reproduce
Scripts (session scratchpad; data paths point at the zealtiger repo):

time_sweep.R — head-to-head timing (JULIA_NUM_THREADS=4 Rscript time_sweep.R).
definitive.R / covered.R — identical-input → 100% per-marker check (Zh).
endtoend.R — nilHMM’s own-fit + covered filter vs RTIGER (Zh, 99.95%).
qc_det.R — determinism across threads + Zl emission match to RTIGER (post-fix).
iters_zl.R — EM iteration counts, nilHMM vs RTIGER’s saved fits.
nnil_vs_rtiger.R — nnil vs rtiger on the shared covered support (Zh).
bench_sweep.R / cmp_refit.R — caller_sweep vs cold timing + refit-mode fidelity.
tseg_check.R — to_segments correctness (vs old RLE) + speed.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Conclusion
nilHMM’s rtiger is a faithful reproduction of RTIGER — identical optimizer, kernels, and delta convergence criterion; exact calls on identical input; and, after the determinism fix, the same converged fit and iteration count on both Zh and Zl, at r = 5 and r = 8 (~99.8–99.95% per-marker). The two behavioural gaps to RTIGER are now closed: decoding all markers (fixed by min_cov) and the under-covered-chain NaN/nondeterminism (fixed by the serial path + 2·rigidity hard stop). On a quiet machine at matched threads, nilHMM’s rtiger is ~30% faster than the Julia RTIGER across the rigidity grid. Beyond parity, the engineering pass (per-sample mclapply, a 5× to_segments, and the fit-once caller_sweep) makes cohort fits and rigidity/rrate scans several-fold cheaper again.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
Does the Broman/simcross BC2S3 simulation reproduce the single-locus breeding-scheme expectation for the REF/Het/ALT genome fractions? This notebook runs that check on the TeoNAM native v5 cM map (the same 118K-marker grid the per-coverage caller calibration uses), validating the simulator this repo’s coverage-degradation sweeps rely on. It is a circularity check: before trusting a caller’s recovery of the truth, confirm the truth-generator itself is unbiased.
The sim stack is zealhmm’s own (R/simulate.R: .bcsft_pedigree, .simulate_dosage); the single-locus expectation and the design-correct goodness-of-fit test are in R/single_locus.R (ported from the exploratory zealtiger repo). Why Hotelling and not Pearson, why Mb on real marker coordinates, and the ergodicity distinction are explained inline.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
Setup


Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(data.table)
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(simcross)
  library(patchwork)
  library(here)
})
source(here("R/simulate.R")) # .bcsft_pedigree, .chr_cm_lengths, .simulate_dosage
source(here("R/single_locus.R")) # single_locus_p0, state_fractions_mb, hotelling_fractions, plot_fraction_forest
set.seed(params$seed)

# TeoNAM native v5 cM map — the 118K-marker grid used by the caller calibration.
mc <- fread(here("data/teonam/markers_v5_gwas118k_cm.tsv"))
map <- mc[, .(chr = as.integer(chr), bp = as.integer(pos_v5), cm = as.numeric(cm))][order(chr, bp)]
markers <- map # the native markers directly carry chr/bp/cm — no re-gridding

bp <- .bcsft_pedigree(n_bc = 2L, n_self = 3L)
ped <- bp$ped
nid <- bp$nil_id
cmlen <- .chr_cm_lengths(map)
cat(sprintf(
  "simcross %s | native markers: %d | genome: %.0f cM | N: %d\n",
  as.character(packageVersion("simcross")), nrow(markers), sum(cmlen$L), params$n
))


simcross 0.8 | native markers: 118514 | genome: 1559 cM | N: 1500

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
1. The single-locus expectation (BC2S3 transition matrix)
The genotype distribution at any single locus after F1 → 2 backcrosses → 3 selfings, from the breeding-scheme transition matrices (map- and interference-independent — a marginal quantity).


Show code
p0 <- single_locus_p0(n_bc = 2L, n_self = 3L)
tibble(state = names(p0), expected = as.numeric(p0)) |>
  mutate(note = c("hom recurrent", "het", "hom donor")) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 6)





state

Show code
cat(sprintf(
  "donor dosage = ALT + 0.5*Het = %.4f (expect 0.1250)\n",
  p0["ALT"] + 0.5 * p0["Het"]
))


donor dosage = ALT + 0.5*Het = 0.1250 (expect 0.1250)

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
2. f_i validation (Mb-based, Hotelling)
Simulate n NILs on the native map, tabulate per-NIL Mb fractions, and test the population means against p0 with the design-correct Hotelling/Wald χ²(2) (sample = unit; empirical between-sample covariance — not per-marker binomial, which linkage overdispersion would make reject a correct sampler).


Show code
F <- t(vapply(seq_len(params$n), function(i) {
  state_fractions_mb(markers, .simulate_dosage(ped, cmlen, markers,
    m = params$m, p = params$p, nil_id = nid
  ))
}, numeric(3)))
colnames(F) <- c("REF", "Het", "ALT")
res <- hotelling_fractions(F, p0)
knitr::kable(res$table, digits = 5)





state
expected
estimate
se

ci_hi

in_ci

Show code
cat(sprintf(
  "Hotelling T^2 = %.3f (df=2), chi2 p = %.4g | N_eff(ALT) = %.1f vs %d markers\n",
  res$T2, res$p_chisq,
  with(res$table[res$table$state == "ALT", ], expected * (1 - expected) / sd_fi^2),
  nrow(markers)
))


Hotelling T^2 = 0.474 (df=2), chi2 p = 0.7891 | N_eff(ALT) = 23.1 vs 118514 markers


Show code
cat(if (all(res$table$in_ci)) {
  "PASS: all expectations inside 95% Wald CIs.\n"
} else {
  "CHECK: an expectation outside its CI.\n"
})


PASS: all expectations inside 95% Wald CIs.


The fractions span ~2 orders of magnitude, so a single linear forest plot can show magnitude or intervals but not both. Panel A (log axis) carries magnitude; panel B (standardized deviation, ±1.96 band) carries the pass criterion — scale-free.


Show code
plot_fraction_forest(res$table,
  title = sprintf(
    "BC2S3 vs single-locus expectation (n=%d, native v5 map)",
    params$n
  )
)









N_eff (not the marker count) is the ergodicity-limited effective sample size: with ~33 crossovers/genome the per-NIL fraction averages over few independent blocks, so the right SE is sd(f_i)/√N, not a per-marker binomial. This is the same ergodicity distinction developed in the wideseq coverage-ergodicity note.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
3. Marker-resolution sweep
The fractions above are measured on the full native grid. How does the Het fraction degrade as the grid coarsens? Short Het tracts that fall below the marker spacing are missed — a truth-measurement floor, distinct from the low-coverage Het miscalling an ancestry caller faces (layer 2). We simulate one set of NILs once and re-measure each on grids of increasing density (built by build_marker_grid), reporting Cohen’s \(d=(\text{mean}-\text{expected})/\text{SD}\) of the Het fraction — an N-independent effect size, negative when Het is undercounted.


Show code
set.seed(params$seed + 1L)
n_sweep <- min(params$n, 800L)
# simulate the shared meiosis once per NIL, then read dosage on any grid
sims <- lapply(seq_len(n_sweep), function(i) {
  simcross::sim_from_pedigree(ped, L = cmlen$L, m = params$m, p = params$p)
})
dosage_from_sim <- function(sim, mk) {
  d <- integer(nrow(mk))
  for (ci in seq_len(nrow(cmlen))) {
    rows <- which(mk$chr == cmlen$chr[ci])
    if (!length(rows)) next
    nil <- sim[[ci]][[nid]]
    cm <- mk$cm[rows]
    d[rows] <- (.hap_allele_at(nil$mat, cm) == 2L) + (.hap_allele_at(nil$pat, cm) == 2L)
  }
  d
}
dens <- c(500L, 1000L, 2000L, 4000L, 8000L, 16000L)
sw <- rbindlist(lapply(dens, function(D) {
  mk <- build_marker_grid(map, n_markers = D)
  het <- vapply(sims, function(s) state_fractions_mb(mk, dosage_from_sim(s, mk))[["Het"]], numeric(1))
  gap <- mk[, as.double(stats::median(diff(sort(bp)))), by = chr][, stats::median(V1)]
  data.table(
    markers = nrow(mk), spacing_Mb = round(gap / 1e6, 3),
    Het_mean = round(mean(het), 4),
    Het_d = round((mean(het) - p0[["Het"]]) / stats::sd(het), 3)
  )
}))
knitr::kable(sw, caption = sprintf(
  "Het fraction vs marker density (same %d NILs re-measured on each grid; expected Het = %.4f). Cohen's d = (mean - expected)/SD.",
  n_sweep, p0[["Het"]]
))



Het fraction vs marker density (same 800 NILs re-measured on each grid; expected Het = 0.0312). Cohen’s d = (mean - expected)/SD.

Show code
ggplot(sw, aes(spacing_Mb, Het_d)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = -0.05, ymax = 0.05, fill = "grey90") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2c7fb8") +
  geom_point(colour = "#2c7fb8", size = 2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "marker spacing (Mb, log)", y = "Het Cohen's d = (mean − expected)/SD",
    title = "Het is undercounted at coarse marker spacing",
    subtitle = "grey band = |d| < 0.05 (negligible)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()









Reading it: Het is undercounted (\(d<0\)) at coarse spacing — small Het segments fall below the marker resolution and are lost — and relaxes to a negligible residual (\(|d|<0.05\)) as the grid densifies. This is a truth-measurement floor, not a caller artifact; the layer-1 validation (§1–§2) does not depend on it.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
Conclusion
On the TeoNAM native v5 map, the simcross BC2S3 simulator reproduces the single-locus genotype fractions — all three states inside their 95% CIs, donor dosage 0.125 — so the truth-generator behind the coverage-degradation sweeps is unbiased at the single-locus margin. The only known deviation is a marker-resolution undercount of Het at coarse spacing (§3, negligible below ~0.5 Mb). Layer 1 (truth) is validated; caller observation/recovery under coverage is the calibration and sweep notebooks.

title: Cross-modality concordance on the paired BrB + skim cohort
text:
The benchmark cohort is the ~400 NILs that have BOTH BRB-seq and skim data. The paired design is the point: the same NIL genotype seen through two modalities, so cross-source disagreement is method, not sample. We apply the sim-calibrated parameters from simulation-calibration.qmd and quantify concordance per sample.

Emits (paper): Figure F4 (400-cohort source × method concordance), Results §3.3. Chromosome painting appears here only as supplementary QC (see nilhmm_sanity_check_paint.qmd), never as a main figure.







ImportantSTATUS: blocked (B5 open input)



Needs the ~400 paired-cohort sample manifest (IDs, donor species, per-source depth). Trace from sample_metatada.csv / memory bzea-sample-donor-metadata plus the BRB and skim sample maps; the vary-skim sweep members are a subset. Record it in DATA.md before running. This note is the analysis harness that consumes that manifest.




Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(nilHMM)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
for (f in list.files(here::here("R"), "\\.R$", full.names = TRUE)) source(f)
mnt <- bzea_mounts()



1. The three source paths (calibrated params from B2.2)

Skim (low-cov WGS + TP teosinte–B73 wideseq filtering): nNIL, RTIGER, binhmm.
BrB (3′ RNA-seq + TP filtering): nNIL, RTIGER, ATLAS.
Target-seq (MolBreeding 45K, high coverage): gt path — completeness only, and doubles as the optional Holland-style calibration cross-check; not headline.



2. Concordance metrics (per sample)
Computed on a shared marker/bin grid so tracks with different breakpoints are comparable (helpers in R/metrics.R):

pairwise segment agreement — pairwise_agreement()
donor-footprint Jaccard — donor_jaccard()
breakpoint concordance — compare breakpoint_count() across tracks
control cleanliness on B73 — control_donor_rate() (should be ~0)



Show code
# manifest <- fread(here::here("data/paired_cohort_manifest.csv"))  # from B5
# for each sample: stage skim + brb calls -> common schema -> metrics on grid.
# jac <- donor_jaccard(skim_calls, brb_calls, grid)
# agr <- pairwise_agreement(skim_calls, brb_calls, grid)




3. Read (to write once data is in)
Cross-source / cross-caller concordance distributions, and where the modalities systematically disagree (expected: BRB-RTIGER RNA false-donor floor; ATLAS most conservative). This is F4.

title: TeoNAM composite genetic map (bcsft(1,4), B73 v5)
text:
What this is. A results view of the composite TeoNAM genetic map — the native per-marker cM (cm) built by scripts/teonam_qtl_permap.R → scripts/teonam_qtl_map.R (per-family maps → union → Chen-2019 flanking imputation → bcsft(1,4) est.map, markers ordered on B73 v5). That build takes 5+ hours, so this notebook only loads the finished map and renders it — it runs no est.map.
The map is island-free on chr7: the v2→v5 displaced block (6 markers isolated by ~5 cM gaps) was caught by the connected-component quirky finder (scripts/map_tools.R) and removed, collapsing that gap 10.22 → 0.76 cM and chr7 154.5 → 144.85 cM (diagnosis in the QTL-recovery notebook).
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
Show code
d <- fread(here("data/teonam/teonam_v5_native.tsv"))
d <- d[!is.na(cm) & !is.na(chr_v5)][order(chr_v5, cm)]

# R/qtl `map` object: one per-chromosome cM vector, origin shifted to 0
chrs <- sort(unique(d$chr_v5))
map <- lapply(chrs, function(ch) {
  z <- d[chr_v5 == ch]
  v <- z$cm - min(z$cm)
  names(v) <- z$marker
  v
})
names(map) <- as.character(chrs)
class(map) <- "map"

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
What this is. The MLM counterpart of teonam-qtl-recovery-118k: the same figures — JLM lollipop (Fig 4A), the authentic 118K GWAS Manhattan, the coverage-degradation composite, caller noisiness — but the GWAS model is a mixed model instead of OLS. The fixed structure is the 5-family factor (the OLS/JLM structure), so this is literally “OLS (Family + marker) + a K random effect” and the OLS↔︎MLM contrast is the K term alone. In TeoNAM the structure is the five families, so this matches Chen’s Q = 5 PCs (Q ≈ Family; interchangeable — scripts/teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). Chen’s exact Fig-4C (TASSEL Q = 5 PCs + Centered_IBS K) and the QQ/λ_GC head-to-head are the sibling notebook teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.
The central MLM result is a confound, not a bug: for flowering time K = teosinte-ancestry relatedness is nearly collinear with the QTL (teosinte is late-flowering, so a RIL is late because it carries teosinte alleles at photoperiod loci like ZmCCT10/ZCN8), so the mixed model absorbs the very signal it is meant to protect — worst where K is best estimated (high coverage / full panel). This is why Chen used JLM (Fig 4A), not MLM, for the DTA QTL, and why the degradation sweep runs backwards under MLM.
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(fastman)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)
library(scales)
library(cowplot)
Show code
source(here("scripts/teonam_notebook_plots.R")) # shared plot helpers: get_transformer, plot_manhattan/lollipop/sweep_line, lambda_gc, read_mlm_tassel, LOD

# Panel B = MLM (Family + K) on the authentic per-SNP panel (R-EMMAX; scripts/
# teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). Family (not 5 PCs) so B and C share the OLS structure and
# the OLS<->MLM contrast is the K term alone. The faithful TASSEL Q+K Fig-4C (Chen's
# exact model) is the sibling notebook teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.html; read_mlm_tassel()
# below still parses it if needed.
mlm <- fread(here("data/teonam/dta_gwas_mlm_family_118k.csv"))

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
text:
Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
For each DTA candidate gene (B73 v5), the closest marker (smallest bp distance to the gene body) vs the window-max marker (highest −log₁₀P within ±500 kb) in the authentic 118K MLM (Family+K) scan. As under OLS they are not the same marker: the closest marker sits on the gene but carries little signal; the local peak is a nearby but distinct marker.


Show code
sc <- copy(mlm)[is.finite(P) & P > 0][, logP := -log10(P)]
# 11 DTA (flowering) candidates, v5-verified (data/teonam/dta_candidate_genes.tsv):
# display symbol + gene_id + coords + Chen 2019 QTL. Read directly (display names
# zcn12/zmcct9/zmcct10 differ from the file's canonical pebp12/cct2/cct1).
gg <- fread(here("data/teonam/dta_candidate_genes.tsv"))
gg[, chr := as.integer(chr)][, qtl := ifelse(qtl_chen2019 == "" | is.na(qtl_chen2019), paste0("DTA(chr", chr, ")"), qtl_chen2019)]
WIN <- 5e5
ctab <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_len(nrow(gg)), function(i) {
  ch <- gg$chr[i]
  s <- gg$start[i]
  e <- gg$end[i]
  m <- sc[CHR == ch]
  d <- pmax(0L, pmax(s - m$BP, m$BP - e))
  kc <- which.min(d)
  w <- m[BP >= s - WIN & BP <= e + WIN]
  kw <- which.max(w$logP)
  data.table(
    gene = gg$symbol[i], QTL = gg$qtl[i], chr = ch, `gene start (v5)` = s,
    `closest Δstart (bp)` = m$BP[kc] - s, `closest -log10P` = round(m$logP[kc], 2),
    `window-max Δstart (bp)` = w$BP[kw] - s, `window-max -log10P` = round(w$logP[kw], 2)
  )
}))
setorder(ctab, chr, `gene start (v5)`)
knitr::kable(ctab, format.args = list(big.mark = ","), caption = paste(
  "DTA candidate genes (B73 v5): CLOSEST marker vs highest -log10P marker within +/-500 kb",
  "(authentic 118K MLM Family+K scan). Δstart = marker bp - gene start (v5), signed."
))

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
JLM QTL genomic distribution (Fig 4A)
The DTA QTL from JLM (TASSEL stepwise, nested-in-family), candidate genes bold-italic above their nearest QTL. This arm is model-independent — JLM, not MLM — so the lollipop is identical to the OLS notebook; it is Chen’s primary DTA analysis and the correct home for these flowering-time QTL (see the MLM confound below).


Show code
p_lolli <- plot_lollipop(
  here("data/teonam/tassel/dta_jlm_native1.txt"),
  mlm,
  "DTA — JLM QTL genomic distribution (TeoNAM)",
  overlap_csv = here("results/sim/teonam/dta_candidate_overlap.csv"),
  label_genes = c("zcn8", "zcn12", "zmcct9", "zmcct10", "dlf1", "tu1"),
  out_png = here("results/sim/teonam/dta_jlm_lollipop_mlm118k.png")
)
p_lolli

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
Authentic 118K panel — MLM (Family + K) scan
The mixed model with the OLS structure — fixed = the 5-family factor, random = K (VanRaden), joint 2-df additive+dominance F (R-EMMAX; scripts/teonam_mlm_family_118k.R) — run on the authentic per-SNP 118K panel (~2.7% missing, per-marker mean-imputed; the real per-SNP scatter is preserved). Family instead of 5 PCs so this is exactly the OLS model (Family + marker) plus K, making the OLS↔︎MLM contrast the K term alone; in TeoNAM the structure is the five families, so Q ≈ Family (interchangeable — candidate peaks within 0.2, λ_GC 1.09 vs 1.12; teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). Chen’s exact Fig-4C model (TASSEL Q = 5 PCs + Centered_IBS K) is the sibling notebook teonam-stam-mlm-gwas-118k.


Show code
p_mlm <- plot_manhattan(
  mlm,
  "DTA — GWAS MLM (Family + K), authentic 118K panel, candidate genes at v5 positions",
  overlap_csv = here("results/sim/teonam/dta_candidate_overlap.csv"),
  lod = teonam_gwas_thr("DTA", "mlm"), # 1000-permutation genome-wide FWER (5%)
  out_png = here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_mlm_manhattan_annotated_118k.png")
)
p_mlm

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: OLS → MLM on the same panel: the ZmCCT10/flowering confound
text:
OLS → MLM on the same panel: the ZmCCT10/flowering confound
The fixed-effect OLS scan (Family + marker, no K) vs the MLM (Family + K) on the identical 118K panel — same fixed part, K the only addition. The MLM deflates λ_GC toward 1 and collapses the flowering peaks: ZmCCT10 (chr10) and ZCN8 (chr8) that dominate OLS are flattened, because teosinte (late-flowering) ancestry there is collinear with K.


Show code
ols <- fread(here("data/teonam/dta_gwas_scan_118k.csv"))[is.finite(P) & P > 0, .(SNP, CHR = as.integer(CHR), BP = as.integer(BP), P)]
lam_tab <- data.table(
  model = c("OLS (Family + marker, no K)", "MLM (Family + K)"),
  markers = c(nrow(ols), nrow(mlm)),
  lambda_GC = c(lambda_gc(ols$P), lambda_gc(mlm$P)),
  max_logP = round(c(max(-log10(ols$P)), max(-log10(mlm$P))), 2)
)

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: Composite (A: JLM lollipop · B: MLM authentic 118K · C: MLM FSFHap ancestry-imputed)
text:
Composite (A: JLM lollipop · B: MLM authentic 118K · C: MLM FSFHap ancestry-imputed)
Panel C is the direct MLM analog of the OLS notebook’s ancestry-imputed panel: the same FSFHap W22↔︎teosinte block mosaic (Chen’s own ancestry inference, teonam_gwas118k_dosage_fsfhap), scanned as Family + K — the OLS panel’s fixed part (the 5-family factor) plus a K random effect, so the OLS↔︎MLM contrast is the K term alone. Family is used instead of 5 PCs because in TeoNAM the structure is the five families (Q ≈ Family; on this mosaic Family+K is the better-calibrated of the two, λ_GC 1.13 vs 1.26 for 5 PCs — scripts/teonam_mlm_family_118k.R). K is estimated from the FSFHap genotypes themselves. Compute: scripts/teonam_mlm_fsfhap_118k.R. So B and C contrast the per-SNP panel against the ancestry-imputed mosaic: peaks at the same loci but C block-terraced, and the mixed model suppresses the flowering peaks in both (K absorbs the ancestry-collinear QTL — the confound).


Show code
fsf <- fread(here("data/teonam/dta_gwas_mlm_fsfhap_118k.csv"))
lgc_fsf <- lambda_gc(fsf$P)
p_anc <- plot_manhattan(
  fsf,
  sprintf("DTA — MLM (Family + K), FSFHap ancestry-imputed 118K panel (λ_GC = %.2f)", lgc_fsf),
  overlap_csv = here("results/sim/teonam/dta_candidate_overlap.csv"),
  out_png = here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_mlm_fsfhap_manhattan_118k.png")
)
composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  p_lolli, p_mlm, p_anc,
  ncol = 1, labels = c("A", "B", "C"), label_size = 20, align = "v", axis = "lr"
)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_composite_mlm_118k.png"), composite, width = 9, height = 13, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
composite

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: RTIGER ancestry × coverage under MLM (degradation sweep)
text:
RTIGER ancestry × coverage under MLM (degradation sweep)
The RTIGER coverage sweep under the MLM (Family+K). Reads are Poisson-sampled from the authentic 118K per-SNP genotypes over λ ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, ∞}×; RTIGER infers ancestry directly on all 118K markers, per chromosome (no thinning, no back-projection), and the per-coverage GL-dosage null (its honest Family+K at that depth) is applied. λ = ∞ is RTIGER’s deterministic ceiling. Compute: scripts/teonam_rtiger_sweep_118k.R (writes the OLS and _mlm sweep CSVs); this chunk plots.
Rigidity. RTIGER’s rigidity is a marker-count minimum-segment length. The calibrated rigidity_star = 2 over-segments ~5–6× on this dense grid; a cM-distance rule fails too because the interpolated cM map is heavily tied (median gap ≈ 0). So rigidity is calibrated empirically on the full grid — chr1, one family, λ=∞, swept against the FSFHap truth breakpoint density (agent/rtiger_rigsweep_chr1.R): rigidity = 60 reproduces FSFHap’s ~10.4 breakpoints/chr1 (recovered 10.6). That truth-matched segmentation is what makes the profile below smooth rather than the earlier over-segmented sawtooth.
The coverage order is inverted relative to OLS: low coverage rides highest, ∞ lowest. At shallow depth the GL-dosage K is too noisy to correct structure, so the ancestry-confounded QTL leaks through; at ∞ the kinship is well-estimated and crushes it. The chr3 broad 0.1× elevation is a low-coverage MLM artifact (poor K + block recovery in the pericentromere). One replicate per cell.


Show code
p_sweep <- plot_sweep_line(
  here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_rtiger_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"),
  "DTA — RTIGER ancestry, MLM (Family+K) −log10P vs coverage",
  base_size = 15
)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_rtiger_118k_mlm_sweep_manhattan.png"), p_sweep, width = 9, height = 4.6, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
p_sweep

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: MLM coverage-degradation composite: control / nNIL / RTIGER / LB-Impute / binhmm
text:
MLM coverage-degradation composite: control / nNIL / RTIGER / LB-Impute / binhmm
The five-caller contrast under MLM. Same pipeline as the OLS composite (authentic per-SNP truth → per-caller ancestry on the 0.1 cM grid → back-project → GWAS), scanned with the per-coverage GL-dosage Family+K null instead of OLS. λ = ∞ is each caller’s own deterministic ceiling (caller-specific, no shared baseline).
The dominant ZmCCT10 (chr10) and ZCN8 (chr8) flowering QTL survive MLM in every caller (only partly confounded with K, and strongest at low coverage), so the panels differentiate mainly on genome-wide false-positive suppression, which follows the caller-noisiness ordering below (a property of the recovered ancestry geometry):

A — interpolation control (GL + HWE, no HMM): noisy — dense false peaks genome-wide on top of the chr8/chr10 QTL; no smoothing for K to work with.
B — nNIL: the busiest ancestry caller — scattered false peaks throughout, with a strong chr8 (ZCN8, to ~17 at 0.1×) and the chr10 (ZmCCT10) spike.
C — RTIGER: clean — chr8 + chr10 dominate, little else survives LOD.
D — LB-Impute: also clean — chr8 + chr10, flat elsewhere.
E — binhmm (1 Mb bins): cleanest — only chr8 (~12) and the chr10 spike survive; the coarse bin resolution suppresses fine-scale false peaks, essentially flat genome-wide.

Read the panels for false-positive control, not QTL power (which MLM deliberately removes). One replicate per (family, λ) cell.


Show code
pA <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_control_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "DTA MLM (Family+K) — interpolation control (GL + HWE)", legend = TRUE)
pB <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_nnil_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "DTA MLM (Family+K) — nNIL ancestry", legend = FALSE)
pC <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_rtiger_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "DTA MLM (Family+K) — RTIGER ancestry", legend = FALSE)
pD <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_lbimpute_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "DTA MLM (Family+K) — LB-Impute ancestry (map-aware cM)", legend = FALSE)
pE <- plot_sweep_line(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_binhmm_118k_mlm_sweep.csv"), "DTA MLM (Family+K) — binhmm ancestry (1 Mb bins)", legend = FALSE)
composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(pA, pB, pC, pD, pE, ncol = 1, align = "v", axis = "lr", labels = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"), label_size = 18)
ggsave(here("results/sim/teonam/dta_sweep_composite_mlm_118k.png"), composite, width = 9, height = 20, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
composite

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
section: ZmCCT10 peak height vs coverage (the inverted trend)
text:
ZmCCT10 peak height vs coverage (the inverted trend)
Quantifying the reversal: the ZmCCT10 (chr10) local peak (max −log₁₀P within ±500 kb) by caller and coverage under MLM. Height falls as coverage rises — the opposite of a normal power curve — because a sharper GL-dosage K at high depth more completely absorbs the ancestry-confounded flowering QTL.

  sw <- fread(here(sprintf("results/sim/teonam/dta_gwas_%s_118k_mlm_sweep.csv", callers[nm])))
  sw[CHR == tb1$chr & abs(BP - tb1$start) <= 5e5 & is.finite(P) & P > 0,
    .(caller = nm, tb1_logP = round(max(-log10(P)), 2)),
    by = coverage
  ]
}))

wide[, covlab := ifelse(is.finite(coverage), formatC(coverage), "Inf")]

  caption = "ZmCCT10 peak -log10P (+/-500 kb) by caller x coverage under MLM (Family+K). Height decreases with coverage (Inf lowest): sharper K absorbs the ancestry-confounded flowering QTL more fully at high depth."
)

title: TeoNAM DTA (days to anthesis) — MLM (Family + K) GWAS with candidate genes + coverage sweep [118K panel]
text:
Caller noisiness (band-limited spectral power)
Spurious fine-scale structure each caller injects, as spectral power in a physically- defined noise band (R, P10) — R = 0.1 cM grid resolution, P10 = 10th-percentile resolvable teosinte introgression tract. This is a property of the recovered ancestry geometry (the back-projected block structure), which is identical between OLS and MLM — only the downstream GWAS model differs — so the ranking below (measured on the DTA OLS sweep tracks, scripts/teonam_sweep_noisiness.R) carries over: RTIGER ≈ binhmm are the cleanest, LB-Impute ≈ control mid, and nNIL is by far the noisiest (~6× control, ~400× RTIGER) — exactly the false-positive ordering seen in the MLM composite above.

knitr::kable(bp, caption = sprintf("Noise band: resolution R = %d kb, P10 resolvable introgression = %d kb (median tract %.2f Mb).", bp$R_kb, bp$P10_kb, bp$median_frag_Mb))

Noisiness is the inverse of spatial autocorrelation. The acf_lag1 column — the lag-1 autocorrelation of the −log₁₀P marker track, measured directly (not from the FFT) — ranks the callers in the reverse order of the band-power, even more starkly than for STAM: rtiger / binhmm are cleanest and most autocorrelated (acf ≈ 0.999), while nNIL is by far the noisiest and least coherent (acf ≈ 0.63). This is Wiener–Khinchin — the periodogram is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function — so band-limited high-frequency power and short-range autocorrelation are two views of the same second-order structure; either selects the same callers. See GWAS association-landscape geometry.

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C)
text:
What this is. The STAM GWAS Manhattan (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) reproduced on the TeoNAM genotypes, with the domestication candidate genes placed at their real B73 v5 coordinates (MaizeGDB genemodel_locus → b73_gene_coords), a red star at each gene’s genomic position, and the LOD = 5 dotted significance line.
This notebook loads pre-computed CSVs and plots (compute lives in the scripts/ directory): the per-marker GWAS scans (teonam_stam_gwas_scan.R interpolated / teonam_stam_gwas_family_imputed.R family-imputed) and the candidate-gene overlap (teonam_candidate_overlap.R). We show both the family-imputed scan (natural scatter) and the interpolated/densified scan (block-terraced — the artifact of step-interpolated genotypes, see below).
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(fastman)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)
library(scales)
library(cowplot)
Show code
# Coordinate transformer replicating fastman_gg's internal x layout, so we can
# place gene labels / lines at arbitrary (chr, bp). From inv4m make_manhattan_plots.Rmd.
get_transformer <- function(m) {
  cmat <- do.call(rbind, lapply(
    split(m[order(m$CHR, m$BP), ], m$CHR[order(m$CHR, m$BP)]),
    function(d) {
      data.frame(
        CHR = d$CHR[1], min_bp = min(d$BP),
        width = max(d$BP) - min(d$BP),
        medgap = if (nrow(d) > 1) median(diff(sort(d$BP))) else 1
      )
    }
  ))
  cmat <- cmat[order(cmat$CHR), ]
  maxgap <- max(cmat$medgap, na.rm = TRUE)
  numc <- nrow(cmat)
  cmat$base <- 0
  cmat$midp <- 0
  cmat$midp[1] <- cmat$width[1] / 2
  for (i in 2:numc) {
    cmat$base[i] <- cmat$base[i - 1] + cmat$width[i - 1] + maxgap
    cmat$midp[i] <- cmat$base[i] + cmat$width[i] / 2
  }
  fac <- numc / cmat$midp[numc]
  cmat$basef <- fac * cmat$base
  function(chr, bp) {
    i <- match(chr, cmat$CHR)
    fac * (bp - cmat$min_bp[i]) + cmat$basef[i]
  }
}

LOD <- 5 # Chen 2019: P < 1e-5 (LOD = 5)

#' STAM GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes at their true v5 positions.
#' Odd chromosomes black, even gray75; y-axis expanded one major-tick unit above
#' the top tick; gene labels repelled into the headroom above the maximum peak
#' (segments back to the red star at each gene's local peak).
#' @param scan_csv per-marker scan (SNP, CHR, BP[v5], P)
#' @param title    plot title
#' @param out_png  optional path to save a PNG
plot_stam_manhattan <- function(scan_csv, title, out_png = NULL, mark = NULL) {
  scan <- if (is.data.frame(scan_csv)) as.data.frame(scan_csv) else as.data.frame(fread(scan_csv))
  scan <- scan[is.finite(scan$P) & scan$P > 0, ]
  m <- scan[order(scan$CHR, scan$BP), c("SNP", "CHR", "BP", "P")]

  ov <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv")) # symbol, chr, start (v5)
  # star height = local max -log10P within +/-500 kb IN THIS scan
  ov[, y := vapply(seq_len(.N), function(i) {
    w <- m$P[m$CHR == chr[i] & abs(m$BP - start[i]) <= 5e5]
    if (length(w)) -log10(min(w)) else NA_real_
  }, numeric(1))]
  tr <- get_transformer(m)
  ov[, BPn := tr(chr, start)]
  ov <- ov[is.finite(y)]

  mp <- -log10(min(m$P)) # maximum peak
  ticks <- pretty(c(0, mp))
  unit <- diff(ticks)[1]
  ytop <- max(ticks) + unit # one major-tick unit of headroom

  p <- fastman_gg(
    m = m, snp = "SNP", col = c("black", "gray75"), maxP = mp,
    genomewideline = NULL, suggestiveline = NULL,
    ylab = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), xlab = ""
  ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = LOD, linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.7, color = "black") +
    geom_point(
      data = ov, aes(x = BPn, y = y), inherit.aes = FALSE,
      shape = 8, size = 3, stroke = 1, color = "red"
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = ov, aes(x = BPn, y = y, label = symbol),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, fontface = "italic", size = 5,
      segment.color = "grey50", min.segment.length = 0, box.padding = 0.5,
      max.overlaps = Inf, ylim = c(mp + 0.3 * unit, ytop)
    ) + # above the max peak
    ggtitle(title) +
    theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      breaks = ticks, limits = c(0, ytop),
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))
    ) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 1, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2)
    )

  if (!is.null(mark)) { # dashed marker at an arbitrary locus (e.g. the chr3 ghost)
    xm <- tr(mark$chr, mark$bp)
    p <- p + geom_vline(xintercept = xm, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.5)
  }
  if (!is.null(out_png)) ggsave(out_png, p, width = 9, height = 4.6, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
  p
}

#' Chen 2019 Fig 4A: JLM QTL genomic distribution as a lollipop plot.
#' Full 10-chromosome axis is obtained by passing the dense scan to fastman_gg
#' with fully transparent points (col = transparent); the JLM QTL are overlaid as
#' blue stems + points, candidate genes labeled bold-italic above.
#' @param jlm_txt  TASSEL StepwiseOLSModelFitter output (stam_jlm_native1.txt): Name,
#'   Locus, Position (v5 bp), `pr>F`
#' @param scan_csv dense per-marker scan, only used (transparent) to lay out the axis
plot_stam_lollipop <- function(jlm_txt, scan_csv, title, out_png = NULL) {
  scan <- as.data.frame(fread(scan_csv))
  scan <- scan[is.finite(scan$P) & scan$P > 0, ]
  maxis <- scan[order(scan$CHR, scan$BP), c("SNP", "CHR", "BP", "P")]

  j <- fread(jlm_txt)
  j <- j[!(Name %in% c("mean", "Family", "Error"))]
  q <- data.table(
    SNP = j$Name, CHR = as.integer(j$Locus), BP = as.integer(j$Position),
    logP = -log10(as.numeric(j$`pr>F`))
  )
  tr_ <- toupper(sub("_jlm.*$", "", basename(jlm_txt))) # JLM perm threshold (Chen-style FWER)
  .thrcsv <- here("data/teonam/jlm_perm_thresholds.csv")
  .jlm_thr <- if (file.exists(.thrcsv)) {
    .d <- fread(.thrcsv)
    .h <- .d[trait == tr_ & alpha == 0.05, thr_neglog10p]
    if (length(.h) && is.finite(.h[1])) .h[1] else LOD
  } else {
    LOD
  }
  q <- q[logP >= .jlm_thr] # keep QTL clearing the 1000-permutation FWER (5%) line
  ov <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv")) # symbol, chr, start (v5)
  ov <- ov[symbol %in% c("ts2", "tb1", "tru1", "zmm16", "tsh4")] # JLM Fig-4A candidates (an1 is GWAS-only)
  q[, gene := ""] # assign each candidate gene to its NEAREST JLM QTL (1:1)
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(ov))) {
    idx <- which(q$CHR == ov$chr[i])
    if (length(idx)) {
      k <- idx[which.min(abs(q$BP[idx] - ov$start[i]))]
      q$gene[k] <- ov$symbol[i]
    }
  }
  tr <- get_transformer(maxis)
  q[, BPn := tr(CHR, BP)]

  maxlogP <- max(q$logP)
  brks <- seq(0, floor(maxlogP / 10) * 10, by = 10)
  ytop <- maxlogP * 1.18 # headroom for the top label

  p <- fastman_gg(
    m = maxis, snp = "SNP", col = c("#00000000", "#00000000"),
    maxP = maxlogP, genomewideline = NULL, suggestiveline = NULL,
    ylab = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), xlab = ""
  ) +
    geom_segment(
      data = q, aes(x = BPn, xend = BPn, y = 0, yend = logP),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, color = "blue", linewidth = 0.5
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = q, aes(x = BPn, y = logP), inherit.aes = FALSE,
      color = "blue", size = 2.5
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = q[gene != ""], aes(x = BPn, y = logP, label = gene),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, fontface = "bold.italic", size = 5,
      segment.color = NA, box.padding = 0.4, point.padding = 0.3,
      nudge_y = 0.05 * maxlogP, seed = 1, max.overlaps = Inf
    ) +
    ggtitle(title) + theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = brks, limits = c(0, ytop), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 1, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2)
    )
  if (!is.null(out_png)) ggsave(out_png, p, width = 9, height = 4, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
  p
}

#' Coverage-sweep line-Manhattan (geom_line, one line per coverage).
#' −log10P vs genomic position along the union grid; `group = interaction(CHR,
#' coverage)` so lines never connect across chromosomes. Color = coverage on a
#' viridis ramp, the λ = ∞ truth baseline in black. LOD = 5 dotted. NO gene labels.
#' Reused for the control / nNIL / RTIGER panels of the degradation composite.
#' @param sweep_csv combined sweep CSV (SNP, CHR, BP[v5], P, coverage; coverage=Inf
#'   for the λ=∞ baseline rows)
#' @param title panel title (markdown)
#' @param legend show the coverage legend (FALSE for composite panels sharing one)
#' @param base_size theme base size
plot_sweep_line <- function(sweep_csv, title, legend = TRUE, base_size = 14) {
  sw <- as.data.table(fread(sweep_csv))
  sw <- sw[is.finite(P) & P > 0]
  sw[, logP := -log10(P)]
  # x layout from the union grid (every coverage carries the identical marker set)
  tr <- get_transformer(as.data.frame(sw[coverage == sw$coverage[1], .(CHR, BP)]))
  sw[, BPn := tr(CHR, BP)]

  # Legend order: infinity (truth) at top, then coverage in descending order.
  cov_levels <- c("∞", "20", "10", "5", "1", "0.5", "0.2", "0.1")
  sw[, cov_lab := factor(ifelse(is.finite(coverage), formatC(coverage), "∞"),
    levels = cov_levels
  )]
  # Colour mapping is fixed by coverage value (0.1 = darkest viridis ... 20 =
  # lightest; ∞ = black), independent of the legend order above.
  cov_asc <- c("0.1", "0.2", "0.5", "1", "5", "10", "20")
  pal <- setNames(grDevices::hcl.colors(7, "viridis"), cov_asc)
  pal["∞"] <- "black"
  axis_df <- sw[, .(mid = (min(BPn) + max(BPn)) / 2), by = CHR][order(CHR)]
  ytop <- 20 # shared y-limit across all panels for cross-method comparison

  # Draw order (z-stack): 20x first, then descending to 0.1x, and ∞ LAST so the
  # darker low-coverage / black-truth profiles sit on top. dord = -coverage sorts
  # finite coverage descending (20 -> -20 first); ∞ -> Inf sorts last.
  sw[, dord := ifelse(is.finite(coverage), -coverage, Inf)]

  ggplot(
    sw[order(dord, CHR, BP)],
    aes(x = BPn, y = logP, color = cov_lab, group = interaction(CHR, coverage))
  ) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = LOD, linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.7, color = "grey30") +
    scale_color_manual(values = pal, name = "coverage (×)", drop = FALSE) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = axis_df$mid, labels = axis_df$CHR, expand = c(0.01, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ytop), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))) +
    labs(x = "chromosome", y = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), title = title) +
    theme_classic(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 0, size = base_size, face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2),
      # legend INSIDE the plotting area, top-right corner (top panel only)
      legend.position = if (legend) c(0.995, 0.97) else "none",
      legend.justification = c(1, 1),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.7), colour = NA),
      legend.key.size = unit(0.85, "lines"),
      legend.title = element_text(size = base_size - 3),
      legend.text = element_text(size = base_size - 4),
      legend.margin = margin(1, 3, 1, 3)
    ) +
    guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(linewidth = 1.2)))
}

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C)
section: Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
text:
Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
For each STAM candidate gene (B73 v5), two different “nearby marker” choices from the pre-JLM marker set (the 51,004-marker family-imputed GWAS scan): the closest marker (smallest bp distance to the gene body) and the window-max marker (highest −log₁₀P within ±500 kb — a 1 Mb window centered on the gene). They are not the same marker at any gene: the closest marker sits essentially on the gene but carries little signal, while the significant local signal is a nearby but distinct marker. This is the reference for what “near the gene” means in the Fig 4A lollipop below (which labels each gene onto its nearest JLM QTL).


Show code
sc <- fread(here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_family_imputed.csv"))
sc <- sc[is.finite(P) & P > 0][, logP := -log10(P)]
cand <- c(
  ts2 = "STAM1.1", tb1 = "STAM1.2", tru1 = "STAM3.1",
  zmm16 = "STAM3.2", tsh4 = "STAM7.1", an1 = "GWAS(chr1)"
)
sym <- fread(here("data/ref/maize_v5_gene_symbols.tsv"))
setnames(sym, c("gene_id", "symbol"))
coord <- fread(here("data/ref/b73_gene_coords.tsv"),
  header = FALSE,
  col.names = c("gene_id", "chr", "start", "end")
)
gg <- merge(sym[symbol %in% names(cand)], coord, by = "gene_id")
gg[, chr := as.integer(sub("^chr", "", chr))][, qtl := cand[symbol]]
WIN <- 5e5 # +/-500 kb around the gene body (1 Mb window)
ctab <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_len(nrow(gg)), function(i) {
  ch <- gg$chr[i]
  s <- gg$start[i]
  e <- gg$end[i]
  m <- sc[CHR == ch]
  d <- pmax(0L, pmax(s - m$BP, m$BP - e))
  kc <- which.min(d) # closest to gene body
  w <- m[BP >= s - WIN & BP <= e + WIN]
  kw <- which.max(w$logP) # max signal in window
  data.table(
    gene = gg$symbol[i], QTL = gg$qtl[i], chr = ch, `gene start (v5)` = s,
    `closest Δstart (bp)` = m$BP[kc] - s, `closest -log10P` = round(m$logP[kc], 2),
    `window-max Δstart (bp)` = w$BP[kw] - s, `window-max -log10P` = round(w$logP[kw], 2)
  )
}))
setorder(ctab, chr, `gene start (v5)`)
knitr::kable(ctab, format.args = list(big.mark = ","), caption = paste(
  "STAM candidate genes (B73 v5): the CLOSEST marker vs the highest -log10P marker",
  "within +/-500 kb (family-imputed GWAS scan, 51,004 markers). Δstart = marker bp -",
  "gene start (v5), signed (negative = upstream); the two markers differ at every gene."
))

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C)
text:
Step-interpolated grid — artifact only
The same scan on the step-interpolated full GWAS set (teonam_map_v5_gwas, 51,004 markers, cross-family gaps densified). This is shown purely to document the interpolation artifact. Ancestry is a piecewise-constant block signal, so step interpolation makes contiguous markers identical → identical p-values → terraced plateaus (peaks still correctly located). The GWAS figure above uses the same markers without densification (family-imputed, per-marker).


Show code
